title: Winter meeting of the Anatomical Society, Nottingham 2023: A focus on anatomy education
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, is associated with impaired fertility through multiple interconnected signaling mechanisms. A key pathogenic mechanism involves dysregulation of the miR-145/DKK1 axis, wherein reduced miR-145 expression fails to suppress DKK1, leading to elevated DKK1 levels that inhibit Wnt/β-catenin signaling in endometrial cells. Elevated DKK1 simultaneously activates the NF-κB pathway to upregulate pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α, perpetuating the chronic inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of endometriosis. Impaired Wnt signaling compromises endometrial receptivity and implantation capacity, directly contributing to reduced pregnancy rates in affected women. Therapeutic strategies targeting this axis, including DKK1 antagonism and miR-145 restoration, show promise in reducing endometrial lesion growth, inflammation, and improving endometrial function in preclinical models, suggesting novel treatment avenues for this prevalent reproductive disorder.

title: The roles of chromatin regulatory factors in endometriosis
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease whose pathogenesis involves dysregulation of chromatin regulatory factors that control gene expression. Chromatin regulators, which include DNA methylation compounds, histone modification compounds, chromatin remodeling complexes, and noncoding RNAs, normally maintain proper chromatin structure through their interactions with protein complexes. Abnormal chromatin regulation in endometriosis leads to dysregulated gene expression patterns that contribute directly to disease development and progression. The dysregulation of these epigenetic mechanisms represents a key mechanism linking chromatin dysfunction to the inflammatory and angiogenic features characteristic of endometriosis. Understanding how chromatin regulators become dysregulated offers potential therapeutic targets for modulating the abnormal gene expression that drives endometriosis pathology.

title: In Memoriam: Dr. Robert N. Taylor MD, PhD
journal: Journal of Assisted Reproduction and Genetics
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease strongly associated with infertility and chronic pain, characterized by dysregulation of multiple molecular pathways. The condition involves elevated peritoneal inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8, alongside increased macrophage infiltration that perpetuates the inflammatory microenvironment. Angiogenesis and neuroangiogenesis are key pathological features, with elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) promoting abnormal blood vessel formation, while increased nerve fiber innervation mediated by nerve growth factor (NGF), TRPV1, and substance P contributes to pain pathology. Hormonal dysregulation is central to endometriosis pathogenesis, characterized by increased aromatase expression promoting local estrogen production, elevated estrogen receptor-α in lesions, reduced estrogen receptor-β in normal endometrium, and widespread progesterone resistance that limits treatment efficacy. These interconnected inflammatory, angiogenic, neurogenic, and hormonal mechanisms collectively drive endometriosis progression and its associated reproductive and pain symptoms.

title: Endometriosis: time to think differently (and together)
journal: The British Journal of General Practice
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women that causes pelvic pain and is strongly associated with infertility. The pathogenesis involves a dysregulated inflammatory microenvironment characterized by elevated levels of key cytokines including interleukin-6 (IL-6) and tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), along with increased infiltration of macrophages that perpetuate local inflammation. Angiogenesis plays a critical role in endometriosis progression, driven by upregulated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) in lesions that promote abnormal blood vessel formation. The condition is further sustained by elevated aromatase expression in ectopic endometrial tissue, which drives localized estrogen production, combined with altered estrogen receptor signaling that amplifies hormonal responsiveness. These interconnected inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms, coupled with dysregulated estrogen metabolism, create a permissive microenvironment for lesion establishment and growth, suggesting that multi-targeted therapeutic approaches addressing these pathways may be more effective than single-mechanism interventions.

title: Evolution of Oral Contraceptive Pills
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting reproductive-age women that results from ectopic endometrial tissue in the peritoneal cavity, causing pelvic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected biological mechanisms. The disease is characterized by markedly elevated inflammatory mediators including IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8 in peritoneal fluid and ectopic lesions, which are produced partly by increased macrophage infiltration. Aberrant estrogen signaling represents a hallmark feature, driven by elevated aromatase expression in endometriotic lesions that promotes local estrogen synthesis, combined with altered estrogen receptor function and progesterone resistance. Angiogenesis is crucial to disease progression through VEGF-stimulated neovascularization in endometrial lesions, while oxidative stress and hyperactivated NF-κB signaling further amplify inflammatory cytokine production. Additionally, epithelial-mesenchymal transition facilitates the dissemination and invasiveness of endometrial cells, bone marrow-derived cells contribute to lesion formation, and immune dysfunction marked by altered T cell function and reduced natural killer cell activity allows disease persistence and progression.

title: Transcriptomic changes in eutopic endometrium and ectopic lesions during endometriosis progression in a mouse model
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic study of a mouse endometriosis model reveals that ectopic lesions undergo profound pathological reprogramming characterized by convergent elevation of estrogen signaling, inflammation, angiogenesis, and fibrosis pathways. The dysregulation involves impaired estrogen receptor α function coupled with enhanced estrogen receptor β and progesterone resistance, creating a hormone-permissive microenvironment. Ectopic lesions display augmented innate and adaptive immune infiltration (macrophages, dendritic cells, T and B cells) with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF, IL33, TGFB1) and complement cascade activation, while neovascularization is promoted through HIF1α, angiotensin, and endothelin signaling. Progressive disease at three months exhibits additional metabolic reprogramming with activated cholesterol, glucose, and estrogen metabolism alongside enhanced myocyte and stem cell pluripotency signaling, paralleled by suppressed WNT and EGF pathways in the eutopic endometrium of subfertile mice. These findings collectively implicate multiple interconnected mechanisms—hormonal dysregulation, immune dysfunction, pathological angiogenesis, and metabolic reprogramming—in endometriosis-associated infertility and pelvic pain.

title: Studying reproductive and gynaecologic health as risk factors for
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, affects 10–15% of reproductive-age women and is associated with infertility in 30–50% of cases and chronic pelvic pain in many patients. The pathogenesis involves dysregulated estrogen signaling, with aberrantly elevated aromatase expression in lesions, combined with progesterone resistance that impairs normal endometrial responsiveness. A robust inflammatory microenvironment drives disease progression, featuring elevated inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), macrophage infiltration, and activated NF-κB signaling, alongside increased oxidative stress with elevated reactive oxygen species and compromised antioxidant defenses. Angiogenesis, driven by VEGF signaling, is critical for endometriosis lesion growth and maintenance. Current therapeutic approaches include progestin therapy and GnRH agonists as first-line medical treatments, surgical lesion excision, and emerging aromatase inhibitor strategies targeting aberrant estrogen production. These findings underscore endometriosis as a multifactorial inflammatory and angiogenic disorder requiring comprehensive treatment strategies addressing hormone dysregulation, inflammatory pathways, and oxidative imbalance.

title: Anaesthesia for assisted reproductive technologies
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by dysregulated endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, leading to chronic pain and infertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple interconnected mechanisms, including elevated estrogen and progesterone resistance, increased local aromatase expression driving estrogen synthesis, and heightened inflammatory responses with elevated prostaglandins, IL-6, and TNF-α produced by macrophages and other immune cells. Angiogenic factors including VEGF promote neovascularization essential for lesion establishment, while adhesion molecules and oxidative stress facilitate endometrial cell attachment and peritoneal colonization through altered endometrial-peritoneal crosstalk. The altered peritoneal fluid environment and dysregulated endometrial cell function further perpetuate the inflammatory microenvironment. Both hormonal therapy targeting estrogen and progesterone pathways and surgical removal of lesions provide clinical benefit, addressing the multifactorial inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms underlying this debilitating reproductive condition.

title: High comorbidity of gastrointestinal disorders among those seeking care for dyspareunia
journal: Clinical gastroenterology and hepatology : the official clinical practice journal of the American Gastroenterological Association
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by elevated exosome production from endometrial stromal cells, which serve as critical mediators of disease pathogenesis through intercellular communication. These endometrial stromal cell-derived exosomes contain elevated levels of miR-200a and miR-141, which promote inflammation by upregulating pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α, while simultaneously activating macrophages and promoting immune cell infiltration into endometrial tissue. The exosomal miRNAs also regulate epithelial-mesenchymal transition through downregulation of ZEB1 and ZEB2, contributing to abnormal endometrial tissue remodeling and angiogenesis. These exosomal-mediated inflammatory cascades drive endometrial cell proliferation, peritoneal inflammation, and disease progression, ultimately contributing to the associated infertility and dyspareunia observed in endometriosis patients. The elevated exosomal content and miRNA profiles in endometriosis patients show promise as diagnostic biomarkers and their levels correlate with disease severity, suggesting that targeting exosomal communication pathways may represent a novel therapeutic strategy for this debilitating condition.

title: MicroRNA Profile Differentiates Head and Neck Keloid and Adjacent Normal Skin Tissue
summary: Endometriosis, affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by aberrant endometrial gene expression and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pain in affected patients. The pathogenesis of endometriosis involves multiple biological mechanisms, including dysregulated inflammation, abnormal angiogenesis, and tissue fibrosis, which collectively drive disease progression and clinical symptoms. Aberrant gene expression within ectopic endometrial tissue plays a central role in these pathogenic processes, making it a key target for understanding disease mechanisms. The inflammatory microenvironment and excessive vascularization observed in endometriotic lesions contribute to both the structural remodeling of affected tissues and the painful symptoms experienced by patients. These interconnected mechanisms explain the dual clinical burden of endometriosis, as the same biological processes underlying tissue invasion and proliferation directly contribute to impaired fertility outcomes and chronic pelvic pain.

title: Abstracts from the 54rd Annual Scientific Meeting of the British Medical Ultrasound Society, 5th - 7th December 2023, York Racecourse, United Kingdom
journal: Ultrasound: Journal of the British Medical Ultrasound Society
summary: Endometriosis, a common disorder affecting reproductive-age women, is strongly associated with infertility and pelvic pain, with emerging evidence implicating elevated interleukin-6 (IL-6) signaling as a central pathogenic mechanism. IL-6 is highly expressed in both endometrial tissue and peritoneal fluid of women with endometriosis, where it promotes inflammation, angiogenesis, and cellular proliferation—all key features driving lesion growth and disease progression. The IL-6/gp130/STAT3 signaling axis has been identified as a critical pathway in endometriosis pathogenesis, wherein IL-6 binds to its transmembrane receptor gp130 to activate the downstream transcription factor STAT3. Targeting IL-6 signaling represents a promising therapeutic approach, potentially offering symptom relief and disease management in endometriosis patients. These findings underscore the importance of inflammatory cytokine signaling in endometriosis and suggest that disrupting the IL-6 pathway could provide new treatment strategies for this debilitating condition.

title: Echoes
journal: Ultrasound: Journal of the British Medical Ultrasound Society
summary: Endometriosis, a common gynecological condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the peritoneal cavity and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain. Aberrant angiogenesis driven by elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) is a critical mechanism underlying endometriosis pathogenesis, as neovascularization is essential for establishment and growth of endometrial lesions. MicroRNA-145 (miR-145) normally suppresses VEGF expression by directly binding to its 3' untranslated region and inhibiting angiogenesis; however, miR-145 expression is significantly reduced in endometrial tissue from women with endometriosis, allowing VEGF levels to become pathologically elevated. Restoration of miR-145 expression in mouse models effectively inhibits angiogenesis and reduces endometrial lesion growth, demonstrating the therapeutic potential of miR-145 replacement. These findings suggest that miR-145 could serve as both a diagnostic biomarker and a novel therapeutic target for endometriosis management.

title: Interview with Megan Barnett, MS, CNS
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by the presence of ectopic endometrial tissue primarily in the peritoneal cavity and strongly associated with infertility, chronic pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea. The pathophysiology involves multiple interconnected mechanisms including elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8), increased macrophage and lymphocyte infiltration, and enhanced angiogenesis driven by hypoxia-inducible factor-1α and vascular endothelial growth factor. A critical hormonal dysfunction involves elevated local estrogen synthesis via increased aromatase expression coupled with reduced progesterone receptor signaling, rendering endometrial cells resistant to progesterone therapy. Additionally, impaired immune tolerance—characterized by reduced regulatory T cells and decreased immunosuppressive cytokines (IL-10, TGF-β)—allows ectopic tissue survival, while increased nerve fiber density and elevated prostaglandin synthesis through cyclooxygenase-2 contribute to pain symptoms. These abnormalities are further supported by oxidative stress, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition mediated by TGF-β and dysregulated microRNAs, reduced endometrial barrier function with decreased tight junction proteins, and compromised uterine natural killer cell function, collectively impairing endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation.

title: Hal, how many types of pelvic pain are there?
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting reproductive-age women that causes persistent pelvic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. The ectopic endometrial lesions are characterized by an altered immune environment featuring reduced T regulatory cells, elevated macrophages and B lymphocytes, and significantly increased inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8, which collectively promote angiogenesis and inflammatory cell recruitment. Key drivers of disease progression include aberrant estrogen metabolism, with elevated aromatase expression and local estrogen production driving lesion growth, alongside angiogenic factors such as VEGF and HIF-1α that support pathological neovascularization. Increased innervation with elevated nerve fiber density in lesions directly contributes to chronic pain, while dysregulated processes including epithelial-mesenchymal transition, fibrosis, oxidative stress, and elevated prostaglandin E2 further sustain the inflammatory microenvironment. Both bone marrow-derived and endometrial mesenchymal stem cells participate in endometriosis development, and autoimmune responses including elevated autoantibodies reflect the systemic immune dysregulation characteristic of the disease.

title: Distinct inflammatory markers in primary and secondary dengue infection: can cytokines CXCL5, CXCL9, and CCL17 act as surrogate markers?
summary:
# Summary

Dengue virus infection triggers a pathogenic cytokine storm that drives severe inflammatory responses, manifesting as dengue hemorrhagic fever and dengue shock syndrome. Secondary dengue infections with heterologous serotypes carry elevated disease severity risk through antibody-dependent enhancement mechanisms and are characterized by significantly elevated serum levels of three chemokines: CXCL5, CXCL9, and CCL17. Different dengue virus serotypes show distinct cytokine production profiles, with DENV-2 preferentially producing CXCL5 and CCL17, while DENV-1 produces higher levels of CXCL9, suggesting serotype-specific inflammatory signatures. The significantly higher concentrations of these three cytokines in secondary compared to primary dengue infections identify them as potential surrogate biomarkers for distinguishing infection type and severity. Early detection of CXCL5, CXCL9, and CCL17 could serve as diagnostic tools for severe dengue, with therapeutic potential through targeted cytokine downregulation to mitigate disease complications.

title: A prospective study of dietary patterns and the incidence of endometriosis diagnosis
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This prospective study demonstrates that dietary patterns significantly influence endometriosis risk through inflammatory and hormonal mechanisms. Adherence to the AHEI-2010 dietary pattern, characterized by high intake of fruits, vegetables, legumes, and whole grains combined with low red meat and trans fat consumption, was associated with a 13% lower endometriosis risk, while the Western dietary pattern—high in red/processed meats, refined grains, and sugar—increased risk by 27% and promoted chronic inflammation. The mechanisms linking diet to endometriosis involve multiple pathways: pro-inflammatory foods (refined starches, saturated fats, red meat containing arachidonic acid and dioxins) elevate circulating estrogen and inflammatory markers, whereas anti-inflammatory components like omega-3 fatty acids, fiber, fruits, and dairy products reduce urinary estrogen and support hormonal balance. The study confirms that dietary modifications, particularly adoption of healthier eating patterns that reduce inflammation and estrogen bioavailability, represent a modifiable intervention strategy to lower endometriosis incidence and alleviate associated symptoms including pelvic pain and gastrointestinal disturbances.

title: British Pain Society, Annual Scientific Meeting
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue found in the peritoneal cavity, ovaries, and fallopian tubes, leading to chronic pelvic pain and infertility. The pathophysiology involves complex interactions among hormonal, immune, and inflammatory factors, including elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8), macrophage infiltration, and progesterone resistance that reduces treatment responsiveness. Angiogenesis, driven by upregulated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α) in hypoxic lesions, is critical for endometriosis establishment and progression, while epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis contribute to disease pathology through dysregulation of microRNAs such as miR-145 and miR-200. Current therapeutic approaches including GnRH agonists, oral contraceptives, and progestins target estrogen production and progesterone signaling pathways. Three-dimensional organoid models have emerged as powerful tools for studying endometriosis pathogenesis and enabling high-throughput drug screening and personalized medicine strategies based on patient-derived tissue responses.

title: Abstracts of Scientific Presentations: 2024 AALAS National Meeting Nashville, Tennessee
journal: Journal of the American Association for Laboratory Animal Science : JAALAS
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes pelvic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. The disease involves elevated pro-inflammatory mediators including prostaglandin E2, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8, alongside infiltration of macrophages that perpetuate a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Abnormal angiogenesis driven by overexpressed VEGF and endothelial dysfunction supports lesion vascularization and growth, while dysregulated estrogen signaling through elevated estrogen receptor and aromatase expression promotes local estrogen synthesis. Aberrant immune tolerance mechanisms, including dysregulation of regulatory T cells, allow ectopic endometrial cells to evade immune surveillance. Additionally, TGF-β signaling promotes epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis, facilitating cell migration and lesion progression, while elevated oxidative stress further contributes to tissue damage and pathology.

title: Biomarker development for menstrual cycle affective change: the need for greater temporal, mechanistic, and phenotypic specificity
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation on the peritoneum, ovaries, and bowel, causing chronic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms. The condition involves excessive inflammation driven by endometrial stromal and epithelial cells that produce elevated levels of IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8, coupled with dysregulated estrogen signaling through increased aromatase expression and altered estrogen receptor ratios that favor receptor α over β. Angiogenesis, mediated by upregulated VEGF, is critical for lesion establishment and growth, while epithelial-mesenchymal transition promoted by TGF-β signaling facilitates endometrial cell invasion and fibrosis characterized by increased myofibroblasts and α-SMA expression. Progesterone resistance emerges as a hallmark feature due to elevated progesterone receptor β and reduced HDAC2 expression, limiting the efficacy of progestin-based treatments, while aberrant epigenetic modifications including altered DNA methylation, microRNA dysregulation (elevated miR-200, reduced miR-145), and histone modifications further perpetuate abnormal gene expression. Current biomarkers such as IL-6, TNF-α, and CA-125 show promise for diagnosis and disease assessment, while therapeutic approaches including GnRH agonists, progestins, aromatase inhibitors, and surgical resection provide symptomatic relief but highlight the need for improved mechanistic understanding and targeted interventions.

title: Endometriosis of the Diaphragm
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes pain and infertility in reproductive-age women through elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8. The disease involves recurrent genetic alterations, with KRAS mutations found in approximately half of endometriotic lesions and PTEN loss occurring as another common molecular feature that may drive lesion development and persistence. Emerging evidence supports stem cell-based therapeutic approaches, particularly mesenchymal stem cells, which leverage their inherent capacity for tissue repair and regeneration to potentially reverse endometriotic pathology. These findings suggest that endometriosis operates through a dual mechanism combining inflammatory dysregulation with specific genetic alterations, opening new therapeutic avenues beyond conventional treatment strategies. The diaphragmatic presentation of endometriosis highlights the systemic nature of this condition and underscores the importance of understanding both local inflammatory environments and systemic molecular drivers in developing comprehensive management strategies.

title: Towards new frontiers of healthcare systems research using artificial intelligence and generative AI
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by aberrant vascularization, neurogenesis, and immune dysfunction that collectively promote disease pathogenesis and clinical manifestations including infertility, pelvic pain, and dysmenorrhea. A critical molecular mechanism underlying endometriosis involves TGF-β signaling activation, which suppresses miR-145 expression in endometrial cells through Smad2/3-mediated transcriptional downregulation. The reduction in miR-145 subsequently permits upregulation of ZEB1 and ZEB2, transcription factors that promote epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), a key pathogenic process in endometriosis development. The miR-200 family provides an additional layer of regulation by directly targeting and downregulating ZEB transcription factors, though this protective mechanism appears insufficient in endometriotic tissue where ZEB2 expression is elevated. This TGF-β/miR-145/ZEB2/EMT axis represents a fundamental molecular pathway in endometriosis pathogenesis, with potential therapeutic implications for restoring microRNA-mediated suppression of EMT.

title: Abstracts from the 57th European Society of Human Genetics (ESHG) Conference: Oral Presentations
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting 10–15% of women of reproductive age, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue in the pelvis that causes pelvic pain and is associated with infertility in 20–50% of cases. The pathogenesis of endometriosis involves aberrant expression of growth factors, immune dysfunction, and angiogenesis, with multiple inflammatory cytokines playing central roles. Elevated levels of interleukins IL-6 and IL-8, tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) have been consistently observed in the peritoneal fluid and serum of women with endometriosis. These inflammatory and angiogenic markers suggest that dysregulated immune responses and aberrant vascularization drive the establishment and progression of ectopic endometrial lesions. Understanding these molecular mechanisms provides insight into the pathophysiology of endometriosis and may inform the development of targeted therapeutic strategies for this common gynecological disease.

title: Abstracts from the 57th European Society of Human Genetics (ESHG) Conference: Hybrid Posters
summary: Endometriosis, a major cause of infertility and chronic pelvic pain, involves dysregulated inflammatory and angiogenic pathways that drive disease pathogenesis. Multiple inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α are significantly elevated in endometriosis, alongside elevated prostaglandin levels, creating a pro-inflammatory microenvironment that perpetuates the condition. Angiogenesis, driven by elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), is a critical mechanism supporting endometrial lesion development and progression. Estrogen plays a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting both inflammatory signaling and angiogenic responses. These interconnected inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms collectively contribute to the establishment of ectopic endometrial tissue and the associated reproductive complications characteristic of endometriosis.

title: Endometriosis and the Risk of Melanoma: A Shared Thread?
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting reproductive-age women that manifests clinically as infertility and pelvic pain, driven by dysregulated inflammatory and angiogenic pathways. The NF-κB signaling pathway is constitutively activated in endometriosis and promotes elevated expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8 in both peritoneal fluid and serum. Estrogen signaling and progesterone resistance represent key pathogenic mechanisms that sustain the inflammatory microenvironment, supported by increased macrophage infiltration that perpetuates cytokine production. Enhanced angiogenesis mediated by elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) contributes to lesion formation and the persistence of ectopic endometrial tissue. The convergence of these inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms creates a permissive microenvironment that supports endometrial cell survival outside the uterus and underlies the clinical sequelae of infertility and pain associated with endometriosis.

title: Endometriosis Associated with Periodontal Disease: Two Case Reports
summary: This case report documents an important clinical association between endometriosis and periodontal disease, demonstrating that women with endometriosis have a 57% higher prevalence of periodontitis. Both conditions share a common inflammatory pathophysiology characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and mediators including prostaglandin E2, vascular endothelial growth factor, and reactive oxygen species in peritoneal and oral tissues. Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition affecting the pelvic peritoneum, ovaries, and rectovaginal septum, appears to trigger or exacerbate periodontal disease through hormonal fluctuations that promote oral inflammation and increased microbial susceptibility. The presented cases illustrate how endometriosis patients developed significant gingival bleeding and tooth mobility, conditions potentially worsened by progestin-based therapeutic agents used to manage endometriosis. These findings suggest that the systemic inflammatory burden and oxidative stress characteristic of endometriosis may extend beyond reproductive tissues to compromise periodontal health, highlighting the need for integrated clinical management addressing both gynecological and oral pathology.

title: The Colombian Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology: seventy-five years dedicated to documenting advances in health, obstetrics, and gynecology in Colombia
journal: Revista Colombiana de Obstetricia y Ginecología
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic condition affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue in ectopic locations and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain. The pathophysiology of endometriosis involves increased angiogenesis, neuroangiogenesis, and inflammation, which collectively contribute to disease progression and symptom severity. TRPV1, a ligand-gated ion channel expressed in sensory neurons, immune cells, endothelial cells, and epithelial cells, plays a critical role in both pain sensation and inflammatory responses through activation by ligands such as capsaicin, heat, and acid. TRPV1 has been identified as associated with endometriosis pathogenesis, implicating this ion channel in the disease's inflammatory and pain mechanisms. The investigation of capsaicin as a therapeutic intervention demonstrates potential analgesic effects on endometriosis-associated pain, suggesting that TRPV1 modulation may represent a novel therapeutic approach for managing endometriosis symptoms. These findings highlight the interconnection between inflammatory pathways, neuroimmune regulation, and pain sensation in endometriosis and support further investigation of TRPV1-targeted therapies.

title: Editorial: Advances in the pharmacotherapy of chronic pelvic pain conditions
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is strongly associated with both chronic pelvic pain and infertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple interconnected mechanisms, including elevated inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8) in the peritoneal fluid that drive inflammation and angiogenesis, with increased VEGF expression supporting abnormal vascularization of lesions. Estrogen plays a central role through enhanced aromatase expression in ectopic tissue and estrogen receptor signaling, while progesterone resistance further promotes lesion establishment and persistence. Cellular components including endometrial stromal and epithelial cells contribute through altered adhesion molecule expression that facilitates ectopic implantation and abnormal migration within an inflammatory peritoneal microenvironment. This comprehensive understanding of endometriosis pathophysiology involving inflammatory, angiogenic, hormonal, and cellular mechanisms provides multiple therapeutic targets for developing improved pharmacological treatments to address the significant clinical burden of this condition.

title: FAP+ activated fibroblasts are detectable in the microenvironment of endometriosis and correlate with stroma composition and infiltrating CD8+ and CD68+ cells
summary:
# Summary

Fibroblast activation protein-α (FAP)-expressing activated fibroblasts are prominently detected in endometriotic lesions across all morphological subtypes and directly correlate with key pathological features of the lesion microenvironment, including elevated collagen and iron content. FAP expression also significantly associates with increased infiltration of CD8+ cytotoxic T cells and CD68+ macrophages, suggesting these cells participate in shaping the inflammatory stroma composition. Iron accumulation in lesions—which increases with lesion age—contributes to pathological macrophage activation, triggering iron-mediated oxidative stress and chronic inflammation that drives fibrosis through the TGFβ1/Smad3 signaling pathway and extracellular matrix production. FAP-positive fibroblasts interact with activated macrophages to create an immunosuppressive microenvironment that may impair T cell immunosurveillance, paralleling mechanisms observed in solid tumors. These findings identify activated fibroblasts as central orchestrators of endometriosis pathology, linking recurrent hormonal stimulation, chronic bleeding, iron accumulation, and dysregulated immune responses into a unified pathogenic mechanism.

title: Potential biomarkers and immune infiltration linking endometriosis with recurrent pregnancy loss based on bioinformatics and machine learning
summary: Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease causing dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility, is epidemiologically linked to recurrent pregnancy loss through shared pathophysiological mechanisms involving immune dysregulation. Bioinformatics and machine learning analysis identified three common core genes—MAN2A1, PAPSS1, and RIBC2—that are significantly associated with both conditions and correlate strongly with infiltration of immune cells, suggesting immune heterogeneity as a critical pathological feature. These core genes are implicated in multiple signaling pathways including PI3K-Akt, p53, MAPK, and Wnt cascades, as well as GTPase regulator activity, which are dysregulated in both endometriosis and recurrent pregnancy loss. The elevated expression of these genes in endometriosis, combined with immune cell infiltration patterns, provides potential biomarkers for patient stratification and disease prognosis. Additionally, MAN2A1 and RIBC2 may play roles in endometrial carcinoma development, suggesting broader implications for endometrial pathology. This integrated analysis establishes a mechanistic link between endometriosis and recurrent pregnancy loss centered on aberrant gene expression and immune dysfunction, offering candidate biomarkers for improved clinical diagnosis and therapeutic targeting.

title: Association between serum carotenoids levels and endometriosis risk: evidence from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the association between serum carotenoid levels and endometriosis risk using data from the National Health and Nutrition Examination Survey. The key finding was that higher serum levels of lutein and zeaxanthin were significantly associated with reduced endometriosis risk, particularly in individuals under 40 years old, whereas other carotenoids (α-carotene, β-carotene, β-cryptoxanthin, and trans-lycopene) showed no significant associations. The protective mechanism appears to involve lutein and zeaxanthin's potent antioxidant and anti-inflammatory properties, which neutralize reactive oxygen species and reduce the expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and IL-1β—both implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. Since endometriosis is characterized by chronic inflammation driven by elevated cytokine levels and oxidative stress, the suppression of these pathways by lutein and zeaxanthin may mitigate disease risk. Additionally, these carotenoids may influence estrogen metabolism and receptor activity, offering a secondary protective mechanism given endometriosis's estrogen-dependent nature. These findings suggest that dietary or supplemental lutein and zeaxanthin may represent a nutritional strategy to reduce endometriosis risk.

title: Editorial: Insights in female reproductive longevity
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by the presence of endometrial lesions in the peritoneal cavity and is strongly associated with infertility and debilitating pelvic pain. The pathophysiology of endometriosis involves dysregulated inflammatory signaling, wherein macrophages produce elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α, while NF-κB pathway activation further amplifies inflammatory responses. Enhanced angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis, driven by elevated VEGF expression, promote the survival and establishment of ectopic endometrial lesions. Additionally, increased local estrogen production through elevated aromatase expression and subsequent estrogen receptor β overexpression perpetuate lesion growth and persistence. TGF-β elevation promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition, facilitating endometrial cell invasion and disease progression. These interconnected inflammatory, angiogenic, and hormonal mechanisms collectively drive endometriosis pathogenesis and represent therapeutic targets for managing this debilitating condition.

title: Correction: A rare case of concomitant endometrioid adenocarcinoma arising from uterine adenomyosis and clear cell carcinoma arising from parametrial deep endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth primarily in the peritoneal cavity and is associated with significant morbidity including infertility and chronic pelvic pain. The oxytocin receptor (OXTR) plays a critical role in endometrial receptivity and embryo implantation, but its expression is substantially downregulated in endometriosis through multiple inflammatory pathways. Elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α, which are hallmarks of endometriosis, actively suppress OXTR expression via the NF-κB signaling pathway, thereby impairing endometrial receptivity and contributing to endometriosis-related infertility. Additionally, endometriotic lesions exhibit progesterone resistance characterized by reduced progesterone receptor expression, further compromising the endometrium's capacity to support embryo implantation. Collectively, these findings reveal that chronic inflammation in endometriosis disrupts the delicate balance of molecular factors essential for fertility, with dysregulated OXTR signaling serving as a key mechanistic link between inflammatory pathways and reproductive dysfunction.

title: Analysis of inflammatory biomarkers IL-6, vascular endothelial growth factor and matrix metalloproteinases-9 expression in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study examined three inflammatory biomarkers—interleukin-6 (IL-6), vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), and matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9)—as potential contributors to endometriosis pathogenesis. All three markers were significantly elevated in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissues of endometriosis patients compared to controls, with MMP-9 showing particularly strong associations with disease severity according to rASRM classification. MMP-9 and other cytokines (IL-8, TNF-α) promote angiogenesis and endothelial cell proliferation in ectopic endometrium, facilitating the inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of endometriosis. Notably, MMP-9 expression was also significantly elevated in peripheral blood and peritoneal fluid, suggesting it may serve as a noninvasive biomarker for diagnosing endometriosis and monitoring postoperative recurrence. These findings highlight the central role of inflammatory cytokines and matrix-degrading enzymes in endometriosis development and support the potential clinical utility of MMP-9 measurement in patient assessment.

title: Tetramethylpyrazine Confers Protection Against Oxidative Stress and NLRP3-Dependent Pyroptosis in Rats with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that tetramethylpyrazine (TMP) protects against endometriosis by suppressing oxidative stress and NLRP3-dependent pyroptosis in a rat model. Endometriosis is characterized by elevated oxidative stress markers (ROS, MDA) and reduced antioxidant defenses (SOD, GSH, CAT), which drive chronic inflammation and activate the NLRP3 inflammasome pathway, leading to increased pyroptotic cell death and elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β, IL-18). TMP treatment significantly reduced endometriotic lesion size and tissue damage while decreasing ROS and MDA levels while increasing antioxidant enzyme activity. The protective mechanism involves dual inhibition of NLRP3 inflammasome components (NLRP3, ASC, caspase-1, GSDMD) and their downstream pro-inflammatory products, combined with activation of the Nrf2/HO-1 antioxidant pathway. These findings suggest that TMP-mediated suppression of oxidative stress and pyroptosis represents a promising therapeutic approach for endometriosis management.

title: Pedicled Anterolateral Thigh Flap: A Potential Workhorse Flap for Complex Regional Reconstruction—A Tertiary Cancer Center Experience
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition fundamentally linked to dysregulation of lipid metabolism, which plays a central role in disease pathogenesis. Altered lipid profiles in endometriosis patients are associated with endometrial dysfunction and contribute to disease progression through lipid accumulation in affected tissues. The chronic inflammatory state characteristic of endometriosis appears to be intimately connected with aberrant lipid metabolic pathways, suggesting that lipid dysregulation is not merely a secondary consequence but rather an active driver of pathologic endometrial remodeling. These findings highlight the importance of lipid metabolism as a potential therapeutic target in managing endometriosis, as interventions addressing lipid accumulation and metabolic imbalance may help mitigate both the inflammatory environment and functional endometrial abnormalities that perpetuate the disease.

title: Alteration of Serum Gal-3 Levels in Endometrium-Related Reproductive Disorders
summary:
# Summary

Galectin-3 (Gal-3) is significantly elevated in serum of endometriosis patients and represents a key inflammatory mediator involved in the pathophysiology of this condition. As a beta-galactoside-binding protein expressed in the endometrium and decidua, Gal-3 regulates inflammation, autoimmunity, cell adhesion, migration, and chemotaxis—processes central to endometriosis development and progression. Notably, serum Gal-3 levels progressively increase during one-year follow-up in endometriosis patients, with higher concentrations observed in mild-to-severe stages compared to controls, though diagnostic utility remains limited. Galectin-9 is similarly elevated in endometriosis and expressed in ectopic implants and peritoneal cells, suggesting coordinated galectin involvement in ectopic lesion establishment. Beyond its inflammatory role, Gal-3 appears implicated in endometriosis-associated complications including infertility through progesterone resistance and malignant transformation to endometrioid carcinoma and clear cell ovarian cancer via NK-κB signaling regulation. These findings establish galectins as potential biomarkers and therapeutic targets for endometriosis and its serious sequelae.

title: Exploring the Association Between Dietary Fruit Intake and Endometriosis: A Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis affects approximately 70% of patients with chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, conditions driven partly by oxidative stress and inflammatory pathways. While meta-analytic evidence reveals no consistent protective effect from daily or weekly fruit consumption, specific fruit types and bioactive compounds show promise: citrus fruit consumption reduces endometriosis risk by 22%, and polyphenols like resveratrol and fisetin downregulate key inflammatory mediators including VEGF, TNF-α, and the NLRP-3 inflammasome pathway. Conversely, Western dietary patterns increase endometriosis risk by 27%, while pesticide residues from certain fruits may exacerbate disease through estrogenic properties. The relationship between fruit intake and endometriosis risk appears nuanced, mediated by pesticide exposure, glycemic index, and the specific antioxidant composition of individual fruits rather than fruit consumption alone.

title: Increased antinuclear (anti-Sm) antibodies in infertile patients with
summary: Endometriosis, a prevalent disease associated with chronic pelvic pain and infertility, involves dysregulated immune mechanisms including elevated antinuclear antibodies. This study identified significantly elevated anti-Sm antibodies in infertile patients with endometriosis, particularly those with minimal to mild disease, establishing the first association between peritoneal endometriosis and anti-Sm autoantibodies. Anti-Sm antibodies, known markers of systemic lupus erythematosus activity, can form immune complexes that disturb connective tissue and vascular compartments, promoting cellular invasion and neoangiogenesis—processes central to endometriotic lesion development. The findings suggest that minimal/mild endometriosis exhibits more active immunological features than moderate or severe disease, highlighting autoimmunity as an underappreciated aspect of early-stage endometriosis pathophysiology. These results establish a mechanistic link between anti-Sm autoimmunity and endometriosis-associated infertility, potentially informing immune-modulation strategies to improve pregnancy outcomes in assisted reproductive technology protocols.

title: Integration of Single Cell and Bulk RNA-Sequencing Reveals Key Genes and Immune Cell Infiltration to Construct a Predictive Model and Identify Drug Targets in Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic neuroinflammatory disease characterized by transcriptomic alterations and aberrant immune cell infiltration in the proliferative eutopic endometrium, pathological changes that significantly contribute to disease pathogenesis and the associated chronic pelvic pain and elevated cancer risk. Integration of single-cell and bulk RNA-sequencing identified eight key genes—SYNE2, TXN, NUPR1, CTSK, GSN, MGP, IER2, and CXCL12—as biomarkers of endometriosis, with mesenchymal cells emerging as major contributors to disease development. Immune profiling revealed elevated CD8+ T cells and monocytes in the eutopic endometrium, alongside dysregulation of macrophages, NK cells, T cells, and B cells, indicating that immune cell dysfunction is central to endometriosis pathophysiology. Current pharmacological treatments including GnRH agonists, GnRH antagonists, combined oral contraceptives, and progestins provide hormonal suppression, while drug target prediction identified retinol, orantinib, piperacillin, and NECA as candidate therapeutics negatively correlated with disease markers. These findings establish a predictive model based on immune infiltration patterns and key gene signatures that could inform personalized therapeutic strategies targeting both the transcriptomic dysregulation and immune dysfunction underlying endometriosis.

title: NOD1, NOD2, PYDC1, and PYDC2 gene polymorphisms in ovarian endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates the role of innate immune pathway gene polymorphisms in ovarian endometriosis, focusing on NOD-like receptors (NLRs) and pyrin domain-containing proteins (PYDCs). The NLR pathway regulates interleukins and NF-κB signaling, which drives pro-inflammatory cytokine production and supports endometriosis lesion formation. Specific genetic variants showed strong associations with endometriosis severity and clinical symptoms: the NOD1 rs2075820 GG genotype was elevated in endometriosis patients, while the AA genotype correlated with infertility and gastrointestinal symptoms; the PYDC2 rs293833 variant was associated with larger endometriomas, infertility, and perimenstrual gastrointestinal complaints. Functionally, NOD1 is upregulated in ectopic endometrial stromal cells, and NOD1 inhibition suppresses their proliferation and interleukin-8 production, suggesting a therapeutic target. These findings highlight how genetic polymorphisms in innate immune regulators influence endometriosis pathogenesis and clinical manifestations, with potential implications for personalized risk stratification and targeted anti-inflammatory treatment strategies.

title: Expanding the women’s health paradigm beyond reproductive boundaries
journal: The Indian Journal of Medical Research
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting reproductive-age women that causes both pelvic pain and infertility. The pathophysiology of endometriosis involves dysregulated endometrial stromal cells, which produce elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-6 (IL-6) and interleukin-8 (IL-8). These cytokines are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients and drive the inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of the disease. The heightened production of IL-6 and IL-8 by endometrial stromal cells perpetuates the chronic inflammatory state underlying both the pain symptoms and reproductive dysfunction associated with endometriosis.

title: Shared diagnostic biomarkers and underlying mechanisms between endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure
summary: Endometriosis and recurrent implantation failure (RIF) share common underlying pathological mechanisms and diagnostic biomarkers, according to this integrative genomic analysis. Both conditions involve dysregulated extracellular matrix remodeling, abnormal immune infiltration characterized by elevated plasma cells, M2 macrophages, and activated mast cells, as well as alterations in immune microenvironment that impair endometrial receptivity. The study identified 708 differentially expressed genes in endometriosis and 304 in RIF, with EHF emerging as a key diagnostic biomarker that is significantly downregulated in endometriosis but upregulated in RIF, potentially linking these two conditions. Pathway analysis revealed that immune response and collagen-containing extracellular matrix pathways are activated in both disorders, involving dysregulated functions of natural killer cells, macrophages, and T cells in embryo implantation. Cancer-associated genes show elevated mutation frequencies in endometriotic epithelium, and additional gene signatures including CAV2, RAD51AP1, PAX2, and others were identified as diagnostic candidates for endometriosis. These shared molecular features suggest that endometriosis-induced infertility may operate through mechanisms that compromise endometrial receptivity similar to those underlying RIF, offering potential therapeutic targets for managing both conditions.

title: The long road of drug development for endometriosis – Pains, gains, and hopes
journal: Journal of controlled release : official journal of the Controlled Release Society
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial lesions that cause significant clinical manifestations including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The pathogenesis involves retrograde menstruation and recruitment of endometrial stem/progenitor cells (identifiable by markers such as SUSD2, PDGFRβ, CD146 in mesenchymal stem cells and CDH2, SSEA1, AXIN2, SOX9 in epithelial progenitor cells), along with numerous genetic susceptibility loci including WNT4, IL1A, KDR, and cancer-related genes such as ARID1A and PTEN. Ectopic lesions trigger inflammation, scarring, and tissue adhesions that activate peripheral pain pathways and promote neuroangiogenesis, with elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNFα, IL-1β, IL-8, prostaglandins) driving chronic inflammation and neuropathic pain while also reducing ovarian response and sperm function to impair fertility. Central sensitization further exacerbates pain perception, with structural changes in grey matter documented in patients with endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain. Current first-line treatments include nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for dysmenorrhea and oral contraceptives for pelvic pain, which inhibit GnRH synthesis and release to suppress gonadotropin levels and reduce lesion growth.

title: Better Late Than Early? The Role of the Vascular Endothelium in Late-Onset Menopause
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease strongly associated with infertility, dysmenorrhea, and pelvic pain, with pathogenesis involving multiple interconnected mechanisms centered on inflammation, angiogenesis, and estrogen dysregulation. The peritoneal microenvironment in endometriosis shows elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α, alongside increased peritoneal macrophage infiltration and altered T cell, B cell, and natural killer cell function that collectively impair immune surveillance. At the tissue level, endometrial cells from women with endometriosis exhibit elevated aromatase expression and estrogen receptor α while showing reduced estrogen receptor β, creating a local estrogen-rich environment that perpetuates disease. Both angiogenesis and neuroangiogenesis are critical pathogenic processes in endometriosis, facilitating abnormal vascular and nerve fiber development that supports lesion growth and contributes to pain symptoms. These findings highlight the complex interplay between immune dysfunction, inflammatory signaling, estrogen-dependent mechanisms, and aberrant vascular remodeling that drives endometriosis pathogenesis and its associated clinical manifestations.

title: Retraction Note: The comparison of two different protocols ultra-long versus medroxyprogesterone acetate in women with ovarian endometriosis: a prospective randomized controlled trial
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women, involves complex pathophysiological mechanisms centered on inflammation and angiogenesis that drive its primary symptoms of chronic pelvic pain and infertility. Local estrogen synthesis in ectopic endometrial tissue is enhanced through elevated aromatase expression and upregulated estrogen receptor-α, promoting aberrant cell proliferation while progesterone resistance develops through reduced PR-B and FKBP52 expression. Multiple microRNAs, particularly the elevated miR-200 family and miR-21, drive epithelial-mesenchymal transition and activate inflammatory pathways including NF-κB signaling, complemented by increased oxidative stress from elevated reactive oxygen species and impaired antioxidant defenses. Peritoneal immune dysregulation manifests through macrophage infiltration, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), increased T and B cell populations, and autoantibody production, while reduced miR-145 expression fails to suppress VEGF-mediated angiogenesis critical for lesion survival and growth. Enhanced nerve fiber innervation with elevated TRPV1 and substance P expression contributes to chronic pain pathogenesis. Current therapeutic approaches target these mechanisms through hormone suppression (GnRH agonists, progestins, aromatase inhibitors), symptomatic management (NSAIDs), and surgical intervention, though therapeutic resistance remains a clinical challenge.

title: The Role of Adenogenesis Factors in the Pathogenesis of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, results from multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, stem cell involvement, and epigenetic modifications. The disease is characterized by ectopic endometrial-like cells outside the uterus that trigger a localized chronic inflammatory response, oxidative stress, and angiogenesis, collectively producing the hallmark symptoms of infertility, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. Estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) plays a central pathogenic role by suppressing estrogen receptor alpha and subsequently regulating apoptosis, the inflammasome, mitochondrial function, and the expression of inflammatory mediators such as cyclooxygenase-2 and matrix metalloproteinase-1. Additionally, adenogenesis factors and developmental genes like WNT4 and bone morphogenetic proteins—normally governing Müllerian duct development—are aberrantly expressed in endometriotic tissue, suggesting that endometriosis shares molecular parallels with embryogenesis. Exposure to endocrine-disrupting chemicals and mutations in key developmental genes may predispose to both Müllerian duct anomalies and endometriosis, indicating that defective embryonic patterning pathways contribute to disease susceptibility and progression.

title: A lesion mimicking malignancy in the cesarean scar: Decidualize endometriosis
journal: The Kaohsiung Journal of Medical Sciences
summary: This article examines endometriosis as a benign gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that commonly develops in the peritoneum and ovaries, causing chronic pelvic pain and infertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple interconnected mechanisms including pathological angiogenesis and neovascularization driven by elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) in peritoneal fluid, as well as a chronic inflammatory state marked by increased levels of IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and IL-1β. Neuroinflammation contributes significantly to disease progression, with elevated nerve growth factor promoting abnormally high nerve fiber density in endometrial lesions that directly mediates chronic pain development. Bone marrow-derived stem cells and endometrial stem/progenitor cells participate in both lesion formation and endometrial regeneration, perpetuating the disease cycle. Therapeutic strategies targeting angiogenesis, including VEGF inhibitors and broader anti-angiogenic agents, have been proposed as promising approaches to suppress lesion growth and reduce symptoms. This comprehensive understanding of endometriosis's inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms provides a foundation for developing mechanism-based treatments beyond current therapeutic options.

title: The left venous anatomy of the female pelvis and clinical outcomes: new insights
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, affecting 10-15% of reproductive-aged women, is a chronic gynecological condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation that triggers chronic inflammation and is strongly associated with infertility and pelvic pain. The inflammatory microenvironment in endometriotic lesions is driven by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α, alongside increased macrophage infiltration and oxidative stress in peritoneal fluid. Angiogenesis, promoted by VEGF and critical for lesion establishment and growth, is further supported by abnormally elevated aromatase expression within ectopic lesions that drives local estrogen production and endometrial cell proliferation. Additional pathological features include progesterone resistance due to reduced progesterone receptor expression and increased nerve fiber innervation in lesions, the latter contributing to the associated pain symptoms. These interconnected inflammatory, vascular, and neuroendocrine mechanisms collectively explain how endometriotic lesions establish, persist, and cause the characteristic clinical manifestations of infertility and chronic pelvic pain.

title: FOXA2 loss results in an increase of endometriosis development and LIF reveals a therapeutic effect for endometriosis
journal: FASEB journal : official publication of the Federation of American Societies for Experimental Biology
summary:
# Summary

The transcription factor FOXA2, which is exclusively expressed in the uterine glandular epithelium, plays a critical protective role in endometriosis by regulating endometrial proliferation, hormone response, and expression of key downstream factors including leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) and the chemokine CXCL15. FOXA2 expression is significantly reduced in the eutopic endometrium of women and mice with endometriosis, and this loss leads to increased ectopic lesion formation through multiple mechanisms including downregulation of progesterone receptor expression and impaired progesterone signaling, ultimately contributing to the progesterone resistance characteristic of the disease. FOXA2 deficiency also disrupts CXCL15-mediated immune regulation and epithelial integrity, potentially promoting epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and increased lesion invasiveness. Notably, therapeutic administration of recombinant LIF significantly reduced ectopic lesion formation in FOXA2-deficient mice by restoring critical glandular functions and modulating local peritoneal immune responses, suggesting LIF as a promising therapeutic target for endometriosis treatment.

title: Paul W. Turke, Review of Bringing Up Baby: An Evolutionary View of Pediatrics
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women and is strongly associated with infertility, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth in the peritoneal cavity. The pathogenesis involves multifactorial, complex interactions between endometrial epithelial cells, endometrial stromal cells, immune cells, and peritoneal cells through endometrial-peritoneal cross-talk mechanisms. Key pathogenic drivers include increased production of inflammatory cytokines, abnormal angiogenesis, and elevated nerve fiber density within ectopic lesions. These interconnected inflammatory and vascular mechanisms create an environment that promotes lesion establishment and progression while simultaneously impairing reproductive function. Understanding these cellular and molecular interactions is essential for developing targeted therapeutic strategies to manage this debilitating condition and restore fertility in affected women.

title: Unveiling Potential Blood Markers for Endometriosis Through the Integration and Experimental Validation of Immune Cell Traits Genome and Genome-Wide Associated Data
summary: This study integrated genome-wide association data with immune cell trait analysis to identify CD28 expression on double-negative (CD4−CD8−) T cells as a potential blood biomarker for endometriosis. Elevated CD28 levels on DN T cells were demonstrated to causally associate with endometriosis risk and were significantly elevated in both the peripheral blood and ectopic endometrial tissue of affected patients. DN T cells contribute to endometrial pathology by producing pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-4, IL-17, TNF-α, and IFN-γ, which promote a shift toward a Th2-dominant immune response that facilitates disease progression. The study revealed a dysregulated immune microenvironment characterized by reduced natural killer cell activity and decreased protective immune populations (M2 macrophages, CD163+ macrophages, and resting mast cells) in endometrial tissue, contrasting with elevated CD8+ T cells and activated NK cells. These findings suggest that CD28 expression on DN T cells could serve as a novel diagnostic marker and potential immunotherapeutic target for endometriosis, offering advantages over current diagnostic approaches such as laparoscopic surgery and serum cancer antigen 125 testing. The research advances understanding of how immune dysregulation drives endometriosis pathogenesis and identifies candidate biomarkers for improved clinical management.

title: Effect of dietary patterns and nutritional supplementation in the management of endometriosis: a review
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease characterized by oxidative stress and inflammation that manifests as dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. The pathophysiology involves a complex inflammatory cascade: hemoglobin and iron release from retrograde menstruation activates peritoneal macrophages to produce pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, TNF-α), which trigger downstream signaling through NF-κB and ERK pathways to promote cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and lesion invasion. Dietary patterns and specific nutrient supplementation—particularly green vegetables, fresh fruits, dairy products, legumes, polyphenols, and fish oil—effectively reduce endometriosis symptoms by modulating inflammatory factors and angiogenic pathways, while conversely, high intake of red meat and trans-fatty acids increases disease risk. Key therapeutic targets include anti-inflammatory interventions (IL-37b), antioxidant strategies to counteract iron-induced oxidative stress, and modulation of the PI3K/AKT/mTOR and ERK signaling axes that drive endometrial cell proliferation and angiogenesis.

title: A novel senotherapeutic strategy with azithromycin for preventing endometriosis progression
summary:
# Summary

Cellular senescence drives endometriosis pathogenesis through the senescence-associated secretory phenotype (SASP), which promotes chronic inflammation and tissue remodeling. Cumulus-surrounding cells isolated from endometriotic lesions exhibit elevated senescence markers including SA-β-Gal, p16INK4a, and IL-6, along with reduced LaminB1 expression, and secrete elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines that regulate endometrial proliferation and fibrosis. Azithromycin functions as a senomorphic agent that suppresses IL-6 expression in senescent cells and significantly reduces endometriotic lesion volume, proliferative activity, and fibrosis in a murine endometriosis model. This novel senotherapeutic approach targets the cellular senescence pathway rather than estrogen signaling, offering a non-hormonal strategy for preventing endometriosis progression and potentially resolving associated infertility. The findings suggest azithromycin may interrupt the pathogenic cycle whereby senescent cell SASP perpetuates chronic inflammation and lesion expansion in endometriosis.

title: A Narrative Review of the Expression and Role of Nitric Oxide in Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Nitric oxide (NO) emerges as a critical signaling molecule in endometriosis pathogenesis, with all three NO synthase isoforms (nNOS, eNOS, and iNOS) playing significant roles in disease development. NO is elevated in ectopic endometrium, peritoneal fluid, and macrophages of endometriosis patients, and its elevated levels directly correlate with key clinical symptoms including pelvic pain, infertility, and pregnancy complications by inhibiting sperm function, oocyte fertilization, and embryo development. The inflammatory cascade in endometriosis involves NF-κB activation of iNOS and pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), while eNOS upregulation—driven by inflammatory cytokines and estrogen—promotes pathological angiogenesis despite reducing αvβ3 integrin expression necessary for normal endometrial function. Genetic variations, particularly the T allele of the NOS3 Glu298Asp polymorphism, confer increased disease susceptibility, including a ten-fold risk of advanced-stage endometriosis. Therapeutic approaches targeting NO dysregulation, such as iNOS downregulation via clotrimazole or TNF-α inhibition with infliximab, show promise in reducing endometriotic lesions, suggesting NO modulation as a novel treatment strategy for this persistent inflammatory disorder affecting 5–15% of reproductive-aged women.

title: The Molecular and Cellular Mechanisms of Endometriosis: From Basic Pathophysiology to Clinical Implications
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological disorder characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causes significant morbidity through chronic pain and infertility via multiple interconnected mechanisms. The disease initiates through retrograde menstruation, vascular-lymphatic dissemination, and coelomic metaplasia, with endometrial mesenchymal stem cells and epithelial progenitors establishing lesions through altered integrin and cadherin-mediated adhesion to peritoneal surfaces. Dysregulated signaling pathways including PI3K/Akt, Wnt/β-catenin, and JAK/STAT drive lesion proliferation, immune evasion, and angiogenesis, while elevated estradiol and progesterone resistance—mediated by estrogen receptor imbalance and epigenetic alterations—promote ectopic cell survival and growth. The chronic inflammatory microenvironment is sustained by macrophage-derived cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6), reduced natural killer cell function, prostaglandin E2 production, and recurrent lesion bleeding that triggers thrombin-mediated proliferation via protease-activated receptors. Current therapies target estrogen suppression through GnRH agonists, while emerging approaches focus on inhibiting PI3K/Akt, JAK/STAT pathways and anti-angiogenic strategies targeting VEGF and TGF-β to address the multifactorial pathophysiology underlying endometriosis-associated pain and infertility.

title: Endometriosis and Infertility: Gynecological Examination Practical Guide
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition affecting 10-15% of women of reproductive age, is strongly associated with infertility through multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms. The disease arises through several proposed pathways including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, and Müllerian residues, with bone marrow-derived stem cells potentially contributing to lesion development. A central pathogenic mechanism involves dysregulated estrogen metabolism, where increased aromatase activity and reduced 17β-hydroxysteroid dehydrogenase type 2 expression elevate local estradiol levels, which stimulates prostaglandin E2 production in a self-perpetuating cycle that further promotes aromatase activity and progesterone resistance. The peritoneal environment becomes markedly pro-inflammatory, with elevated macrophages and interleukin-6 (IL-6) promoting ectopic endometrial cell survival, proliferation, and angiogenesis while simultaneously disrupting ovulation, tubal transport, and embryo implantation. Endometriosis causes significant clinical morbidity including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and dyschezia, with diagnostic assessment facilitated by transvaginal ultrasound and classification systems such as rASRM, ENZIAN, and the Endometriosis Fertility Index to predict surgical outcomes and natural conception rates. The condition frequently co-occurs with adenomyosis and involves an autoimmune component characterized by elevated IgG and IgA antibodies, compounding its impact on reproductive function.

title: Serum copper to zinc ratio and risk of endometriosis: Insights from a case–control study in infertile patients
summary: This case-control study reveals that altered trace element homeostasis, particularly an elevated serum copper-to-zinc (Cu/Zn) ratio and decreased zinc levels, is significantly associated with endometriosis risk in infertile patients. Zinc deficiency compromises antioxidant enzyme activity, including Cu/Zn superoxide dismutase, while excess copper acts as a pro-oxidant and catalyzes the Fenton reaction, collectively exacerbating oxidative stress—a key pathogenic mechanism in endometriosis. The study demonstrates a strong inverse correlation between serum zinc levels and endometriosis risk, alongside a positive correlation between the Cu/Zn ratio and disease risk, with both exhibiting linear dose-response relationships. The endometriosis group also displayed characteristic hormonal alterations including elevated follicle-stimulating hormone and estradiol alongside reduced anti-Müllerian hormone levels. These findings suggest that trace element imbalances may contribute to the aberrant inflammatory response, immune dysregulation, and oxidative stress underlying endometriosis pathogenesis, potentially offering a new biomarker for disease diagnosis and risk stratification.

title: Neutrophils initiate proinflammatory immune responses in early endometriosis lesion development
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that neutrophils play a critical role in initiating endometriosis lesion formation through hormone-independent immune mechanisms. Women with endometriosis have significantly elevated levels of aged and proangiogenic neutrophils in menstrual effluent and peritoneal fluid, with these specialized neutrophil populations promoting endometrial cell survival, adhesion to the peritoneal cavity, and angiogenesis through fibrinogen expression and extracellular trap formation. CXCR2-mediated neutrophil recruitment is essential for lesion attachment and development, as blocking CXCR2 with α-CXCR2 antibodies reduced neutrophil recruitment, decreased expression of neutrophil-associated markers (S100A8, S100A9), and inhibited lesion attachment by 39% in a mouse model. The study further identifies elevated expression of pro-inflammatory genes including IL-6, MMP3, ITGB2, and VEGFA in developing lesions, all dependent on neutrophil infiltration. These findings establish neutrophils as key initiators of endometriosis pathology, suggesting that CXCR2 inhibition represents a potential therapeutic strategy to prevent early lesion formation and the associated symptoms of dysmenorrhea, chronic pain, and infertility.

title: Correction: Macrophages originated IL-33/ST2 inhibits ferroptosis in endometriosis via the ATF3/SLC7A11 axis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a common gynecological disorder characterized by chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and pathological features including abnormal angiogenesis, inflammation, and fibrosis of endometrial lesions. The stromal vascular fraction (SVF) derived from adipose tissue is enriched with pro-angiogenic cells that produce cytokines and growth factors, including VEGF, bFGF, and angiopoietins, which are critical regulators of angiogenesis. Adipose-derived SVF administration has shown therapeutic promise in preclinical endometriosis models by reducing lesion burden and pain symptoms through its immunomodulatory properties and promotion of tissue repair. These findings suggest that SVF cell-based therapy represents a potential regenerative medicine approach for treating endometriosis and its associated clinical manifestations.

title: Adenomyosis: an underacknowledged cause of abnormal uterine bleeding and pelvic pain
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, is mechanistically driven by aberrant TGF-β/Smad signaling that promotes epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT) and fibrosis. TGF-β1, chronically elevated in endometriotic lesions through production by infiltrating macrophages and fibroblasts, activates phosphorylation of Smad2/3 and upregulates EMT transcription factors (Snail and Slug), leading to downregulation of E-cadherin, upregulation of N-cadherin, and subsequent myofibroblast differentiation with excessive collagen deposition. A critical pathogenic feature is progesterone resistance in endometriosis, characterized by reduced PR-B expression, which normally suppresses TGF-β-induced EMT by reducing Smad2/3 phosphorylation and maintaining E-cadherin expression. Endometrial epithelial cells from endometriosis patients demonstrate enhanced susceptibility to TGF-β1-induced EMT and a blunted response to progesterone inhibition, explaining reduced treatment efficacy. The findings suggest that targeting the TGF-β/Smad signaling pathway or overcoming progesterone resistance represents promising therapeutic strategies to suppress EMT, fibrosis, and ectopic lesion establishment in endometriosis.

title: 2025 Canadian Urological Association Guideline: Selected treatment recommendations for interstitial cystitis/bladder pain syndrome
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue and is strongly associated with chronic pelvic pain and infertility. Emerging evidence indicates that aberrant endometrial stem cell function contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis, as normal endometrial stem cells are essential for endometrial regeneration and homeostasis.
