The Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway, a key regulator of stem cell self-renewal and differentiation, appears dysregulated in endometriosis, with elevated β-catenin expression observed in endometrial lesions from affected patients compared to controls. Both endogenous endometrial stem cells and bone marrow-derived stem cells normally participate in endometrial repair and regeneration, but this regenerative capacity is compromised in endometriosis. Stem cell-based therapies have emerged as a promising therapeutic approach for endometriosis, offering potential to restore aberrant stem cell function and symptom relief. These findings suggest that targeting stem cell dysfunction and Wnt/β-catenin pathway dysregulation may represent novel treatment strategies for this debilitating reproductive disorder.

title: Plasma proteins and persistent post-surgical pelvic pain among adolescents and young adults with endometriosis
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This study identified plasma protein biomarkers associated with persistent pelvic pain following surgical treatment of endometriosis in adolescents and young adults. Elevated pre-surgical levels of inflammatory markers CD63, CD47, and macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) were independently associated with worsening or persistent pain one year post-surgery, while higher parathyroid hormone levels predicted improved outcomes. Conversely, lower pre-surgical Sonic Hedgehog (SHH) protein levels were protective, though paradoxically, increases in SHH from pre- to post-surgery correlated with a fourfold increase in post-surgical pain risk, potentially through activation of nociceptor sensitization pathways. Pathway analysis revealed that patients with persistent pain demonstrated upregulation of cell migration and leukopoiesis pathways alongside downregulation of angiogenesis pathways, suggesting systemic immune dysfunction contributes to pain chronicity. These findings indicate that inflammatory and immune dysregulation detected in pre-surgical blood may identify endometriosis patients at high risk for treatment failure and chronic pain, offering potential targets for personalized intervention strategies.

title: Association between the composite dietary antioxidant index and risk of endometriosis in women: a national population-based study
summary: This population-based study demonstrates that higher composite dietary antioxidant index (CDAI) scores are inversely associated with endometriosis risk, with particularly strong protective effects observed in women aged 30-39 years. Women with endometriosis showed significantly lower intake of key antioxidants including vitamins E, A, and C, beta-carotene, and selenium, suggesting that deficiency in these micronutrients may increase susceptibility to the disease. The protective mechanisms appear to involve multiple pathways: vitamin E reduces reactive oxygen species and inflammatory markers through anti-angiogenic effects, vitamin C inhibits endometrial cell adhesion, and beta-carotene enhances natural killer cell function and prevents lipid peroxidation. Additionally, antioxidant-rich dietary components including fruits, vegetables, fish oil, and omega-3 fatty acids were associated with symptom reduction, while dietary factors such as trans-unsaturated fatty acids, red meat, and alcohol consumption were associated with increased endometriosis risk. The findings highlight trace element involvement in endometriosis pathogenesis, particularly through regulation of matrix metalloproteinases (MMP) by zinc and selenium. Overall, this work supports the therapeutic potential of antioxidant-rich dietary interventions as a preventive and adjunctive approach for endometriosis management.

title: How telomere maintenance affects endometriosis development: a preliminary study
summary:
# Summary

This preliminary study identifies telomere maintenance as a novel contributor to endometriosis development, with implications for understanding both disease pathogenesis and therapeutic intervention. Women with endometriosis exhibit significantly longer telomeres and elevated telomerase activity in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial tissue compared to normal endometria, which may facilitate the survival and proliferation of ectopic endometrial cells through retrograde menstruation. Key telomere maintenance genes including FOS, FOSB, SRC, MAPK3, and others were differentially expressed in endometriotic tissue and were enriched in sex hormone-related pathways, linking telomere biology to the hormone-dependent mechanisms underlying endometriosis. The study identified a diagnostic gene signature comprising EXO1, FOS, KDM4A, SMARCC1, and SULT1C2, and demonstrated that telomerase inhibition by BIBR1532 suppresses endometrial cell proliferation, migration, and invasion, suggesting telomerase inhibitors as potential therapeutic agents. Together, these findings reveal that aberrant telomere maintenance represents a critical molecular mechanism connecting endometrial cell immortalization with the clinical symptoms of dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility characteristic of endometriosis.

title: Endometriosis and depression: only a psychological effect or even a causal occurrence?
summary:
# Endometriosis and Depression: Inflammatory and Vascular Mechanisms

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological disorder affecting reproductive-age women that causes chronic pelvic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected pathophysiological mechanisms. The disease is characterized by peritoneal inflammation driven by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines—particularly IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α—produced by infiltrating macrophages and dysfunctional endometrial stromal cells in ectopic lesions. Aberrant angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis, mediated by VEGF, occur alongside progesterone resistance and impaired endometrial receptivity, which together compromise both endometrial function and embryo implantation. Additional contributing factors include estrogen-dependent disease progression, elevated oxidative stress, and reduced natural killer cell numbers that normally suppress aberrant endometrial growth. Current therapeutic approaches target these mechanisms through GnRH agonists, hormonal contraceptives, aromatase inhibitors to reduce local estrogen production, and surgical resection of ectopic lesions. Understanding these inflammatory and vascular mechanisms provides insight into endometriosis pathogenesis and guides development of more targeted therapeutic interventions.

title: D-chiro-inositol effectively counteracts endometriosis in a mouse model
summary: D-chiro-inositol (DCI) effectively treats endometriosis in a mouse model by reducing endometriotic lesion number and size through multiple molecular mechanisms. The compound downregulates Sirt1 expression and upregulates E-Cadherin, thereby inhibiting epithelial-mesenchymal transition, while simultaneously suppressing cell proliferation and blood vessel formation—key pathological features of ectopic endometrial growth. In ovarian tissue, DCI downregulates aromatase (Cyp19a1), the enzyme responsible for estrogen biosynthesis, and increases primordial to antral follicles, suggesting beneficial effects on fertility. Unlike current hormonal treatments that cause adverse effects such as hot flashes and mood disorders, DCI operates as a natural molecule that lacks these complications while addressing both lesion pathology and ovarian dysfunction. These findings suggest DCI may represent a promising alternative or adjunctive therapeutic approach for endometriosis management with improved tolerability.

title: Molecular Profile (Estrogen Receptor, Progesterone Receptor, Bcl-2 and Ki-67) of the Ectopic Endometrium in Patients with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease characterized by altered expression of steroid receptors and dysregulated apoptotic mechanisms in ectopic endometrial tissue. The ectopic endometrium exhibits elevated estrogen receptor-beta (ER2) levels (~140× higher) and reduced estrogen receptor-alpha (ER1) and progesterone receptor (PR) expression, leading to progesterone resistance that promotes aberrant cell proliferation and reduced endometrial receptivity. This steroid hormone imbalance is driven by increased aromatase and 17β-HSD1 activity combined with decreased 17β-HSD2 levels in endometriotic lesions, creating a local hyperestrogenic microenvironment. Elevated Bcl-2 and Ki-67 antigen expression in both epithelial and stromal compartments reflect enhanced cell survival and proliferation that correlate with disease aggressiveness, clinical pain symptoms (particularly dyspareunia), and lesion progression. The condition manifests clinically as dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, with combined oral contraceptives offering therapeutic benefit through reduction of cell proliferation and Bcl-2 expression while stimulating apoptosis.

title: The Effects of Endometriosis on Oocyte and Embryo Quality
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic gynecological disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue, causes significant clinical sequelae including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms affecting oocyte and embryo quality. At the cellular level, endometriosis induces dysregulated energy metabolism, oxidative stress, and mitochondrial dysfunction within the ovarian microenvironment, with metabolomic analyses revealing decreased glucose and increased lactate and pyruvate concentrations in follicular fluid of affected patients. The disease impairs cumulus cell function through dysregulations in molecular pathways involving TNF signaling, NF-κB signaling, and inflammatory responses, while also compromising steroidogenesis and altering the hormonal milieu necessary for optimal follicular development. Oocytes from endometriosis patients exhibit morphological abnormalities including cytoplasmic granularity, vacuoles, zona pellucida defects, and reduced mitochondrial content in metaphase II oocytes, with oocyte quality progressively worsening with increasing disease stage. Oxidative stress and inflammation in the follicular environment promote oxidative damage and impaired mitochondrial function, while peritoneal fluid from endometriosis patients contains elevated reactive oxygen species that reduce sperm motility, viability, and zona pellucida binding capacity. Collectively, these pathological changes lead to reduced follicular pools, impaired oocyte maturation, poor embryo development, and compromised reproductive outcomes in endometriosis patients.

title: Premature ovarian insufficiency and the risk of breast cancer
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized as a chronic inflammatory disease driven by dysregulated signaling pathways in ectopic endometrial tissue. Elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α are produced by endometrial stromal cells in endometriotic lesions, where lipopolysaccharide stimulation activates the TLR4-NF-κB pathway to amplify this inflammatory response. Histone deacetylase (HDAC) inhibitors emerge as a promising therapeutic strategy, suppressing pro-inflammatory cytokine production and NF-κB activation through increased histone acetylation, which restricts NF-κB DNA binding and transcriptional activity at inflammatory gene promoters. Beyond inflammation, NF-κB activation also upregulates anti-apoptotic proteins and angiogenic factors like VEGF that promote lesion persistence and vascularization, both of which may be suppressed by HDAC inhibitor treatment. These findings suggest that targeting histone deacetylation represents a multi-targeted approach to counter the inflammatory, pro-survival, and pro-angiogenic mechanisms underlying endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Bidirectional Two-Sample Mendelian Randomization Study Reveals Causal Associations Between Aging and Endometriosis
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies aging as a causal contributor to endometriosis risk, with leukocyte telomere length (LTL) serving as a key molecular link. Longer leukocyte telomeres were significantly associated with increased endometriosis risk overall, with particularly strong associations for ovarian and rectovaginal endometriosis subtypes, suggesting that extended telomeres may create a cellular environment permissive for disease development. The study proposes that aging-related cellular senescence and immune dysregulation impair clearance of ectopic endometrial cells while promoting chronic inflammation, alongside hormonally mediated mechanisms including decreased ovarian hormone levels. Multiple immune cell populations including macrophages, natural killer cells, dendritic cells, neutrophils, and T and B cells contribute to vascularization and fibrogenesis of endometriotic lesions. Notably, bidirectional analysis found no reverse causation from endometriosis to LTL, and conventional aging biomarkers showed no significant association with endometriosis risk. The findings suggest that lifestyle interventions preserving telomere length through regular physical activity, healthy diet, and stress reduction may offer preventive strategies for endometriosis.

title: Circular RNAs in endometriosis analyzed through RNA sequencing and bioinformatics for expression profile
summary:
# Summary

This RNA sequencing study identified dysregulated circular RNA (circRNA) expression profiles in ovarian endometriosis, with 371 circRNAs upregulated and 308 downregulated in affected tissues. CircRNAs function as competing endogenous RNAs (ceRNAs) that sequester microRNAs, thereby regulating miRNA availability and controlling downstream gene expression through circRNA-miRNA-mRNA regulatory networks. Key dysregulated circRNAs include hsa_circ_000005 (upregulated) and hsa_circ_000011 (downregulated), which interact with multiple miRNAs including miR-5787 and contribute to endometriosis pathogenesis through altered epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition and β-catenin signaling. Bioinformatic analysis revealed that differentially expressed circRNAs associate with cytoskeletal components, microtubule binding, muscle attachment, and cAMP-mediated signaling, potentially facilitating aberrant endometrial cell migration and tissue invasion. Additionally, circRNA dysregulation appears linked to metabolic and endocrine disruptions, involving vitamin B6 metabolism and parathyroid hormone signaling pathways. These findings establish circRNAs as biomarkers and potential therapeutic targets for endometriosis diagnosis and treatment.

title: Prospective evaluation of plasma proteins in relation to surgical endometriosis diagnosis in the Nurses’ Health Study II
summary: This prospective study identified a distinct plasma proteomic signature associated with endometriosis risk using aptamer-based proteomics on blood samples collected years before surgical diagnosis. Five proteins showed particularly strong associations with endometriosis: S100A9, TOP1, ICAM2, HIST1H3A, and CD5L were all significantly elevated in cases, while IGFBP1 was reduced. Pathway analyses revealed upregulation of innate immune response and multiple immune-related pathways in endometriosis cases, supporting the hypothesis that systemic immune dysregulation contributes to disease development and pathogenesis. The associations persisted even in samples collected more than two years before diagnosis, suggesting these circulating biomarkers may reflect underlying biological processes preceding clinical manifestation. NEGR1 and NRXN3 were notably reduced in endometriosis cases, indicating complex proteomic alterations affecting both inflammatory and neuronal pathways. These findings establish plasma proteomics as a promising approach for identifying non-invasive biomarkers of endometriosis risk and provide biological insight into the inflammatory mechanisms driving this chronic gynecological condition.

title: Prediction of genomic biomarkers for endometriosis using the transcriptomic dataset
summary: This study identified genomic biomarkers for endometriosis using machine learning analysis of transcriptomic data from endometrial tissue. Endometriosis, characterized by the presence of endometrial glands outside the uterine cavity, causes severe dysmenorrhea, pelvic discomfort, and decreased fertility, with inheritance occurring through polygenic and multifactorial mechanisms. MicroRNAs play a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by regulating cell survival, matrix remodeling, and angiogenesis, while circulating serum miRNAs combined with traditional biomarkers like CA125 enhance diagnostic accuracy. Machine learning models including Bagged CART, AdaBoost, XGBoost, and Stochastic Gradient Boosting successfully identified 21 key predictor genes using Elastic Net regularization, with TEC (100% importance), CUX2 (78.5%), and CLMP (60%) demonstrating the highest predictive value. Four genes—IL1R1-AS1, CCDC81, CD200, and ESYT3—were significantly elevated in endometriosis, while CRABP2, AKAP8L, and MROH5 were reduced, alongside other associated genes including CEP131, EHD4, CDH24, ILRUN, LINC01709, HOTAIR, SLC30A2, and NKG7. These transcriptomic biomarkers represent novel diagnostic and therapeutic targets that could improve early detection of endometriosis beyond the current gold standard of laparoscopic visualization.

title: The relationship between 1,25(OH)2D3 levels and interleukin-32 and vascular endothelial growth factor levels in endometriosis cyst tissue: An original article
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by aberrant expression of inflammatory mediators and excessive angiogenesis, with vitamin D deficiency playing a central pathogenic role. This study demonstrated that active vitamin D metabolite 1,25(OH)2D3 is significantly reduced in endometriosis cyst tissue and exhibits strong negative correlations with the proinflammatory cytokine interleukin-32 (IL-32) and the angiogenic factor vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF). IL-32 and VEGF themselves are elevated in endometriotic lesions and positively correlated, whereby IL-32 drives inflammation and angiogenesis by activating NF-κB and p38 signaling pathways, inducing production of multiple inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8) and stimulating VEGF-mediated vessel formation. 1,25(OH)2D3 exerts protective anti-inflammatory and anti-angiogenic effects through multiple mechanisms including direct NF-κB inhibition via IKKβ binding, suppression of VEGF expression, and cell differentiation inhibition. These findings suggest that vitamin D supplementation may represent a therapeutic strategy to reduce elevated IL-32 and VEGF levels, thereby mitigating the chronic inflammation and pathological angiogenesis that sustain endometriotic lesion growth and associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility.

title: Exploring the link between serum uric acid and endometriosis: a cross-sectional analysis utilizing NHANES data from 1999-2006
summary: This cross-sectional study of NHANES data (1999-2006) identified a significant positive association between serum uric acid levels and endometriosis risk, with women in the highest uric acid quartile demonstrating a 133% increased risk compared to the lowest quartile. The mechanism underlying this association appears to involve uric acid-driven inflammatory and oxidative pathways, as elevated uric acid promotes the formation of monosodium urate crystals that activate the NLRP3 inflammasome, triggering conversion of pro-interleukin-1β to mature IL-1β and subsequent activation of NF-κB, PI3K, and Akt signaling cascades. Patients with endometriosis display significantly elevated levels of multiple pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, interleukin-8, tumor necrosis factor-alpha, and the chemokines CCL2 and CCL5, alongside increased vascular endothelial growth factor, all of which promote oxidative stress and abnormal angiogenesis. The dose-dependent relationship between uric acid and endometriosis risk suggests that elevated serum uric acid may perpetuate the chronic inflammatory milieu characteristic of endometriosis and contribute to its associated symptoms of pain, menstrual abnormalities, and infertility. These findings establish serum uric acid as a potential biomarker for endometriosis and implicate uric acid-mediated inflammation as a novel therapeutic target in this debilitating reproductive condition.

title: Plasma metabolites associated with endometriosis in adolescents and young adults
summary: Adolescents and young adults with endometriosis display dysregulated plasma metabolomic profiles characterized by elevated inflammatory and oxidative stress markers. Specifically, endometriosis cases show significantly higher levels of fatty acyls, ceramides, and eicosatrienoic acid—metabolites associated with proinflammatory responses—alongside elevated xanthine indicating increased oxidative stress. Conversely, steroid derivatives and apoptosis-related metabolites such as glycocholic acid are downregulated in endometriosis, suggesting impaired steroid metabolism and deficient programmed cell death pathways. Notably, surgical treatment of endometriosis reverses many metabolic abnormalities, with fatty acyls and steroid derivatives normalizing post-operatively, although ceramides remain persistently elevated even after surgery compared to controls. These findings identify 63 endometriosis-associated plasma metabolites that could serve as potential biomarkers, while highlighting dysregulation of lipid metabolism, inflammation, and apoptosis as key pathophysiological mechanisms underlying endometriosis in this young population.

title: Examining the interplay between endometriosis and later-life cerebro-cardiovascular diseases: A systematic review, meta-analysis, and trial sequential analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates that endometriosis significantly increases the risk of major adverse cerebro-cardiovascular events, with women with endometriosis experiencing 24% higher odds of combined cardiovascular and cerebrovascular complications, 49% elevated stroke risk, 53% increased myocardial infarction risk, and notable increases in heart failure and arrhythmias. The mechanism underlying these associations involves endometriosis-induced chronic systemic inflammation and oxidative stress triggered by retrograde menstruation and ectopic endometrial tissue, which collectively alter lipid metabolism, promote atherosclerosis, and damage the vascular endothelium. At the cellular level, oxidative stress from reactive oxygen species impairs cardiac ion channels and electrophysiology, creating arrhythmogenic substrates and increasing susceptibility to cardiac dysfunction. Additional cardiovascular risk factors associated with endometriosis include hypercholesterolemia and hypertension, while surgical management strategies such as hysterectomy and postmenopausal hormone therapy may further contribute to atherosclerosis development. These findings establish endometriosis as a systemic inflammatory condition with substantial long-term cardiovascular and cerebrovascular consequences, highlighting the need for cardiovascular monitoring and risk stratification in affected women.

title: Endometriosis: Challenges in Clinical Molecular Diagnostics and Treatment
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation in pelvic organs including the ovaries, fallopian tubes, and extragonadal sites such as the liver, lungs, and intestines. The condition manifests through multiple debilitating symptoms including severe pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility, with significant psychological burden including depression and anxiety. Multiple etiological mechanisms contribute to endometriosis development, including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, embryonic rests from Müllerian ducts, and immune system dysfunction characterized by failure to eliminate misplaced endometrial tissue. At the molecular level, endometriosis involves dysregulation of key genes including HOXA10 (reduced expression), estrogen receptor beta (elevated expression), and COX-2 (elevated expression), alongside numerous genetic variants identified through genome-wide association studies in genes such as SRP14, HOXB9, IFNG, WNT4, and TP53. The pathophysiology is driven by aberrant inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms, with elevated chemokines and cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, IL-1α, IL-1β, CCL-2) promoting macrophage polarization and subsequent secretion of pro-angiogenic factors including VEGF, semaphorin 3A, and nerve growth factor. MicroRNAs including miR-210 (angiogenesis), miR-21 (tissue remodeling), and miR-20a/miR-148a (hypoxic injury) further regulate endometriotic lesion establishment and progression.

title: Long COVID and endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis reveals a significant association between endometriosis and long COVID, with women with endometriosis showing elevated risk of long COVID development. Both conditions share common pathogenic mechanisms centered on chronic inflammation, autoimmunity, and blood clotting disorders, suggesting overlapping inflammatory pathways. Endometriosis is characterized by dysregulated microRNAs (miR-21-5p, miR-145, miR-200c) that promote invasive endometrial stromal cell behavior and epithelial-mesenchymal transition through dysregulation of cell proliferation, apoptosis, angiogenesis, and inflammatory responses. A proposed mechanistic link involves SARS-CoV-2 interaction with ACE2 receptors expressed in female reproductive tissues, which disrupts the renin-angiotensin system and shifts the balance toward increased angiotensin II signaling, promoting inflammation, fibrosis, oxidative stress, and thrombosis. Anti-ACE2 antibodies that develop in long COVID patients may further impair ACE2 function and vascular integrity. Both endometriosis and long COVID feature chronic estrogen-driven inflammation marked by elevated IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α, and reactive oxygen species, providing a molecular basis for their observed clinical association.

title: MCT-modified ketogenic diet as an adjunct to standard treatment regimen could alleviate clinical symptoms in women with endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent condition characterized by ectopic tissue implantation that causes pelvic pain, dyspareunia, dyschezia, and infertility through multiple pathogenic mechanisms including estrogen signaling activation, systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, abnormal angiogenesis, enhanced cell division, and impaired apoptosis, with the Wnt/β-catenin signaling pathway playing a central role in disease initiation and progression. An MCT-modified ketogenic diet, which accelerates ketogenesis, counters these pathogenic mechanisms by suppressing inflammation and oxidative stress, inhibiting angiogenesis and cell division, promoting apoptosis, and downregulating the Wnt/β-catenin pathway. In clinical application, the MCT-modified ketogenic diet as an adjunct to standard endometriosis treatments significantly reduced dyspareunia and dyschezia symptoms compared to controls, with a marginally significant reduction in pelvic pain and improved hepatic function markers. This dietary intervention demonstrates therapeutic potential by directly addressing the inflammatory and angiogenic underpinnings of endometriosis while providing symptomatic relief alongside conventional pharmacological approaches such as combined oral contraceptives and progestogens.

title: Is JAK Inhibitor A Therapeutic Option For Endometriosis?
journal: Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology of India
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a prevalent gynecological disorder characterized by chronic inflammation and associated with infertility through multiple mechanisms including distorted pelvic anatomy, adhesions, impaired oocyte quality, and immune dysregulation. The JAK-STAT signaling pathway, particularly the JAK2/STAT3 axis, plays a central role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting endometrial growth and anti-apoptosis, while also regulating immune cell function and inflammatory cytokines like interleukin-6 that impair implantation. JAK inhibitors, specifically tofacitinib, have demonstrated therapeutic potential in preclinical models by reducing endometrial lesions and adhesive burden in surgically induced endometriosis. Clinical evidence is emerging from a case report where a woman with both rheumatoid arthritis and endometriosis-related infertility achieved pregnancy following tofacitinib treatment, suggesting that JAK inhibition may provide a dual benefit by treating inflammation-driven autoimmune disease while simultaneously addressing endometriosis. The shared inflammatory and genetic mechanisms between endometriosis and rheumatoid arthritis, including dysregulation of the JAK pathway and common susceptibility genes such as PTPN22 and HLA alleles, further support JAK inhibition as a promising therapeutic strategy for endometriosis-associated infertility.

title: Correction
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, causing pelvic pain and infertility through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. The disease involves aberrant activation of the NF-κB pathway in endometrial stromal cells, which drives elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-8) and promotes both local inflammation and angiogenesis supporting lesion growth. Dysregulated estrogen signaling amplifies this inflammatory cascade, as estrogen activates NF-κB through estrogen receptor α while simultaneously promoting aromatase expression to increase local estrogen production, coupled with reduced expression of progesterone receptors that normally exert anti-inflammatory effects. Elevated COX-2 and prostaglandin production further contribute to pain and inflammation, while the heightened pro-inflammatory environment recruits macrophages and promotes lesion vascularization through IL-6 and TNF-α-mediated angiogenesis. Current treatments including GnRH agonists and progestins suppress estrogen production, while emerging therapeutic approaches targeting NF-κB inhibition, aromatase inhibitors, and selective estrogen receptor modulators offer potential to interrupt the estrogen-inflammation cycle and reduce cytokine-driven pathology.

title: Bidirectional associations between endometriosis and Sjögren's syndrome in the era of multi‐omics
summary:
# Article Summary

This multi-omics study reveals significant bidirectional associations between endometriosis and Sjögren's syndrome, suggesting shared immunological mechanisms underlying both conditions. Dendritic cell maturation and hepatic fibrosis signaling pathways are notably enriched in both diseases, indicating common pathogenic pathways. Key proteins including B-cell activating factor (BAFF), HLA-DQA1, and HLA-DRA are upregulated in both conditions, alongside immunomodulatory factors such as IL-1 receptor antagonist and CTLA-4, highlighting shared adaptive immune dysregulation. The study also identifies connections between endometriosis and malignancies including endometrial cancer and uterine sarcoma, with CTLA-4 and IL1-Ra pathways implicated in endometrial cancer pathogenesis. Environmental factors like air pollutants may exacerbate Sjögren's syndrome through IL-6 and NF-κB signaling upregulation. The authors emphasize that advanced techniques such as single-cell RNA sequencing and spatial transcriptomics are essential for providing detailed insights into the cellular and tissue-level mechanisms of these conditions.

title: The inconsistent pathogenesis of endometriosis and adenomyosis: insights from endometrial metabolome and microbiome
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals distinct yet overlapping metabolomic and microbiomic signatures in endometriosis and adenomyosis, two interrelated gynecological disorders that cause chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. Both conditions exhibit dysbiosis involving alterations in the Proteobacteria phylum and dysregulation of critical metabolic pathways, particularly linoleic acid and glycerophospholipid metabolism, where the phosphatidylcholine species PC(40:8) emerged as a key shared metabolite. Specific bacterial taxa including Chryseobacterium kwangjuense, Bacillus cereus, and Enterobacter cloacae correlate with elevated PC(40:8) levels and activate phospholipase A2, linking the microbiota to lipid dysmetabolism in both conditions. While adenomyosis shows distinctive associations with immune-related pathways (toll-like receptor signaling and immunoglobulin A production) and metabolites like N-acetylaspartylglutamylglutamate, endometriosis is characterized by elevated indole and activation of cell adhesion and signaling pathways (PI3K-Akt, MAPK, and extracellular matrix interactions). These findings demonstrate that although endometriosis and adenomyosis share common dysbiotic and metabolomic features driving inflammation, disease-specific pathogenic mechanisms exist that may require differentiated therapeutic approaches.

title: Endometriosis is not the endometrium: Reviewing the over-representation of eutopic endometrium in endometriosis research
summary:
# Summary

This critical review challenges the prevailing assumption that endometriosis lesions are simply ectopic endometrium, revealing instead that they represent fundamentally distinct tissue with unique cellular and molecular characteristics. Endometriotic lesions are highly heterogeneous, enriched in stromal cells, endothelial cells, and diverse immune populations (monocytes, macrophages, and mast cells), with notably lower epithelial cell content compared to eutopic endometrium. The ectopic tissue exhibits pronounced inflammatory activation, fibrosis, and enhanced cell proliferation alongside increased reactive oxygen species and hypoxia that drive angiogenesis and metabolic reprogramming. Endometriotic cells display elevated adherence, migration, and invasion capabilities coupled with altered apoptotic responses, instead undergoing pyroptosis and ferroptosis upon cell death, distinguishing them from normal endometrial cells. Endometriosis is characterized by orchestrated immune dysfunction, including elevated memory B cells, CD4+ and CD8+ T cells, but reduced regulatory T cells and natural killer cells, creating a systemic proinflammatory milieu with elevated Th2 cytokine responses. Despite these distinctions, emerging diagnostic biomarkers—including cytokines (IL-1A, IL-6, RANTES), microRNAs (miR-210-3p), and nerve fiber markers—identified in eutopic endometrium suggest this abnormal tissue may serve as a clinically accessible indicator of endometriosis.

title: N6-methyladenosine methylation regulators can serve as potential biomarkers for endometriosis related infertility
summary:
# Summary

N6-methyladenosine (m6A) methylation and its regulatory proteins represent potential biomarkers for endometriosis-related infertility, with seven key regulators—IGFBP3, FTO, HNRNPA2B1, LRPPRC, HNRNPC, YTHDF2, and IGF2BP1—showing diagnostic value (AUC >0.9) and strong associations with immune cell infiltration patterns. HNRNPA2B1 and HNRNPC are significantly elevated in endometrial immune cells from infertile endometriosis patients but remain higher in the eutopic endometrium of fertile women, suggesting dysregulated m6A-mediated immune regulation. The m6A writer METTL3 drives endometriosis progression by promoting M2 macrophage polarization and suppressing microRNA maturation to enhance cell migration and invasion, while METTL3 loss impairs oocyte maturation through suppression of steroid hormone synthesis genes. Immune dysbalance, characterized by insufficient regulatory T cells and altered macrophage and dendritic cell populations, combines with hormonal imbalance—including estrogen dominance and progesterone resistance—to create an aberrant inflammatory endometrial state that reduces receptivity and fertility. In ovarian endometriosis, cyclic bleeding from ectopic lesions generates iron overload, triggering ferroptosis in granulosa cells and leading to oocyte immaturity, which synergizes with m6A regulatory dysfunction to compromise reproductive outcomes.

title: Non-Surgical Options for The Diagnosis of Endometriosis in Low-Resource Settings: A Comparative Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent condition characterized by the implantation of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, manifesting primarily through dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, with dysmenorrhea onset more than three years after menarche serving as a significant diagnostic indicator. The pathophysiology involves dysregulated retrograde menstruation combined with a compromised immune system, wherein elevated macrophages, dysfunctional natural killer cells, altered T cell responses, and increased pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) create an inflammatory microenvironment that facilitates lesion establishment and pain sensitization. Estrogen drives disease progression through linear effects on angiogenic factors including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and insulin-like growth factor-1 (IGF-1), while dysregulated apoptotic pathways and matrix metalloproteinase activity further support ectopic endometrial persistence. Clinical presentation encompasses menorrhagia, shortened menstrual cycles, and cyclic gastrointestinal symptoms (bloating, diarrhea, constipation), with advanced deep infiltrating endometriosis causing dysuria and dyschezia. Diagnostic approaches in low-resource settings include transvaginal ultrasound and emerging biomarkers such as CA-125, microRNAs, and inflammatory cytokines, while therapeutic options range from ovulation-suppressing agents (contraceptive steroids, progestogens, leuprolide acetate, dienogest) and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs to surgical lesion excision and in vitro fertilization for infertility management.

title: Alternative healthy eating index may predict a reduced odd of endometriosis: results from a case-control study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study demonstrates that adherence to the Alternative Healthy Eating Index (AHEI) substantially reduces endometriosis risk, with high adherence associated with approximately 92% lower odds of disease. The protective effect is driven by increased consumption of fruits, vegetables, and legumes, each independently reducing endometriosis odds by 85%, 95%, and 74% respectively. Conversely, high intake of red and processed meats, trans fatty acids, and sodium significantly increases endometriosis risk, with trans fatty acids showing a 12-fold increase in odds. The study attributes these dietary effects to modulation of inflammatory pathways, as specific nutrients regulate elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and RANTES) characteristic of endometriosis, while also influencing menstrual cycle regulation, estrogen activity, and prostaglandin metabolism. These findings highlight dietary intervention as a potentially effective preventive strategy for this estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition that affects reproductive-age women and is associated with infertility and pelvic pain.

title: The association between shorter disease course and sarcopenia in women with endometriosis: a retrospective analysis of NHANES 1999–2006
summary:
# Summary

This retrospective analysis of NHANES data (1999–2006) reveals an unexpected association between shorter endometriosis disease course (diagnosis within five years) and increased sarcopenia risk in women, with an adjusted odds ratio of 4.83. The study identifies overlapping pathogenic mechanisms between endometriosis and sarcopenia, primarily involving chronic low-grade inflammation, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1, IL-6), and oxidative stress that collectively inhibit muscle protein synthesis and promote muscle loss. Additionally, endometriosis-induced chronic pelvic pain contributes to reduced physical activity, leading to muscle weakness and atrophy. Demographic factors significantly modify this association, with racial minorities and individuals with higher educational levels showing disproportionately greater sarcopenia risk, while the protective effect of lower educational levels suggests potential differences in diagnostic accessibility or disease awareness across socioeconomic strata. The shorter disease duration association likely reflects either delayed diagnosis masking earlier disease onset or rapid disease progression in recently diagnosed cases, both indicating more aggressive disease phenotypes. These findings suggest that endometriosis management strategies should incorporate assessment for sarcopenia and physical rehabilitation to mitigate metabolic and musculoskeletal complications.

title: Endometriosis and Adenomyosis: From Pathogenesis to Follow-Up
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are chronic inflammatory diseases driven by estrogen dysregulation and progesterone resistance, with pathogenesis involving multiple interconnected mechanisms. Local hyperestrogenism results from increased aromatase activity and estrogen receptor expression, creating a positive feedback loop where estrogen production induces cyclooxygenase-2, which stimulates prostaglandin E2 and further aromatase activity. Retrograde menstruation delivers endometrial cells and stem/progenitor cells to the peritoneum, where genetic predisposition, somatic mutations, and epigenetic modifications may enhance their survival and implantation; ectopic basal endometrial debris subsequently triggers chronic inflammation via TNF-α and IL-1β signaling, activating macrophages to secrete growth factors and cytokines that promote ectopic tissue proliferation. Fibrosis emerges as a key pathogenic driver, contributing to disease progression and clinical manifestations including adhesions and pelvic pain, while progesterone resistance develops through downregulation of progesterone receptors and pro-inflammatory cytokine signaling. Early diagnosis and medical hormonal therapy are essential for managing these conditions, as such treatment reduces symptoms, improves quality of life, and slows disease progression despite the complex molecular alterations underlying pathogenesis.

title: Causal association of cathepsins and endometriosis: A Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study establishes cathepsin H as a causal risk factor for endometriosis, with genetically predicted elevated cathepsin H levels significantly increasing the risk of overall endometriosis and its specific subtypes including ovarian endometriosis, peritoneal endometriosis, and deep infiltrating disease. Cathepsin H promotes endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple mechanisms: it degrades key extracellular matrix components (laminin and type IV collagen) to facilitate ectopic endometrial tissue invasion, is upregulated by estrogen, and promotes cellular proliferation and migration. Beyond cathepsin H, the study identifies a broader role for cathepsins in endometriosis, including cathepsin B's amplification of TNF-α/IL-1β inflammatory signaling and cathepsin S's activation of PAR-2 in neurogenic inflammation, while also demonstrating that cathepsins suppress immune surveillance by impairing natural killer cell and macrophage function. Notably, a bidirectional relationship exists whereby endometriosis can also increase cathepsin H levels, and progesterone resistance promotes cathepsin L upregulation, which subsequently degrades progesterone receptors in a vicious cycle. These findings suggest cathepsin inhibitors, such as cathepsin B inhibitor CA-074 already showing efficacy in primate models, represent promising therapeutic targets for managing endometriosis and its associated symptoms.

title: Association of COX-2 Selectivity in Pain Medication Use with Endometriosis Incidence: Retrospective Cohort Study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting 5–15% of reproductive-age women and characterized by dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, pelvic pain, and infertility. The disease involves elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines including interleukin-1β and tumor necrosis factor-α, which activate nuclear factor κB and hypoxia-inducible factor-1α signaling pathways, leading to increased COX-2 expression in endometrial tissue and ectopic lesions. COX-2 drives prostaglandin E2 synthesis, which promotes local estrogen production via aromatase upregulation, creating a self-sustaining inflammatory loop that contributes to lesion growth and pain. This retrospective cohort study found that selective COX-2 inhibitors (celecoxib, nimesulide, meloxicam, etodolac, and diclofenac) were significantly more effective at reducing endometriosis incidence and related pain than traditional non-selective NSAIDs over a five-year follow-up period. By selectively inhibiting COX-2, these medications suppress prostaglandin E2 and downstream aromatase activity, thereby reducing both inflammation and local estrogen synthesis. These findings suggest that COX-2-selective inhibitors may offer therapeutic advantages over conventional NSAIDs for managing endometriosis symptoms and potentially preventing disease development.

title: UDP-Glucose Ceramide Glucosyltransferase Inhibition, Immune Cell Mediation, and Endometriosis Risk: A Mendelian Randomization Study
summary:
# Summary

This Mendelian randomization study identifies UDP-glucose ceramide glucosyltransferase (UGCG) as a novel therapeutic target for endometriosis, demonstrating that UGCG inhibition reduces endometriosis risk with an odds ratio of 0.915. The mechanism involves complex immune cell-mediated pathways, with 12 of 731 analyzed immune cell types significantly mediating the protective effect of UGCG inhibition, particularly terminally differentiated CD4+ T cells, which accounted for the largest proportion (26.7%) of this mediation. The study reveals that endometriosis involves dysregulated immune surveillance characterized by reduced CD8+ T cell cytotoxicity, altered macrophage profiles, and impaired natural killer cell function, which UGCG inhibition appears to counteract. Additionally, UGCG inhibition exerts complex immunomodulatory effects, acting as a protective factor for 84 immune cell types while serving as a risk factor for 72 others, suggesting dual and context-dependent immune regulatory mechanisms. The findings suggest that existing FDA-approved UGCG inhibitors, such as eliglustat and migalastat, may offer repurposing opportunities as potential endometriosis therapeutics, though the intricate immune cell-mediated pathways warrant further investigation to optimize clinical application.

title: Commentary: The causal role of gastroesophageal reflux disease in endometriosis: a bidirectional Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study provides evidence for a causal relationship between gastroesophageal reflux disease (GERD) and endometriosis, with the association appearing unidirectional—GERD increases endometriosis risk, but not vice versa. The proposed mechanism involves multiple pathways: GERD-induced acid reflux promotes chronic systemic inflammation characterized by elevated IL-6, TNF-α, and IL-1β, which activate pelvic macrophages and enhance endometrial adhesion and angiogenesis. Additionally, acid reflux causes gut microbiota dysbiosis leading to immune dysregulation that disrupts the gut-endometrial axis, while chronic reflux activates the vagus nerve to trigger neurogenic inflammation in the uterine microenvironment. The estrogen-regulated inflammatory cascade of endometriosis subsequently manifests in clinical symptoms including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and psychological distress, with potential neuroendocrine mediation through the hypothalamic-pituitary-adrenal axis and vagus nerve signaling linking the two conditions. These findings suggest that GERD management may represent a novel therapeutic target for preventing or modulating endometriosis development.

title: The influence of genetics on the endocannabinoid system gene expression and relevance for targeting reproductive conditions
summary: This study investigates how genetic variants regulate endocannabinoid system (ECS) gene expression in endometrial tissue and its relevance to reproductive conditions including endometriosis. The ECS, composed of endocannabinoids like anandamide and 2-arachdonoylglycerol, controls critical cellular processes including proliferation, migration, survival, and inflammation, making it a promising therapeutic target for reproductive disorders characterized by chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. The analysis identified 49 of 70 ECS genes significantly affected by genetic variants in endometrial tissue, with particularly strong genetic regulation of catabolizing enzymes (FAAH, MGLL, NAAA, ABHD12) that degrade endocannabinoids and thereby influence their bioactive duration. Notably, the female reproductive system showed substantially fewer genetic expression quantitative trait loci (eQTLs) for ECS genes compared to other tissues like sun-exposed skin, with the uterus having only seven genes with eQTLs, suggesting limited genetic regulation of the ECS in reproductive tissues. These findings indicate that genetic variation modulates endocannabinoid bioavailability through effects on degradation enzymes, which may explain individual differences in endocannabinoid-dependent physiological processes and could inform cannabinoid-based therapeutic strategies for endometriosis and related reproductive pathologies.

title: Prevalence of endometriosis in Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser syndrome variants: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This systematic review and meta-analysis examined the association between Mayer–Rokitansky–Küster–Hauser (MRKHS) syndrome and endometriosis, finding that endometriosis prevalence is substantially higher in MRKHS patients who retain functional endometrium in uterine remnants (32.0%) compared to those without functional endometrium (1.5%). The strong association supports the retrograde menstruation hypothesis of endometriosis pathogenesis, as the presence of menstrual blood and a functional endometrium—typically considered essential for disease development—strongly predicts lesion formation, particularly ovarian endometriomas that develop ipsilateral to the functional endometrium. The findings demonstrate that endometriosis can occur in patients with congenital uterine absence when functional endometrial tissue persists in embryonic remnants, thereby establishing the critical requirement for eutopic or remnant endometrium in disease pathogenesis. While alternative mechanisms such as coelomic metaplasia may contribute, the preferential association with functional endometrium indicates that retrograde menstruation through patent Müllerian structures remains the predominant mechanism driving endometriosis development in this unique patient population.

title: Contrast-Enhanced Ultrasound Findings of Peritoneal Polypoid Endometriosis: A Case Report and Literature Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition characterized by abnormal implantation of endometrial glands and stroma outside the uterine cavity, affecting 30–50% of patients with chronic pelvic pain and infertility. Polypoid endometriosis (PEM), a rare peritoneal variant, develops through multiple interconnected mechanisms including somatic mutations (KRAS and PIK3CA mutations in ~25% of cases), hormonal imbalance, neoangiogenesis, and immune evasion that alter the peritoneal microenvironment. Inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α drive microenvironment remodeling by activating the NF-κB pathway, which promotes fibroblast-to-myofibroblast transformation (α-SMA+) and ultimately polypoid structure formation, while HMGA1 and HMGA2 somatic alterations drive abnormal stromal proliferation. PEM presents diagnostic challenges because it frequently mimics malignant tumors with elevated CA125 levels and hypervascularity, achieving a misdiagnosis rate as high as 42%, though contrast-enhanced ultrasound (CEUS) can differentiate benign characteristics through dynamic contrast observation. Despite its aggressive appearance, PEM has a favorable prognosis with only 5.9% postoperative recurrence, and approximately 5–10% of cases may undergo malignant transformation to endometrioid adenocarcinoma or clear cell carcinoma.

title: Serum creatinine as a risk factor for endometriosis: insights from cross-sectional study, mendelian randomization analysis, and diagnostic model study
summary:
# Summary

This cross-sectional study with Mendelian randomization analysis identifies serum creatinine as an independent risk factor for endometriosis, with bidirectional causal relationships demonstrated through genetic analysis. Elevated creatinine levels, which reflect metabolic status and renal function, increased endometriosis risk by approximately 8% per 10 μmol/L increase, with particularly pronounced associations in non-Hispanic white populations. The mechanistic link between creatinine and endometriosis appears to involve chronic inflammation and metabolic dysregulation, as pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α and IL-6) are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients and promote ectopic endometrial tissue growth through enhanced cell proliferation and increased oxidative stress. Insulin resistance and metabolic syndrome, which elevate pro-inflammatory markers, further contribute to disease pathogenesis. A diagnostic model incorporating creatinine alongside age, weight, and BMI demonstrated good predictive ability, suggesting that serum creatinine could serve as a non-invasive biomarker for endometriosis screening and early diagnosis.

title: Endometriosis and Autoimmunity
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting one in ten women, characterized by the implantation and proliferation of ectopic endometrial cells in the peritoneal cavity that trigger both local and systemic inflammation and fibrosis. The disease is strongly associated with pelvic pain, infertility, and dysmenorrhea, and notably co-occurs with autoimmune conditions, suggesting that immune dysfunction is central to its pathogenesis. A proinflammatory and hypoxic peritoneal microenvironment promotes ectopic implantation, angiogenesis, and lesion growth, while retrograde menstruation may escape immune clearance due to reduced Th17 activity, fewer macrophages, and lower TGF-α levels in menstrual effluent from affected women. The eutopic endometrium in endometriosis demonstrates a dysregulated immune microenvironment characterized by elevated M1 macrophages, elevated Th17 cells, abnormal uNK and Treg activity, and a reduced Treg to Th17 ratio that collectively sustain chronic inflammation and impair reproductive function including oocyte quality and endometrial receptivity. The lesion microenvironment is marked by a hyper-estrogenic state, elevated reactive oxygen species, and increased expression of angiogenic factors like VEGF and NGF that promote cellular adhesion and proliferation, while dysregulated macrophage phagocytosis and diminished NK cell cytotoxicity further compromise immune surveillance. Notably, animal models lacking functional immune systems fail to develop endometrial lesions, confirming that chronic inflammatory responses are essential for disease establishment and progression.

title: Endometriosis: An Immunologist’s Perspective
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by a complex inflammatory microenvironment in which endometriotic lesions overproduce cytokines, metalloproteinases, prostaglandins, reactive oxygen species, and extracellular vesicles. Multiple immune cell populations—including macrophages, dendritic cells, mast cells, Th1 and Th2 lymphocytes, regulatory T cells, and NK cells—are dysregulated in the disease, with reduced NK cell function and impaired immune surveillance contributing to ectopic endometrial growth. Constitutive NF-κB activation within lesions drives inflammation, invasion, and angiogenesis, processes that underlie the clinical manifestations of chronic pelvic pain, menstrual pain, and infertility. Genetic and epigenetic alterations in genes such as ARID1A, PIK3CA, PTEN, ITGB3, and HOTAIR lncRNA promote endometrial stromal cell proliferation and migration, while specific cytokine polymorphisms (IL-1A, IL-10, IL-12B, IL-17A, TNF) are associated with disease susceptibility and severity. Additionally, endometriosis carries a significant risk for progression to epithelial ovarian carcinoma through reactive oxygen species imbalance and oncogenic mutations, highlighting the need for immunological perspectives in understanding both the pathophysiology and cancer-associated complications of this disease.

title: Detection of Cadherin 12 in Plasma and Peritoneal Fluid Among Women with Endometriosis Using Novel Surface Plasmon Resonance Imaging (SPRi) Method
summary:
# Summary

Cadherin 12 (CDH12), a calcium-dependent adhesion molecule, plays a significant role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting cell migration, invasion, adhesion, and angiogenesis, as well as epithelial-mesenchymal transition. Using a novel surface plasmon resonance imaging (SPRi) biosensor method, researchers detected CDH12 in both plasma and peritoneal fluid from women with endometriosis, finding that plasma concentrations were significantly higher than peritoneal fluid levels. Notably, peritoneal fluid CDH12 concentrations were lower in infertile women compared to fertile controls, suggesting a mechanistic link between CDH12 dysregulation and endometriosis-associated infertility. The study also identified a significant positive correlation between CDH12 concentration and patient age, indicating that age may be a modifying factor in adhesion molecule expression. These findings suggest CDH12 functions as an adjunctive factor rather than a primary driver in endometriosis pathogenesis and represents a potential biomarker for both disease detection and infertility assessment. Future investigations should examine CDH12 levels in additional biological fluids such as endometrial fluid and menstrual blood to refine diagnostic and prognostic applications.

title: Pelvic Pain Symptoms and Inflammation Among Adolescents and Adults with and Without Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue, affects approximately 10% of reproductive-aged individuals and is strongly associated with multiple pain phenotypes including dysmenorrhea (35–90%), acyclic pelvic pain, and dyspareunia. The condition involves profound immune dysregulation, including elevated peritoneal macrophages and neutrophils, reduced natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and recruitment of inflammatory molecules that directly trigger endometriosis-associated pain and central sensitization. Key inflammatory cytokines show complex relationships with pain symptoms: IL-1β is consistently elevated in peritoneal fluid of symptomatic women, while IL-8 acts as a neutrophil chemoattractant, and distinct interleukin profiles (elevated IL-6, IL-8, IL-12p70, IL-16; reduced IL-19, IL-22) correlate with specific pain presentations. Notably, inflammatory marker patterns differ between endometriosis cases and controls, and analgesic use modulates cytokine-pain associations, suggesting that medication effects must be considered when interpreting inflammatory signatures. These findings underscore that endometriosis-related pain is driven by multifactorial inflammatory mechanisms rather than a single pathway, with individual cytokine profiles potentially serving as biomarkers for specific pain phenotypes and treatment response.

title: CME Test
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex inflammatory disease characterized by altered immune responses, elevated macrophage infiltration into peritoneal fluid, and dysregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8, which collectively promote a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Local estrogen production is amplified in ectopic lesions through elevated aromatase expression and estrogen receptor signaling, while progesterone resistance—driven by reduced progesterone receptor expression and altered receptor isoform ratios—impairs the normal anti-inflammatory response and perpetuates disease progression. The pro-inflammatory environment and elevated growth factors like VEGF and nerve growth factor facilitate both angiogenesis and neuroangiogenesis, supporting lesion vascularization and contributing to pain pathology, while parallel mechanisms including epithelial-mesenchymal transition, oxidative stress, and aberrant microRNA expression enhance endometrial cell migration and survival. Additionally, TGF-β-driven fibroblast activation and excessive extracellular matrix deposition promote endometrial fibrosis and tissue remodeling. Current therapeutic approaches including NSAIDs and hormonal contraceptives address pain and disease progression, targeting the inflammatory and hormonal drivers of endometriosis pathophysiology.

title: Progressively diminished estrogen signaling concordant with increased fibrosis in ectopic endometrium
summary:
# Summary

This study reveals a paradoxical pattern in ectopic endometrium where estrogen biosynthesis capacity and fibrosis show an inverse relationship. While ovarian and adenomyotic endometriosis exhibit elevated expression of estrogen biosynthetic enzymes (StAR, aromatase, HSD3β2, HSD17β1) and their product 17β-estradiol, deep endometriosis demonstrates comparable biosynthetic capacity but dramatically reduced estrogen receptor signaling, particularly through ERβ and GPER, with suppressed ERα expression. Notably, increased lesional fibrosis correlates inversely with both estrogen biosynthesis and estrogen receptor expression across all ectopic lesion types, with collagen I accumulation particularly pronounced in deep endometriotic lesions. Prostaglandin E2 signaling appears to drive estrogen biosynthesis and ERβ upregulation in ovarian and adenomyotic lesions, yet this enhanced estrogenic microenvironment does not prevent the progressive fibrosis that characterizes deep endometriosis. These findings suggest that the transition from estrogen-responsive to fibrotic ectopic lesions involves a fundamental shift in estrogen signaling capacity rather than absolute estrogen production, with important implications for understanding phenotypic heterogeneity and treatment resistance in endometriosis.

title: Unraveling the interrelationship between breast cancer and endometriosis based on multi-omics analysis
summary:
# Summary

This multi-omics study reveals significant molecular overlap between endometriosis and breast cancer, both conditions characterized by estrogen dependence and progressive growth patterns. Multi-omics analysis identified 47 common genes and 11 core biomarkers (including OLFM4, APOBEC3B, EZH2, and FOS) associated with both diseases, which are enriched in cell cycle regulation, chromosome segregation, and oxidative stress response pathways. A prognostic model incorporating three key genes (SHCBP1, PMAIP1, and LTF) stratified breast cancer patients into risk groups with distinct immune profiles: low-risk patients showed enhanced infiltration of protective immune cells (eosinophils, NK cells, mast cells), while high-risk patients displayed elevated activated T cells and regulatory T cells. These shared molecular mechanisms and biomarkers suggest that endometriosis-associated estrogen signaling and inflammatory pathways may contribute to breast cancer development, potentially explaining the epidemiological link between these conditions and providing therapeutic targets.

title: Integrative bioinformatics analysis of lipid metabolism-related genes and immune infiltration in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This integrative bioinformatics study identified 58 dysregulated lipid metabolism-related genes (LMRGs) in endometriosis patients, with eleven core genes including HMGCR, CYP27A1, PPARG, and HSD17B1 emerging as potential diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets. Abnormal immune infiltration, particularly elevated fibroblasts and B lineage cells, was significantly associated with endometriosis, with fibroblasts linked to ten core LMRGs and involved in fibrosis-mediated pain and infertility. Key lipid metabolic pathways dysregulated in endometriosis include steroid hormone metabolism, arachidonic acid metabolism (contributing to chronic inflammation and dysmenorrhea via prostaglandin synthesis), and cholesterol metabolism. The study identified promising therapeutic strategies: statins inhibit HMGCR to reduce lesion size and postoperative pain, while PPARG agonists attenuate inflammation, invasion, and angiogenesis in endometrial lesions. Additionally, dysregulated estrogen biosynthesis through HSD17B1-mediated estradiol conversion and fibroblast-associated progesterone resistance contribute to the pathophysiology of infertility and reduced endometrial receptivity in endometriosis.

title: Differences in the proteomic profiles of the eutopic endometrium in patients with internal and external adenomyosis
summary: This proteomic study reveals distinct inflammatory and immunological profiles differentiating internal from external adenomyosis in the eutopic endometrium. External adenomyosis is characterized by heightened innate immune activation, with significantly more differentially expressed proteins that activate immune pathways including complement cascade, neutrophil degranulation, and Fc gamma receptor-dependent phagocytosis, driven by upstream regulators such as lipopolysaccharide, TGF-β1, IL-4, and interferon-gamma. In contrast, internal adenomyosis associates with altered signaling related to cell migration and apoptosis, with activation of MAPK1 and IRF2BP2 pathways. Both adenomyosis subtypes involve immune dysregulation and chronic inflammation within the eutopic endometrium, leading to the clinical manifestations of abnormal uterine bleeding, dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility. The proteomic evidence suggests neutrophils play a central role in external adenomyosis pathology by promoting angiogenesis and modulating immune responses, establishing differential mechanistic pathways that may guide subtype-specific therapeutic interventions.

title: Endometriosis and autoimmunity: a large-scale case-control study of endometriosis and 10 distinct autoimmune diseases
summary: This large-scale case-control study demonstrates a striking association between endometriosis and multiple autoimmune diseases, including rheumatoid arthritis, systemic lupus erythematosus, Hashimoto's disease, multiple sclerosis, Sjögren's syndrome, and others. The underlying mechanism involves systemic chronic inflammation driven by ectopic endometrial lesions, which trigger elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1, interleukin-6, and interleukin-8) and promote local inflammation through increased peritoneal neutrophils and macrophages. Endometriosis is characterized by dysregulation of immune cells including suppressed natural killer cells, reduced myeloid dendritic cells, and defective T cell and regulatory cell functioning, which parallels immune dysfunction patterns seen in autoimmune disease pathogenesis. These shared inflammatory pathways and immune cell defects suggest that the systemic chronic inflammation associated with endometriosis may increase susceptibility to developing various autoimmune conditions. The findings support the conceptualization of endometriosis as a systemic inflammatory disease with mechanistic overlap to established autoimmune conditions, potentially explaining the elevated co-occurrence of endometriosis with multiple distinct autoimmune disorders.

title: Evaluation of inflammatory serum parameters as a diagnostic tool in patients with endometriosis: a case-control study
summary: This case-control study identifies serum inflammatory biomarkers as potential diagnostic tools for endometriosis, with interleukin-6 (IL-6) and soluble urokinase-type plasminogen activator receptor (suPar) emerging as the most significant markers. Both IL-6 and suPar were significantly elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls and demonstrated high diagnostic importance in machine learning models, suggesting their value as pro-inflammatory serum biomarkers. IL-6 is produced by macrophages, which are abundant in endometriomas and peritoneal lesions, and correlates with endometriosis-associated pain intensity and infertility, with some studies showing stage-dependent increases in advanced disease. Hepcidin, involved in iron homeostasis, was also elevated in endometriosis patients, particularly in the upper quartile distribution. While IL-6 and suPar showed stronger associations with endometriosis diagnosis than established markers like CA-125, the inflammatory markers did not significantly correlate with endometriosis stage (rASRM), indicating they reflect overall disease presence rather than severity. These findings support the underlying immune-inflammatory mechanisms driving endometriosis and propose IL-6 and suPar as potentially superior diagnostic serum parameters compared to conventional clinical markers.

title: Prolactin and pain of endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth in the ovaries and peritoneal surfaces, leading to chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Prolactin, elevated in endometriosis patients, plays a central role in disease pathology by sensitizing nociceptive neurons, promoting pain sensitivity, and modulating angiogenesis and immunoregulation; dysregulation of prolactin and its receptors contributes to increased pain severity through activation of the peripheral and central nervous systems. The disease involves a complex proinflammatory microenvironment where ectopic lesions secrete cytokines and neurotrophic factors that promote their own proliferation, vascularization, and the sprouting of nerve fibers—elevated nerve fiber density directly correlating with severe pain symptoms. Estrogen receptor beta is overexpressed in ectopic tissue, promoting endometrial cell proliferation by inhibiting apoptosis and enhancing adhesion, while progesterone's anti-proliferative effects are compromised in endometriotic lesions. Adipose tissue inflammation amplifies prolactin release and contributes to systemic inflammation, while central sensitization and altered brain function extend endometriosis from a localized pelvic disease to a systemic condition associated with mood and anxiety disorders. Dopamine D2 receptor agonists offer a potential therapeutic avenue by suppressing prolactin secretion and its pain-promoting effects.

title: Endometriosis and Cardiovascular Disease: Exploring Pathophysiological Interconnections and Risk Mechanisms
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is increasingly recognized as a systemic disease strongly associated with cardiovascular disease, including atherosclerosis, coronary heart disease, and ischemic heart disease, with women with endometriosis showing approximately 40% higher cardiovascular risk. This pathophysiological interconnection is driven by shared inflammatory mechanisms, including elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β) secreted by infiltrating macrophages and dysregulated T lymphocytes, combined with enhanced oxidative stress that generates reactive oxygen species and impairs antioxidant enzyme activity. Endometriosis directly causes endothelial dysfunction and arterial stiffness, measurable through reduced Reactive Hyperemia Index and elevated Augmentation Index, which contribute to thrombosis and atherosclerotic progression. Additionally, endometriosis is associated with metabolic dysregulation including dyslipidemia, hypertension, and metabolic syndrome, amplified by excess estrogen that elevates LDL and impairs endothelial health through altered nitric oxide signaling. Shared genetic loci, notably CDKN2B-AS1, link endometriosis and cardiovascular disease susceptibility, suggesting common underlying genetic vulnerability. Emerging pharmacologic options including statins, metformin, and antiplatelet agents show promise as dual-benefit treatments targeting both endometriosis and cardiovascular risk reduction.

title: Global Trends and Emerging Frontiers on Ovarian Endometriosis: A Bibliometric and Visualization Analysis
summary:
# Ovarian Endometriosis: Clinical Impact, Inflammatory Mechanisms, and Emerging Therapeutics

Ovarian endometriosis is a prevalent condition causing dysmenorrhea and infertility in reproductive-age women, with affected individuals facing elevated risks for ovarian cancer and potentially other malignancies. The disease involves complex inflammatory mechanisms including iron overload-induced GSDME-mediated pyroptosis triggering IL-16 activation, which drives endometriosis-associated chronic inflammation through ectopic endometrial epithelial cells that activate T cells via HLA class II signaling and chemokine secretion. Key pathological features include excessive fibrosis driven by recurrent tissue injury-repair cycles mediated by galectin-3, as well as abnormal persistence of estrogen and progesterone receptors, particularly ER-alpha, in both glandular and stromal compartments. Ovarian endometriomas produce regulatory proteins including inhibin A and activin A that modulate local cell growth and differentiation, while serum biomarkers such as CA-125, CA-199, and miR-200 show association with disease presence. Treatment approaches have evolved from traditional progesterone therapy to include GnRH agonists, aromatase inhibitors, and laparoscopic minimally invasive surgery, with emerging therapeutic candidates like Z30702029 targeting GSDME-mediated pyroptosis showing promise in preclinical studies. This bibliometric analysis underscores angiogenesis, atypical endometriosis, and receptor signaling as emerging research frontiers, with China now leading global publication output in this field.

title: Endometriosis and ovulatory menstruation: beyond the Sampson principle
summary:
# Summary: Endometriosis and Ovulatory Menstruation

Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory disease caused by ovulatory menstruation and retrograde menstruation, which establishes ectopic endometrial implants that cause dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility. The ectopic endometrial stromal cells undergo epigenetic alterations that increase aromatase expression and estrogen receptor-β while reducing progesterone receptor expression, leading to local estrogen excess and progesterone resistance that drives further inflammation and tissue remodeling with fibrosis. Key molecular defects include reduced expression of HSD17B2 (which normally catabolizes estradiol) and glycodelin in endometriotic tissue, along with enrichment of cancer-driver mutations such as KRAS and PIK3CA in endometriotic lesions. Clinically, suppression of ovulation through oral contraceptives prevents endometriosis development and alleviates pain, while in vitro fertilization can improve outcomes without worsening disease, whereas intrauterine insemination exacerbates symptoms. Beyond traditional Sampson theory, this work demonstrates that ovulatory cycles themselves are essential for endometriosis pathogenesis, explaining why menstruating species and anovulatory disorders like polycystic ovary syndrome show distinct epidemiological patterns with endometriosis.

title: Association between anti-Mullerian hormone levels and age in women with endometriosis: insights from a population-based study
summary: This population-based study demonstrates that women with endometriosis have significantly lower anti-Mullerian hormone (AMH) levels compared to healthy controls, reflecting impaired ovarian reserve driven by multiple mechanisms. The pathophysiology involves retrograde menstruation, immune dysregulation, hormonal imbalance, and coelomic metaplasia, with endometriosis causing estrogen-dependent inflammation and pelvic scarring that reduces ovarian function. Ovarian endometriomas directly damage follicles through reactive oxygen species, free iron, and proteolytic enzymes, reducing follicular density and increasing atresia while causing cumulative tissue fibrosis. A critical finding is that healthy women show a steeper AMH decline before age 27 years, but women with endometriosis lack this age-related breakpoint pattern, suggesting endometriosis supersedes normal age-related ovarian aging trajectories. These impaired ovarian reserve mechanisms contribute to the condition's hallmark symptoms of dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. The study provides molecular and clinical insights into how endometriosis compromises reproductive capacity through mechanisms beyond typical age-dependent ovarian senescence.

title: The comparison of hematological inflammatory parameters before and after surgery in women with endometriosis: retrospective cohort study
summary: This retrospective cohort study examined hematological inflammatory markers as biomarkers for endometriosis by comparing preoperative and postoperative values in women undergoing surgery. Endometriosis is fundamentally characterized by chronic inflammation driven by cytokines such as TNF-α, and the study found that platelet-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), neutrophil-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), and monocyte-lymphocyte ratio (MLR) were all elevated preoperatively in the endometriosis group compared to controls with benign ovarian cysts. Notably, postoperative values of these inflammatory markers paradoxically increased across both groups following surgical intervention, while CA-125 levels—a well-established endometriosis marker—decreased significantly after surgery in the endometriosis group, maintaining diagnostic effectiveness during follow-up. The researchers identified cutoff values of RDW > 13.5, PLR > 123.4, and CA-125 > 35 as predictive thresholds for endometriosis diagnosis. Stage 3–4 endometriosis patients exhibited the highest inflammatory marker elevations and were associated with nulliparity, bilateral ovarian cysts, and prior endometriosis surgeries. Surgical excision effectively reduced the chronic inflammatory stimulus by removing ectopic endometrial tissue, supporting the use of surgery combined with medical treatments such as oral progesterone, oral contraceptive pills, or gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists in comprehensive endometriosis management.

title: EZH2 promotes endometriosis progression through estrogen receptor and TNFα expression
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates a novel epigenetic mechanism driving endometriosis progression centered on EZH2, a histone methyltransferase that catalyzes H3K27me3 modifications.
