EZH2 is significantly elevated in endometriotic lesions where it suppresses estrogen receptor α (ERα) expression through H3K27me3-mediated chromatin condensation at the ERα promoter, preventing transcription factor Sp1 binding. This reduction in ERα paradoxically allows estrogen receptor β (ERβ) to dominate signaling, leading to enhanced TNFα expression and driving a pro-inflammatory cascade that promotes oxidative stress, cell proliferation, and lesion growth. The EZH2 inhibitor GSK343 effectively reverses this pathology in mouse models by restoring ERα expression, suppressing TNFα and other inflammatory cytokines, and reducing lesion size and proliferation markers. These findings identify EZH2-mediated epigenetic reprogramming as a key driver of endometriosis and suggest that targeting EZH2 represents a promising therapeutic approach for managing this chronic inflammatory condition.

title: Beyond pelvic pathology: retinal microvascular rarefaction as a systemic marker in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis is associated with retinal microvascular rarefaction detected by optical coherence tomography angiography (OCTA), revealing a systemic vascular phenotype beyond pelvic pathology. Women with endometriosis exhibited significantly reduced vessel area density and reduced FD-300 values in both superficial and deep retinal vascular complexes compared to healthy controls, with a paradoxical finding of decreased capillary density despite endometriosis being a hyperangiogenic disease characterized by elevated VEGF expression. The chronic inflammatory milieu of endometriosis—marked by elevated cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), oxidative stress, and reduced nitric oxide bioavailability—drives endothelial dysfunction and progressive microvascular rarefaction that extends systemically. These retinal microvascular alterations correlate with the increased prevalence of systemic vascular disorders in endometriosis patients, including cardiovascular events, hypertension, stroke, and other microvascular syndromes such as migraine and Raynaud's phenomenon. The findings support endometriosis as a systemic disease characterized by widespread endothelial dysfunction, and OCTA-derived metrics may serve as non-invasive biomarkers of microvascular integrity in this patient population.

title: Effect of melatonin for the management of endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is an estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder associated with chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and fatigue, characterized by dysregulation of inflammatory cytokines and cell adhesion molecules including Bcl-2, Fas, ICAM-1, TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-8, as well as impaired natural killer cell function. This study evaluates melatonin as a potential therapeutic agent for managing endometriosis, building on multiple reports suggesting melatonin's efficacy in disease management. The research focuses on measuring endometrial biomarkers and inflammatory markers through immunohistochemistry and serum analysis to determine melatonin's effects on the molecular drivers of endometriosis pathogenesis. By targeting the inflammatory mechanisms underlying this condition, melatonin may offer a novel treatment approach for reducing symptoms and improving outcomes in affected women. The findings are expected to provide updated clinical evidence supporting melatonin's role in endometriosis management and advancing therapeutic options beyond conventional estrogen-focused treatments.

title: Effects of Endometriosis on Anti-Müllerian Hormone
summary: Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent chronic inflammatory condition, causes significant clinical symptoms including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility while compromising ovarian reserve through multiple pathological mechanisms. The disease triggers elevated estrogen levels that promote proliferation of endometrial stromal cells and stimulate pro-inflammatory factors, which increase transforming growth factor beta-1 and contribute to fibrotic tissue formation. Granulosa cells within endometriotic cysts experience oxidative stress, inflammation, and apoptosis that disrupts normal follicle development and reduces oocyte recovery, collectively impairing ovarian reserve through inflammatory, fibrotic, apoptotic, and oxidative stresses. Paradoxically, women with endometriosis aged 20–31 years exhibit elevated anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) levels compared to healthy controls, though AMH decreases with age and is significantly lower in women with endometriotic ovarian cysts, suggesting that younger endometriotic patients may have compensatory hormone production despite reduced follicle quality. AMH, produced by granulosa cells of small antral follicles and widely used as a biomarker for ovarian reserve assessment, becomes particularly important given the high recurrence rate of endometriosis and the dual challenge that surgical treatment—while necessary for symptom relief—further reduces ovarian reserve. These findings underscore the complex relationship between endometriosis-induced inflammatory pathology and reproductive hormone markers, indicating that chronological age, ovarian location of disease, and surgical history significantly influence AMH levels and fertility outcomes in affected women.

title: Endothelial Protein Changes Indicative of Endometriosis in Unexplained Infertility, an Exploratory Study
summary:
# Summary

This exploratory study identified a distinct endothelial protein signature in unexplained infertility that parallels endometriosis-associated changes. Women with unexplained infertility showed significantly decreased VCAM-1 and VEGF levels alongside elevated sICAM-3, angiopoietin-1, and decreased sTie-2 compared to controls with male factor infertility. The reduced VCAM-1 indicates endothelial stress and impaired endothelial responsiveness, while decreased VEGF suggests compromised endometrial angiogenesis, both critical for embryo implantation and pregnancy success. Elevated sICAM-3 reflects heightened immunological activity through enhanced leukocyte adhesion, consistent with the inflammatory milieu observed in endometriosis, suggesting that undiagnosed subclinical endometriosis may underlie a significant proportion of unexplained infertility cases. These protein changes correlated with reproductive hormonal parameters, supporting their relevance to fertility status. Notably, vitamin D levels did not differ between groups, indicating that the endothelial dysfunction in unexplained infertility operates through mechanisms independent of vitamin D status.

title: Deficiency of N-acetyl-β-d-glucosaminidase in neutrophils of cancer patients
journal: Journal of Cancer Research and Clinical Oncology
summary: N-acetyl-β-D-glucosaminidase (NAG), an enzyme present in neutrophils, shows significant alterations in enzymatic activity across various malignancies and benign uterine conditions. The enzyme activity is substantially reduced in neutrophils from patients with uterine cancer, breast cancer, lung cancer, and uterine myoma, with a more modest reduction observed in stomach carcinoma and laryngeal precancerous states. Notably, patients with endometriosis demonstrated elevated NAG activity in their neutrophils, contrasting with the enzymatic deficiency seen in cancer patients. Since neutrophils exert cytotoxic effects on tumor cells, the reduced NAG activity in cancer patients may reflect an impaired neutrophil function that compromises anti-tumor immunity. This enzyme deficiency pattern suggests that NAG activity in neutrophils could serve as a potential biomarker for cancer development and progression, with endometriosis showing an inverse relationship. The elevated enzyme activity in endometriosis may indicate heightened neutrophil-mediated inflammatory responses characteristic of this benign gynecological condition.

title: Causal links and mediating effects of lipid metabolism, immune cells, and inflammatory proteins in endometriosis: Evidence from Mendelian randomization
summary:
# Summary

Using Mendelian randomization analysis, this study establishes causal links between lipid metabolism, immune dysregulation, and endometriosis pathogenesis. Abnormalities in multiple lipid species—including phosphatidylcholines, phosphatidylethanolamines, sphingomyelins, triacylglycerols, and diacylglycerols—directly associate with endometriosis risk, with lipid metabolites mediating their effects through immune and inflammatory pathways. Key inflammatory mediators including elevated IL-17A, TNF-α, and IL-6 promote disease progression, while protective factors such as TNF-related apoptosis-inducing ligand and C–C motif chemokine 4 exhibit defensive effects. The metabolic dysregulation involves upregulation of regulatory genes (PDK1, HIF1A, SMS1) that disrupt cell adhesion, immune responses, and microenvironmental balance, with specific lipid species including triacylglycerol (52:3) demonstrating mediation effects on immune regulation via IL-17A signaling at 17.59% effect size. Additionally, immune cell abnormalities—characterized by macrophage dysfunction, imbalanced T cell subsets, and reduced natural killer cell activity—synergize with lipid-driven metabolic imbalance to perpetuate ectopic endometrial lesion formation and the associated clinical manifestations of dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility.

title: Menstrual reform: cultural roots of delayed endometriosis diagnosis in the Asia–Pacific region
journal: The Lancet Regional Health: Western Pacific
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth in the peritoneum and ovaries, causing dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, and infertility. The pathogenesis involves multiple interconnected mechanisms including immune dysregulation marked by elevated macrophages and reduced natural killer cells in peritoneal fluid, heightened inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α), and activation of the NF-κB signaling pathway. Angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis are critical processes supporting ectopic tissue establishment, driven by upregulated IL-8 and vascular endothelial growth factor, while epithelial-mesenchymal transition facilitates endometrial cell invasion and dissemination. Estrogen dysregulation via upregulated aromatase and progesterone resistance further perpetuate disease progression through elevated oxidative stress and reactive oxygen species in the peritoneal microenvironment. Current therapeutic approaches target these mechanisms through GnRH agonists, progestins, surgical excision, and nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs for symptom management, though the article emphasizes that cultural factors in the Asia-Pacific region significantly delay diagnosis and treatment initiation.

title: Leptin receptor polymorphism increases the risk of painful symptoms in Brazilian women with endometriosis
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: This Brazilian study demonstrates that a leptin receptor polymorphism (LEPR rs1137100) significantly increases susceptibility to endometriosis-related pain symptoms. The LEPR rs1137100 variant, located in exon 4, was associated with a 1.75-fold increased risk of chronic pelvic pain and 1.78-fold increased risk of dyspareunia in women with endometriosis. Leptin and its receptor are substantially elevated in the peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients, where leptin activates signaling pathways that promote proliferation, inflammation, and angiogenesis—processes that contribute to disease pathology. The inflammatory cascade in endometriosis involves elevated pro-inflammatory mediators including IL-6, IL-8, and prostaglandin E2, which activate visceral and peritoneal nerve fibers and heighten pain sensitivity. The findings suggest that the LEPR rs1137100 G allele may alter leptin receptor conformation, enhancing leptin binding and amplifying pro-inflammatory and neoangiogenic responses that drive endometriosis-associated pain.

title: Association of Bioavailable 25(OH)D Levels With Endometriosis in Infertile Bangladeshi Women: A Cross‐Sectional Study
summary: This cross-sectional study demonstrates a significant association between bioavailable vitamin D (BVD) deficiency and endometriosis in infertile Bangladeshi women, with 98% of endometriosis patients exhibiting moderate to severe BVD deficiency compared to 30% in infertile women without endometriosis. The findings support vitamin D's critical role in modulating the inflammatory and immune dysregulation characteristic of endometriosis, as BVD exerts immunomodulatory, anti-inflammatory, and antiproliferative effects through vitamin D receptors present in reproductive tissues including the ovary, endometrium, and placenta. Vitamin D deficiency compromises these protective mechanisms, potentially exacerbating immune dysregulation, chronic inflammation, and tissue remodeling that drive endometrial lesion formation and impair ovarian function, implantation, and early pregnancy maintenance. The study also identified sociodemographic factors influencing BVD status, including increased deficiency with obesity, higher age, and lower socioeconomic status, suggesting that nutritional and lifestyle factors may modulate endometriosis risk. These findings suggest that correcting vitamin D deficiency through supplementation may represent an accessible therapeutic avenue to ameliorate inflammatory pathways and improve reproductive outcomes in women with endometriosis.

title: Impact of gut microbiota on endometriosis: linking physical injury to mental health
journal: Frontiers in Cellular and Infection Microbiology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder strongly linked to gut microbiota dysfunction, which contributes to disease through dysregulated inflammation, aberrant estrogen metabolism, and immune dysfunction. Retrograde menstruation introduces endometrial fragments that establish ectopic lesions; these lesions are driven by estradiol and characterized by elevated estrogen receptor beta expression, which suppresses estrogen receptor alpha and impairs progesterone signaling, perpetuating pathological estrogen-driven proliferation. Dysbiosis-induced immune imbalance promotes the recruitment and M2 polarization of macrophages while simultaneously suppressing natural killer cell cytotoxicity and expanding immunosuppressive regulatory T cells, creating a microenvironment that protects ectopic endometrial cells from immune clearance and facilitates lesion adhesion and growth. Multiple immune populations—including Th17 cells, B lymphocytes, and myeloid-derived suppressor cells—actively support endometriosis progression through chronic inflammation and angiogenesis, while elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines such as interleukin-6 further perpetuate the disease state. Beyond pelvic pathology, endometriosis associates with dysmenorrhea, persistent pelvic pain, infertility, and significant mental health sequelae including depression and fatigue, highlighting its systemic impact. These findings suggest that targeting gut microbiota restoration and immune rebalancing could represent novel therapeutic strategies for managing both the physical manifestations and psychological burden of endometriosis.

title: Complement and coagulation cascade cross-talk in endometriosis and the potential of Janus Kinase inhibitors—a network meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

This network meta-analysis reveals that endometriosis involves complex cross-talk between complement and coagulation cascades, with dysregulated immune and inflammatory pathways as central mechanisms. The ectopic endometrium (EEM) in endometriosis shows distinct transcriptomic signatures compared to eutopic endometrium (EH), characterized by elevated expression of complement components (C1QA, C3, C7), coagulation-related serpins (SERPINE1, SERPINE2), and mast cell markers (CPA3, KIT), alongside reduced expression of protective genes including BIRC3, CEL, and LEFTY1. The inflammatory environment is further promoted by upregulation of the chemokine CCL21 and the JAK/STAT pathway (increased STAT5A and STAT5B), which regulates cell migration and invasion. Pharmacogenomic analysis identified JAK inhibitors—particularly Ruxolitinib and Tofacitinib—as promising therapeutic candidates, with evidence demonstrating their capacity to reduce lesion size, suppress endometrial cell proliferation, and inhibit VEGF-mediated angiogenesis. The findings underscore complement C3 as a critical mediator and support targeting JAK/STAT signaling to modulate the dysregulated immune response underlying endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Royal College of Obstetricians & Gynaecologists (RCOG) World Congress
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by abnormal neovascularization and is strongly associated with infertility and pain. Estrogen plays a pivotal role in disease pathogenesis, with elevated aromatase in ectopic endometrial tissue driving local estrogen synthesis that sustains the inflammatory microenvironment. Key inflammatory mediators including IL-6 and TNF-α are significantly elevated in peritoneal fluid and endometrial tissue, where they promote both pain and inflammation. Angiogenic factors such as VEGF enhance vascular permeability and new vessel formation within lesions, further establishing the abnormal vascular environment characteristic of endometriosis. Neuroinflammation and increased nerve fiber innervation in endometrial lesions represent emerging mechanisms contributing to endometriosis-associated pain. Together, these findings highlight the interconnected roles of estrogen signaling, inflammatory cytokines, angiogenesis, and neuroinflammation in endometriosis pathogenesis and symptom generation.

title: Endometriosis and Adenomyosis Unveiled Through Single-cell Glasses
journal: American journal of obstetrics and gynecology
summary: Single-cell RNA sequencing has revealed that endometriosis and adenomyosis, closely related estrogen-driven disorders originating from the basalis endometrium, share fundamental cellular and molecular abnormalities despite differing anatomical locations. Both conditions involve inflammatory activation, pathological neoangiogenesis, and fibrosis mediated by fibroblasts and immune cells, alongside altered progesterone signaling in epithelial and stromal compartments. The eutopic endometrium of endometriosis patients displays reduced expression of immune modulators PAEP and CXCL14, decreased uterine natural killer cells, and defective decidualization in stromal fibroblasts, collectively impairing the receptive state required for successful pregnancy implantation. Adenomyosis lesions are characterized by abnormal epithelial-to-mesenchymal transitions, elevated estradiol and aromatase production, and a unique perivascular cell population supporting immune cell trafficking and neoangiogenesis in progestin-treated patients. Wnt signaling pathway activation in adenomyotic epithelium and inflammasome activation in both conditions represent emerging therapeutic targets. These shared pathogenic mechanisms explain why both diseases present with overlapping clinical manifestations including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, and highlight the need for cell-type-specific interventions to restore endometrial function.

title: Association Between Atherogenic Index of Plasma and Endometriosis: Evidence from NHANES 1999–2006
summary: This study demonstrates a significant association between elevated atherogenic index of plasma (AIP) and endometriosis risk, with women having endometriosis showing 1.76-fold higher odds in the highest AIP quartile compared to the lowest. The mechanism appears to involve dyslipidemia characteristic of endometriosis, including elevated triglycerides and low HDL-cholesterol, which promote formation of small, dense LDL particles susceptible to oxidation. Oxidized LDL subsequently activates endothelial cells and macrophages to release pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) while simultaneously generating reactive oxygen species that drive oxidative stress and enhance endometrial cell adhesion and invasion. Additionally, elevated phospholipids, sphingolipids, and phosphatidic acid in endometriosis further amplify inflammatory and oxidative processes. The findings suggest AIP serves as a useful biomarker for identifying endometriosis patients at heightened cardiovascular risk, as endometriosis itself is linked to coronary heart disease, stroke, and elevated hospitalization rates for cardiovascular events through mechanisms of inflammatory dysregulation, oxidative stress, and adverse lipid profiles.

title: Analysis of Vitamin D and VDR Expression in Women with Advanced Endometriosis: A Case–Control Study in Thailand
summary: This Thai case-control study investigated the role of vitamin D and vitamin D receptor (VDR) expression in advanced endometriosis, given vitamin D's known immunomodulatory and anti-inflammatory properties. While vitamin D deficiency has been epidemiologically linked to endometriosis and theoretically could contribute to disease through modulation of inflammatory cytokines such as IL-6 and IL-8, this study found no statistically significant difference in serum 25-hydroxyvitamin D levels between women with endometriosis and controls. Notably, VDR expression was significantly elevated in endometriotic epithelial cells compared to stromal cells, and endometriotic tissue showed higher VDR and 1α-hydroxylase expression than eutopic endometrium, suggesting local vitamin D signaling dysregulation despite circulating vitamin D levels. However, neither serum vitamin D status nor VDR expression demonstrated significant associations with endometriosis severity as measured by the revised American Society of Reproductive Medicine (rASRM) score. The findings suggest that while vitamin D metabolism may be altered locally within endometriotic lesions, circulating vitamin D levels and genetic polymorphisms in VDR and vitamin D-binding protein do not substantially predict endometriosis susceptibility or severity in this population.

title: Divergent Immune–Metabolic Profiles in Endometriosis and Ovarian Cancer: A Cross-Sectional Analysis
summary:
# Endometriosis and Ovarian Cancer: Divergent Immune-Metabolic Profiles

This study reveals fundamentally divergent immunometabolic environments between endometriosis and high-grade serous ovarian cancer (HGS-OC). Endometriosis is characterized by M2-polarized macrophages, elevated intracellular mTOR and AKT signaling, and a pro-oxidative, iron-rich peritoneal environment where free iron and reactive oxygen species (ROS) directly correlate with lesion persistence. In contrast, HGS-OC displays predominantly M1-polarized tumor-associated macrophages with elevated circulating levels of IL-6, hepcidin, and ferritin, reflecting a higher M1/M2 ratio that correlates with inflammatory markers and oxidative stress. The iron metabolism dysregulation differs markedly between conditions: endometriosis shows accumulated free iron and ROS that drive macrophage-mediated chronic inflammation, fibrosis, and immune tolerance, while HGS-OC demonstrates iron sequestration through elevated hepcidin. These contrasting immune phenotypes suggest that iron overload and macrophage plasticity sustain endometriosis lesion persistence, whereas the M1-dominant inflammatory profile in HGS-OC may enhance chemotherapeutic responsiveness and anti-tumorigenic immunity.

title: Disease Severity- and Hormonal Status-Dependent Alterations of EGF and MIF in the Serum of Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease caused by ectopic endometrial tissue engraftment, leading to dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, dyschezia, dysuria, and infertility. The study revealed disease severity- and hormonal cycle-dependent alterations in key inflammatory mediators: serum epidermal growth factor (EGF) was significantly lower in mild endometriosis and during the secretory phase, whereas macrophage migration inhibitory factor (MIF) and interleukin-6 (IL-6) were elevated across all endometriosis stages, with MIF additionally increased in the secretory phase and in patients not receiving hormonal therapy. The neuropeptide somatostatin was similarly reduced in endometriosis patients and showed strong positive correlations with dysmenorrhea and dysuria in mild disease, alongside EGF, suggesting these factors may modulate pain symptoms. Notably, vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) and other inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-8, TNF-α) showed no significant serum differences, and growth factors and cytokines were not elevated in severe endometriosis lesions, indicating that systemic biomarker changes may be more reflective of disease severity than tissue-level alterations. These findings identify EGF, MIF, IL-6, and somatostatin as potential disease stage-dependent biomarkers and therapeutic targets for managing endometriosis-associated pain.

title: Non-Hormonal Strategies in Endometriosis: Targets with Future Clinical Potential
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causes chronic pelvic pain, infertility, dyspareunia, menorrhagia, and gastrointestinal complications through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The disease develops via retrograde menstruation combined with peritoneal inflammation, with risk factors including early menarche, short menstrual cycles, nulliparity, low body mass index, and family history, while IL-6 signaling mediates genetic susceptibility. Estrogen drives lesion proliferation and inflammatory responses through dysregulated local production via elevated aromatase and steroidogenic acute regulatory protein, combined with reduced 17β-HSD2 activity, leading to estrogen receptor beta dominance that promotes pro-inflammatory gene expression while suppressing progesterone receptors, ultimately triggering prostaglandin E2 production that drives pain and angiogenesis. The peritoneal microenvironment exhibits persistent elevation of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), immune cell infiltration (neutrophils, macrophages), altered T cell populations (elevated Th2 and Th17 cells with reduced natural killer cells), and heightened chemokine activity, all contributing to lesion development and pain amplification. Current hormonal treatments including combined oral contraceptives, GnRH agonists, and progestins inhibit ovulation and reduce symptoms, while non-hormonal strategies encompass NSAIDs for pain management, laparoscopic surgery for lesion removal, hysterectomy for severe cases, and assisted reproductive technologies for fertility restoration, with emerging attention to endocrine-disrupting chemical exposure as a modifiable risk factor.

title: Correction to: WERF Endometriosis Phenome and Biobanking Harmonisation Project for Experimental Models in Endometriosis Research (EPHect-EM-Heterologous): heterologous rodent models
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder affecting reproductive-age women that causes pelvic pain and infertility through complex immunological and molecular mechanisms. The disease is characterized by elevated expression of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-alpha, produced by both endometrial stromal cells and infiltrating macrophages recruited via MCP-1 signaling, which activate adhesion molecules like ICAM-1 on epithelial cells. Local estrogen production is amplified through increased aromatase expression and a shift toward estrogen receptor alpha dominance, driving COX-2-mediated prostaglandin E2 synthesis that further enhances IL-6 production. Angiogenesis is promoted through HIF-1alpha-dependent VEGF upregulation in hypoxic lesions, while oxidative stress and constitutive NF-kB and MAPK pathway activation perpetuate the inflammatory microenvironment. Pain generation is mediated by elevated nerve fiber innervation driven by NGF and marked by increased substance P, CGRP, and P2X3 receptor-expressing sensory nerves, with mast cell-derived tryptase further sensitizing these pathways through PAR-2 activation, collectively explaining the persistent symptoms and reduced fertility associated with endometriosis.

title: Exploring the therapeutic potential of H1-antihistamines in endometriosis—A gene regulation-based perspective
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by immune dysregulation and chronic inflammation involving dysregulated pro-inflammatory pathways including NF-κB signaling, TNF-α, interleukins (IL-6, IL-1β, IL-8), COX-2, and chemokine ligands that drive lesion formation, neuroangiogenesis, and the clinical manifestations of chronic pelvic pain and infertility. Gene expression analysis in deep infiltrating endometriosis revealed marked upregulation of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 ~4×, IL-11 and IL-34 ~2×), their receptors, chemokines (CCL2, CCL13, CXCL12), adhesion molecules (VCAM1, ICAM1, SELE >4×), and neurotrophic factors (NGF ~3×), alongside dysregulation of the histamine synthesis and metabolism pathway with elevated tissue histamine levels. H1-antihistamines represent a promising therapeutic strategy given their potent anti-inflammatory and immunomodulatory properties, which function through histamine H1 receptor antagonism to suppress multiple pro-inflammatory cascades activated in endometriosis. This gene regulation-based perspective identifies H1-antihistamines as a potential novel treatment approach capable of addressing the underlying inflammatory and immune dysfunctions central to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Association between visceral adiposity index and endometriosis: a population-based study
summary:
# Summary

This population-based study demonstrates a statistically significant positive association between visceral adiposity index (VAI) and endometriosis risk, with women in the highest VAI quartile exhibiting a 1.78-fold increased disease risk compared to the lowest quartile. VAI, a composite metabolic index derived from waist circumference, BMI, triglycerides, and HDL cholesterol, effectively reflects visceral adiposity accumulation, abnormal lipid metabolism, insulin sensitivity, and inflammatory status—all implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. The mechanistic link involves ectopic endometrial tissue triggering localized peritoneal inflammation characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-8, IL-6, IL-33) and dyslipidemia-driven inflammatory responses, which in turn activate NF-κB signaling and promote visceral adipose tissue dysfunction and browning. Elevated triglyceride levels in endometriotic lesions and reduced hepatic lipid synthesis further exacerbate the inflammatory microenvironment. These findings highlight VAI as a novel, clinically applicable indicator for endometriosis risk assessment and suggest that visceral adiposity-driven metabolic and inflammatory dysfunction represents a key pathogenic mechanism linking obesity and metabolic dysregulation to endometriosis development.

title: C57BL/6J mice best recapitulate fibrosis and inflammatory pathophysiology in syngeneic mouse model of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by fibrosis, dysregulated cell proliferation, angiogenesis, and epithelial-mesenchymal transition (EMT), which collectively contribute to debilitating chronic pelvic pain and infertility. This study identified C57BL/6J mice as the optimal strain for modeling endometriosis, exhibiting the highest disease incidence (92.85%) alongside pronounced fibrotic pathology including elevated stromal collagen deposition, increased expression of fibrotic markers (Col1A1, α-SMA, Nestin), and EMT-associated molecular changes characterized by reduced E-cadherin and elevated N-cadherin and S100A4. C57BL/6J endometriotic lesions showed hallmark inflammatory features including high cell proliferation (Ki-67+), significant neovascularization (CD31+), and substantial macrophage infiltration (F4/80+), with a pro-fibrotic peritoneal environment dominated by M2 macrophages. ENDO-induced mice across all strains displayed behavioral and sensory phenotypes consistent with disease burden, including reduced exploratory behavior and burrowing activity, increased mechanical hyperalgesia, and anxiety-like behavior, alongside elevated circulating estrogen and strain-specific inflammatory cytokine alterations. The C57BL/6J model most closely recapitulates the inflammatory and fibrotic characteristics of human endometriosis, making it a valuable preclinical model for investigating disease mechanisms and therapeutic interventions.

title: Leveraging epigenetic aberrations in the pathogenesis of endometriosis: from DNA methylation to non-coding RNAs
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological disorder involving multiple epigenetic aberrations that drive its pathophysiology and contribute to associated infertility, which affects 25-50% of infertile patients. DNA methylation abnormalities, particularly hypomethylation of key regulatory genes, are central to disease progression; hypomethylation of NR5A1 and its encoded steroidogenic factor-1 (SF-1) leads to enhanced estrogen production in ectopic endometrial tissue, while increased DNA methyltransferase expression (DNMT1, DNMT3A, DNMT3B) further perpetuates these epigenetic changes. Additional hypomethylation events affecting GATA6 and COX-2 promoters amplify steroidogenic enzyme expression and prostaglandin E2 production, establishing a pro-endometriotic microenvironment that promotes cell proliferation and tissue invasion. Elevated estrogen signaling through hypomethylation-induced overexpression of estrogen receptor beta (ESR2) perpetuates a pathogenic feedback loop. The infertility associated with endometriosis results from multiple mechanisms including impaired folliculogenesis, oocyte dysfunction, ovulation disturbances, and defective implantation, all underpinned by these epigenetic alterations alongside immune and genetic dysfunction. These findings highlight epigenetic modifications, particularly DNA methylation patterns, as promising biomarkers for early endometriosis diagnosis and potential therapeutic targets.

title: H3K27me3-mediated epigenetic regulation of TET1 in the eutopic endometrium of women with endometriosis and infertility
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder associated with epigenetic dysregulation and infertility, involving dysregulated expression of the ten-eleven translocation (TET) genes in the eutopic endometrium. This study reveals that H3K27me3, a repressive histone mark catalyzed by the PRC2 complex enzyme EZH2, is aberrantly elevated in endometriotic tissues and represses TET1 expression, particularly at its promoter region. Unlike healthy women who show high TET1 expression during the secretory phase—supporting optimal DNA demethylation and endometrial receptivity—women with endometriosis display reduced TET1 activity due to elevated H3K27me3-mediated silencing. The opposing roles of H3K27me3 (repression) and TET1 (activation) in regulating genes essential for endometrial function and implantation receptivity suggest that this epigenetic imbalance contributes to endometriosis-related infertility. These findings implicate the EZH2–PRC2–H3K27me3 axis as a therapeutic target, supported by evidence that histone methyltransferase inhibition reduces endometriotic cell growth and invasiveness. The work highlights how epigenetic dysregulation of critical demethylation pathways underlies both the pathogenesis of endometriosis and its associated reproductive complications.

title: The effect of combined oral contraceptive pills on angiogenesis in endometriotic lesions
summary:
# Summary

Neoangiogenesis is critical for the establishment and progression of endometriotic lesions, requiring a rich vascular network to supply oxygen and nutrients to ectopic endometrial tissue. Although oral contraceptive pills are clinically used to suppress endometriosis symptoms, this study reveals a paradoxical finding: combined oral contraceptive pill administration significantly upregulates multiple key angiogenic factors including VEGF, tissue factor (TF), fibroblast growth factor-1 (FGF1), and the transcription factor Sp1 in endometriotic lesions. These angiogenic mediators operate through interconnected pathways, with estradiol and inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α) driving VEGF expression, while tissue factor interacts with PAR-2 to promote endothelial cell activation and vascular sprouting. The study demonstrates that despite clinical symptom improvement, oral contraceptives do not suppress the neoangiogenic microenvironment of endometriotic lesions; instead, they may paradoxically enhance angiogenic signaling, suggesting that alternative mechanisms account for symptomatic relief and highlighting the complexity of hormonal contraceptive effects on endometriosis pathology.

title: Can the MMP-9/NGAL ratio be a diagnostic biomarker for the diagnosis of endometrioma in infertile patients?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, particularly endometriomas, causes infertility through multiple mechanisms including ovulatory dysfunction, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pain, processes fundamentally driven by dysregulated immune responses and chronic inflammation characterized by infiltrating immune cells. The pathophysiology involves elevated matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP-9), which degrades the extracellular matrix and facilitates ectopic lesion invasion and adhesion, while neutrophil-derived NGAL (neutrophil gelatinase-associated lipocalin) reflects the inflammatory state but is paradoxically reduced in endometrioma patients. The MMP-9/NGAL ratio emerges as a promising diagnostic biomarker, with ratios greater than 1.75 demonstrating 86.1% sensitivity and 84% specificity for endometrioma detection, correlating strongly with disease presence, pain severity (VAS score), and disease severity while negatively correlating with ovarian reserve (AMH levels). Notably, surgical intervention significantly reduces both MMP-9 levels and the MMP-9/NGAL ratio postoperatively, providing utility for disease monitoring and treatment efficacy assessment. These findings suggest that the MMP-9/NGAL ratio surpasses conventional markers like CA-125 in diagnostic specificity by directly reflecting both the inflammatory milieu and extracellular matrix degradation characteristic of endometriotic lesions.

title: Membrane Progesterone Receptor Beta Regulates the Decidualization of Endometrial Stromal Cells in Women with Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies membrane progesterone receptor beta (mPRβ) as a critical regulator of endometrial decidualization that is significantly downregulated in endometriosis. Endometriosis causes multiple clinical symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility; the infertility is linked to impaired decidualization, a progesterone-dependent process essential for successful pregnancy establishment. The researchers demonstrated that mPRβ activation by the agonist OrgOD-2 induces expression of key decidualization markers including insulin-like growth factor binding protein 1, prolactin, and transcription factors HAND2 and FOXO1, whereas mPRβ knockdown with siRNA substantially reduces these markers by 60-99%. Notably, decidualization marker expression is significantly lower in endometriotic cells compared to healthy endometrial cells, and mPRβ expression itself is reduced in endometriotic tissues. These findings establish that diminished mPRβ expression contributes to the decidualization defects observed in endometriosis, suggesting that mPRβ agonists represent a potential therapeutic approach to restore endometrial function and improve fertility outcomes in affected women.

title: Role of Transcription Factor, LIM Homeobox 9 (LHX9) in Inflammatory Response by PGE2 and Thrombin in SERPINA1‐Silencing Endometrial Stromal Cells
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies a critical inflammatory pathway in endometriosis involving the anti-inflammatory protein SERPINA1 and multiple transcription factors. SERPINA1 expression is reduced in endometriotic lesions, and its knockdown in endometrial stromal cells upregulates toll-like receptors 3 and 4, leading to elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines IL6, CXCL8, and IL1B. Pro-inflammatory mediators present in peritoneal hemorrhage—specifically prostaglandin E2 and thrombin—further amplify this inflammatory response by activating these cytokine pathways. The study identifies five key transcription factors (SNAI1, HDAC5, SOX4, EPAS1, and LHX9) that activate inflammatory cytokine expression, with LHX9 notably elevated in ectopic lesions and serving as a potential therapeutic target. Silencing any of these transcription factors suppresses IL6, CXCL8, and IL1B expression even in the presence of PGE2 and thrombin stimulation, while SERPINA1 overexpression similarly reduces inflammatory cytokine levels. These findings suggest that targeting LHX9 or restoring SERPINA1 levels may represent promising strategies for mitigating the chronic inflammation that characterizes endometriosis.

title: Analysis and validation of novel biomarkers related to palmitoylation in adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies palmitoylation as a critical post-translational modification mechanism in adenomyosis pathogenesis. Estrogen upregulates acyl-protein thioesterases APT1 and APT2, which promote depalmitoylation of the Scribble protein, causing its translocation from the cell membrane to cytoplasm and disrupting endometrial epithelial cell polarity. Loss of cell polarity subsequently enhances abnormal proliferation, migration, and invasion of endometrial epithelial cells, facilitating their infiltration into the myometrium and adenomyosis development. Beyond Scribble regulation, palmitoylation influences adenomyosis through ZDHHC12-mediated NLRP3 palmitoylation, which modulates inflammatory responses, and through correlation with M2 macrophage infiltration. Integrated analyses identified 549 differentially expressed genes associated with adenomyosis that participate in cell cycle regulation, chromosome segregation, lipid metabolism, steroid hormone biosynthesis, and VEGF signaling pathways. Novel biomarkers including elevated serum CA125, exosomal proteins HSP90A/STIP1/TAGLN-2, and urinary miR-92a-3p correlate with disease severity and promote angiogenesis and tumor infiltration. These findings underscore palmitoylation as a novel therapeutic target for adenomyosis management.

title: Integrative Single‐Cell Analysis Reveals Iron Overload‐Induced Senescence and Metabolic Reprogramming in Ovarian Endometriosis‐Associated Infertility
summary:
# Summary

This integrative single-cell analysis reveals that iron overload is a critical pathogenic factor in ovarian endometriosis-associated infertility (OEI), driving cellular senescence and metabolic reprogramming that compromises reproductive function. Iron accumulation in follicular fluid from OEI patients induces senescence phenotypes in oocytes and granulosa cells, characterized by elevated DNA damage signatures, suppressed meiotic processes, and upregulation of oxidative stress and ferroptosis pathways, while simultaneously downregulating DNA repair and oxidative phosphorylation mechanisms. In granulosa cells, iron overload triggers cellular senescence and iron absorption pathway activation alongside metabolic reprogramming, with key transcriptional regulators NR4A1 and EGR1 dysregulated to inhibit cell proliferation and immune function. At the oocyte level, morphological abnormalities including cortical granule loss and zona pellucida hardening occur alongside downregulation of genes essential for fertilization success (WEE2, SIN3A) and upregulation of oxidative damage-associated genes (SLC25A5, APOE, PRDX family). Ovarian endometriosis patients demonstrate significantly reduced numbers of antral follicles and mature oocytes, culminating in worse assisted reproductive outcomes and higher rates of congenital and neonatal defects, linking iron-induced cellular dysfunction directly to infertility phenotypes.

title: Serum CA125, CA199, estradiol, FSH, IL-6, TNF-a in endometriosis after administration of Tripterygium wilfordii glycosides
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated Tripterygium wilfordii glycosides as a therapeutic agent for endometriosis, a condition characterized by ectopic endometrial growth that causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility. Treatment with Tripterygium glycosides achieved a clinical effectiveness rate of 90.74%, significantly higher than the control group, and produced substantial reductions in both hormonal markers (estradiol and follicle-stimulating hormone) and tumor-associated antigens (CA125 and CA199) used clinically to assess endometriosis recurrence. The drug's therapeutic mechanism involves suppressing the inflammatory cascade by inhibiting key inflammatory cytokines IL-6 and TNF-α, as well as downstream elevation of HS-CRP, thereby reducing pelvic adhesions and fibrosis. Additionally, Tripterygium glycosides exert reversible inhibitory effects on endometrial and ovarian tissue, with combination therapy alongside gestrinone further improving ovarian endocrine function. These findings demonstrate that Tripterygium glycosides represent an effective traditional medicine-derived treatment that addresses both the inflammatory pathophysiology and hormonal dysregulation underlying endometriosis.

title: Effects of vitamin supplementation on related symptoms in women with endometriosis: a systematic review and meta-analysis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, an estrogen-dependent gynecological condition, causes dysmenorrhea, infertility, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain through mechanisms involving abnormal endometrial cell proliferation, inflammatory responses, immune dysfunction, and oxidative stress. Iron overload in endometriotic lesions generates excess reactive oxygen species, which triggers IκB phosphorylation and NF-κB activation, promoting cell proliferation and survival. Women with endometriosis exhibit significantly depleted levels of antioxidant vitamins, particularly vitamins C, D, and E, along with reduced glutathione. Meta-analysis evidence demonstrates that vitamin E supplementation effectively reduces pelvic pain, while combination therapy with vitamin C and vitamin E significantly decreases levels of malondialdehyde (a marker of oxidative stress) and improves dysmenorrhea and dyspareunia. Vitamins C and D inhibit IκB phosphorylation, thereby suppressing NF-κB signaling and the inflammatory cascade, though monotherapy with individual vitamins shows limited efficacy against some endometriosis symptoms compared to combination approaches.

title: The role of TGF-β superfamily in endometriosis: a systematic review
summary:
# Summary

The TGF-β superfamily, particularly TGF-β1 and related ligands, emerges as a key contributor to endometriosis pathogenesis through multiple interconnected mechanisms. These signaling molecules regulate critical cellular processes including proliferation, differentiation, migration, and immune modulation, directly promoting the adhesion, invasion, and proliferation of ectopic endometrial cells while simultaneously mediating fibrosis, angiogenesis, and altered immune responses within endometriotic lesions. Elevated levels of TGF-β1 and growth differentiation factor-15 (GDF-15) are detected in peritoneal fluid of affected women and correlate with disease severity, with TGF-β1 also downregulating progesterone receptor expression and inducing decidual-like changes that impair normal endometrial function. The broader TGF-β superfamily, including bone morphogenetic proteins and activins/inhibins, additionally dysregulates reproductive processes such as follicular development and ovulation while controlling endometrial remodeling. Understanding these TGF-β-mediated mechanisms is critical for developing targeted therapeutics, as current treatments rely on hormonal manipulation and surgical intervention, which address only symptomatic relief rather than underlying molecular drivers of endometriosis, including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and associated infertility.

title: Identification of Candidate Genes for Endometriosis in a Three-Generation Family with Multiple Affected Members Using Whole-Exome Sequencing
summary:
# Summary

Using whole-exome sequencing of a three-generation family with familial endometriosis, this study identified five candidate genes—LAMB4, EGFL6, NAV3, ADAMTS18, and SLIT1—that harbor rare missense variants segregating with disease. These genes encode proteins involved in critical cellular processes including proliferation, adhesion, migration, and tumor suppression, mechanisms directly implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis. The inflammatory cytokine profile in endometriosis is characterized by elevated IL-1β, IL-6, IL-28, and chemokines including CCL2, CCL5, and VEGF, while epigenetic dysregulation of estrogen metabolism genes and progesterone receptor genes contributes to the characteristic hormonal imbalance and progesterone resistance observed in affected tissues. The candidate genes function within broader dysregulated pathways including MAPK/Ras and PI3K-AKT-mTOR signaling, and several overlap with somatic mutations in endometriotic lesions, supporting both inherited and acquired genetic mechanisms in endometriosis development. These findings suggest that endometriosis involves complex interactions between inherited genetic variants affecting epithelial integrity and cell migration, acquired somatic alterations in growth pathways, and inflammatory dysregulation, collectively driving the chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility characteristic of the disease.

title: Expression of Toll-like Receptors on Lymphocyte Subpopulations and Their Soluble Forms in Serum and Urine of Women with Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial growth that causes chronic pelvic pain and infertility through dysregulation of immune responses. This study identified abnormal expression of multiple toll-like receptors (TLR2, TLR3, TLR4, TLR7, TLR8, and TLR9) on peripheral blood lymphocytes in endometriosis patients, with TLR2, TLR3, TLR4, and TLR9 also expressed in ectopic endometrial lesions, stromal and epithelial cells, and infiltrating macrophages. TLR activation triggers MyD88 and TRIF-dependent signaling cascades leading to NF-κB activation and production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α), while chronic sterile TLR stimulation sustains inflammation, promotes angiogenesis, and supports ectopic endometrial cell survival. The inflammatory microenvironment is perpetuated by estrogen dominance and progesterone resistance, as estrogens upregulate TLR2 and TLR4 and enhance immune activation, while progesterone's inhibitory effects on immune responses and NF-κB signaling are blunted. Endometriosis patients exhibited systemic immune alterations including elevated C-reactive protein, increased neutrophils, reduced eosinophils, decreased IgG, and elevated IgM and IgA levels, suggesting TLR expression patterns have diagnostic potential for differentiating endometriosis from healthy controls. These findings implicate dysregulated toll-like receptor signaling as a central mechanism linking immune dysfunction, hormone dysbalance, and persistent inflammation in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: New therapeutic targets for endometriosis predicted through mendelian randomization analysis and case-control trials
summary:
# Summary

Using Mendelian randomization analysis and case-control validation, this study identified RSPO3 and FLT1 as novel proteomic targets associated with endometriosis susceptibility. Genetically predicted increases in RSPO3 levels were causally linked to higher endometriosis risk (OR = 1.31), while decreased FLT1 levels paradoxically contributed to increased disease risk, suggesting these proteins play distinct roles in endometriosis pathogenesis. The analysis also identified 19 blood metabolites with potential causal relationships to endometriosis, including protective factors such as citrate, leucine, and 3-methyl-2-oxovalerate, alongside risk-increasing metabolites like hydroxyisovaleroyl carnitine. These findings propose RSPO3 as a promising therapeutic target for endometriosis treatment and highlight the complex interplay between genetic factors, circulating proteins, and metabolic dysregulation in disease development. The robust colocalization analysis confirmed these associations, providing a foundation for future therapeutic interventions targeting these molecular pathways in women with endometriosis-associated chronic and menstrual pain.

title: Increase in Angiogenesis and Vascularization in Patient-Derived Endometriosis Tissue: Insights from a 3D In Vivo Model
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that endometriosis exhibits tumor-like angiogenic characteristics, with significantly increased blood perfusion and vascularization compared to healthy endometrium in a three-dimensional chicken embryo model. The peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients contains elevated levels of proangiogenic factors, including VEGF and angiopoietin-2, which promote neovascularization and support lesion survival and growth. Blood perfusion increased in 75% of endometriosis tissue samples, and this vascularization correlated with disease severity, particularly in peritoneal and tubal lesions; notably, larger lesions (>3 cm) paradoxically showed lower perfusion in rectovaginal categories. The heightened angiogenic response is driven by hypoxia-induced factor expression following tissue engraftment and the estrogen-dependent nature of the disease, mechanisms that parallel those observed in malignant tumors. These findings underscore the critical role of neovascularization in endometriosis pathogenesis and suggest that anti-VEGF therapies warrant further investigation as potential therapeutic interventions.

title: Mass Spectrometry Imaging Reveals Alterations in Protein and N-Glycan Molecular Signatures in Endometriosis Tissues
summary:
# Summary

This mass spectrometry imaging study identifies distinctive molecular signatures in endometriosis lesions that reveal dysregulated inflammatory and glycosylation processes. Endometriosis tissues showed significantly elevated neutrophil defensins (DEFA1 and DEFA2) and altered N-glycan profiles, including increased fucosylated and branched N-glycans (biantennary, triantennary, and tetraantennary structures) compared to normal endometrium. These N-glycan alterations, along with enriched focal adhesion and PI3K/AKT signaling pathways, suggest involvement of abnormal cell-cell interactions and adhesion processes in lesion development. Extracellular matrix proteins including collagen, tubulin, actin, and filamin were spatially localized to endometriosis lesions, indicating aberrant tissue remodeling. The elevated fucosylation observed in endometriosis may facilitate immune evasion and promote uncontrolled growth, similar to mechanisms described in cancer cells. Together, these findings establish that dysregulation of immune responses, aberrant N-glycosylation, and altered extracellular matrix composition are key molecular features associated with endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: The secrets of menstrual blood: emerging frontiers from diagnostic tools to stem cell therapies
journal: Frontiers in Cell and Developmental Biology
summary:
# Summary

Menstrual blood is a complex biofluid derived from endometrial spiral arteries and endometrial cells that undergoes cyclical regeneration under regulation by the hypothalamus-pituitary-ovary axis. The article demonstrates that menstrual blood serves as a valuable diagnostic source, with menstrual blood-based testing achieving 97.7% sensitivity for detecting high-risk HPV and offering noninvasive alternatives for diagnosing conditions such as genital tuberculosis and cervical cancer. A key finding in endometriosis pathology is the elevated expression of P450 aromatase, chemokine ligand 5, and interleukin-1 receptor antagonist in both eutopic and ectopic endometrial lesions, contributing to the inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms underlying pelvic pain symptoms. Menstrual blood-derived stem cells (MenSCs) emerge as a promising therapeutic platform with demonstrated efficacy in treating infertility, premature ovarian insufficiency, and intrauterine adhesions through multiple mechanisms, including FAK/AKT signaling-mediated microenvironmental homeostasis and Hippo signaling-driven suppression of TGF-β-induced fibrosis. Beyond reproductive applications, MenSCs show remarkable plasticity, differentiating into multiple cell lineages including osteoblasts, chondrocytes, smooth muscle cells, hepatocyte-like cells, and keratinocytes, with clinical success demonstrated in treating Duchenne muscular dystrophy. These findings establish menstrual blood as both a powerful diagnostic resource and a source of multipotent stem cells for regenerative medicine applications across diverse organ systems.

title: Analysis of menstrual effluent uncovers endometriosis-specific cell populations and impaired cellular pathway processes
summary: This study analyzed menstrual effluent from women with and without endometriosis to identify disease-specific cellular and molecular signatures. The researchers found that menstrual effluent reflux into the peritoneal cavity, the leading hypothesis for endometriosis etiology, contains distinct immune cell populations in affected women, including elevated aged neutrophils and anti-inflammatory macrophages during early menstruation. These shifts in immune cell subpopulations alter the peritoneal microenvironment in ways that favor disease establishment and progression. The study revealed that impaired cellular clearance pathways represent a key mechanism underlying endometriosis development, as defective endometrial tissue clearance allows ectopic endometrial implants to persist outside the uterus. These findings suggest that endometriosis-specific alterations in menstrual effluent composition and immune dysfunction contribute fundamentally to the chronic inflammatory environment characteristic of this disease.

title: Chemokines and Their Association With Symptom Severity in Women With Endometriosis
summary: This study identifies key inflammatory chemokines—CCL2, CXCL8, and CXCL1—as important biomarkers associated with endometriosis symptom severity, particularly pain. CCL2 recruits macrophages to inflammatory sites and promotes neuropathic pain through CCR2 signaling, while CXCL8 (secreted by macrophages) promotes inflammation, angiogenesis, and nociception via CXCR1/CXCR2 receptors. CXCL1 emerged as the strongest predictor of symptom severity, as it drives both peripheral and central sensitization through multiple mechanisms. Notably, serum levels of CCL2 and CXCL1 were paradoxically lower in endometriosis patients compared to controls, yet increased endometrial expression of CXCL8 and CXCL1 correlated significantly with pain severity on validated pain scales (VAS and EHP-30 scores). These findings suggest that local endometrial chemokine expression, particularly CXCL1, may be more clinically relevant than systemic levels for understanding endometriosis-related pain and could represent a therapeutic target for symptom management.

title: Non-invasive diagnosis of endometrioma through cervical swabs using Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary: This study evaluated Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy applied to cervical swab samples as a non-invasive diagnostic tool for endometrioma, the ovarian cyst form of endometriosis. Analysis revealed distinctive biochemical signatures in endometrioma samples, particularly elevated carbon dioxide peaks at 2350 cm⁻¹ and increased oligosaccharide and polysaccharide peaks at 1050 cm⁻¹, reflecting alterations in carbon dioxide and carbohydrate metabolism. These metabolic changes are consistent with inflammatory states and cellular hypoxia associated with endometriosis, and carbohydrate metabolism alterations appear stage-dependent, with elevated carbohydrate bond vibrations in early-stage disease that diminish in advanced stages. Additionally, endometrioma samples demonstrated reduced lipid saturation, decreased properly folded proteins, and increased unfolded proteins in granulosa cells, indicating broader metabolic disruption. By identifying endometriosis-specific metabolic biomarkers in easily accessible cervical samples, FTIR spectroscopy offers potential to overcome the current 8-12 year diagnostic delay and reduce unnecessary surgical interventions, particularly when combined with machine learning algorithms for clinical implementation.

title: Transcriptomic-based analysis of endometrial tissues from adenomyosis patients reveals significant inflammation biomarkers: A bioinformatics study
summary:
# Summary

This transcriptomic analysis of adenomyotic endometrial tissues identifies dysregulated TNF and Rap1 signaling pathways as central mechanisms driving disease pathogenesis. TNF-α activates inflammatory responses through multiple downstream effectors, particularly TNF-α-induced protein 6 (TNFAIP6), which regulates extracellular matrix stability and immune responses, while TNF-α-induced protein 3 (TNFAIP3) suppresses NF-κB signaling to modulate inflammation. Key inflammatory biomarkers also include matrix metalloproteinase 7, which promotes extracellular matrix degradation and endometrial cell invasion, and serum and glucocorticoid-regulated kinase 1, which links cellular stress to inflammation and tissue repair processes. The Rap1 pathway independently drives cell invasion and tissue remodeling through effects on cell adhesion and migration, while macrophage differentiation emerges as a critical inflammatory process contributing to tissue remodeling and fibrosis. These interconnected inflammatory and matrix-remodeling mechanisms directly cause adenomyosis symptoms including dysmenorrhea, excessive menstrual bleeding, and infertility, suggesting that targeting TNF signaling and associated biomarkers may provide novel therapeutic approaches for this disease.

title: Genetic factors associated with the co-occurrence of endometriosis with antiphospholipid syndrome (Review)
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by immune dysregulation featuring reduced T-cell reactivity and natural killer cell cytotoxicity alongside increased peritoneal macrophage activation, with proinflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and angiogenic factors (VEGF) driving pathology. The disease carries a 2.84-fold increased risk of developing antiphospholipid syndrome and is associated with multiple autoimmune comorbidities including systemic lupus erythematosus, Sjögren's syndrome, and Crohn's disease, as well as malignancies and cardiovascular disease. Genetic susceptibility is conferred by shared HLA alleles (HLA-DQB1*0301, HLA-DQA1*0301) and polymorphisms in key immunoregulatory and coagulation genes including STAT4, TLR4, SERPINE1, and MTHFR, which influence T helper cell differentiation, Toll-like receptor signaling, fibrinolysis, and homocysteine metabolism. The SERPINE1 4G allele increases both endometriosis and hypofibrinolysis risk through impaired fibrin degradation, while STAT4-driven Th1 responses correlate with deep infiltrating endometriosis phenotypes. These overlapping genetic and immunologic mechanisms suggest that endometriosis and antiphospholipid syndrome share common pathogenic pathways involving thrombotic and inflammatory processes.

title: From the Editor’s Desk
journal: Journal of Healthcare, Science and the Humanities
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain. The pathophysiology of endometriosis involves a robust inflammatory response characterized by significantly elevated levels of key pro-inflammatory cytokines in peritoneal fluid, including interleukin-6 (IL-6), tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α), and interleukin-8 (IL-8). These elevated inflammatory mediators in the peritoneal environment appear to drive the disease's clinical manifestations and may contribute to both pain symptoms and reproductive dysfunction. The identification of this cytokine signature in peritoneal fluid provides insight into the inflammatory mechanisms underlying endometriosis and suggests that modulating these cytokine pathways could represent a therapeutic strategy for managing this debilitating condition. Understanding the role of these inflammatory factors is essential for developing targeted interventions that address both the symptomatic burden and fertility complications associated with endometriosis.

title: Effectiveness of Medical Nutrition Therapy in the Management of Patients with Obesity and Endometriosis: from the Mediterranean Diet To the Ketogenic Diet, Through Supplementation. The Role of the Nutritionist in Clinical Management
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterus, affects approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is driven by interconnected pathophysiological mechanisms including systemic inflammation, oxidative stress, hormonal imbalance, and aberrant angiogenesis involving macrophages and T-cells. Medical nutrition therapy targeting these mechanisms offers substantial symptom management, with the Mediterranean diet demonstrating particular efficacy through its modulation of prostaglandin synthesis, estrogen clearance, and oxidative balance via omega-3 polyunsaturated fatty acids and polyphenols. Specific micronutrient supplementation—including omega-3 fatty acids, N-acetylcysteine, resveratrol, vitamins C and E, and probiotics—significantly reduces endometriosis symptoms by suppressing pro-inflammatory prostaglandin synthesis, reducing oxidative damage in peritoneal fluid, and restoring microbial homeostasis. Ketogenic diet interventions lower insulin levels and modulate insulin-like growth factor-1 signaling and aromatase activity, thereby reducing pelvic pain and inflammatory pathways. Obesity paradoxically worsens endometriosis severity through adipocyte dysfunction-mediated increases in inflammation, oxidative stress, and angiogenesis, whereas lower BMI inversely correlates with disease risk. Integrating nutritional interventions with clinical management represents a promising adjunctive strategy for comprehensive endometriosis treatment.

title: The Role of Endometriosis in Intestinal Inflammation: A Combined Mendelian Randomization and Cellular Study
journal: Journal of Cellular and Molecular Medicine
summary: This study establishes a causal relationship between endometriosis and inflammatory bowel disease using Mendelian randomization analysis, demonstrating significant associations with both ulcerative colitis and Crohn's disease. Mechanistically, peritoneal fluid from endometriosis patients impairs intestinal epithelial barrier function by downregulating tight junction proteins (ZO-1, Occludin, Claudin-3, and Claudin-4) and increasing paracellular permeability, while simultaneously promoting intestinal epithelial cell apoptosis through upregulation of pro-apoptotic proteins Bax and Cytochrome c and downregulation of anti-apoptotic Bcl-2. The peritoneal fluid contains elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and IL-1β) that activate NF-κB signaling and JAK/STAT and MAPK/ERK pathways, driving epithelial barrier disruption and immune dysregulation. These findings reveal a mechanistic link where endometriosis-derived inflammatory mediators directly compromise intestinal epithelial integrity through convergent inflammatory and apoptotic pathways, providing a molecular basis for the increased prevalence of IBD in endometriosis patients and identifying potential therapeutic targets involving barrier function restoration and cytokine modulation.

title: Feasibility of SNP Genotyping Using Dried Blood Spot Samples Collected in an Epidemiological Study and Its Integration With Genetic Risk Analysis for Endometriosis
summary: This study evaluated the feasibility of SNP genotyping using dried blood spot (DBS) samples for genetic risk analysis of endometriosis. Five endometriosis-associated SNPs (rs10965235, rs12700667, rs12024204, rs16826658, and rs801112) were genotyped in case samples, with DBS samples demonstrating robust suitability for SNP genotyping across various storage conditions, including mail-based epidemiological studies. Among the tested variants, only the CC genotype of rs10965235 showed a potentially protective association with endometriosis (odds ratio: 0.19), though this finding requires further validation. The study identified multiple genetic and molecular contributors to endometriosis susceptibility, including polymorphisms in inflammatory genes (IL1A, IL10), hormone-related genes (HSD17ß1, ESR2, PGR), and developmental genes (WNT4, VEZT, FSHB, CDH1), alongside non-genetic factors such as hormonal imbalances, immune dysfunction, inflammation, and oxidative stress. While DBS samples maintained stable fluorescence signals across most storage conditions, degradation was observed at 70°C, suggesting optimal storage protocols are necessary. These findings establish DBS as a practical and reliable sample collection method for genetic endometriosis research while highlighting the complex multifactorial nature of disease etiology.

title: Polychlorinated biphenyl Endocrine Disruptor Alters Estrogen Receptor beta–Mediated Epigenetic Regulation, Promoting Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Polychlorinated biphenyl-126 (PCB-126), an environmental endocrine disruptor, promotes endometriosis progression through a coordinated activation of the AXL/ERβ/DNMT3A signaling axis. PCB-126 upregulates the AXL/GAS6 signaling pathway, which enhances estrogen receptor-β (ERβ) activity and downstream activation of steroid receptor coactivator-1 (SRC-1) and matrix metalloproteinase-9 (MMP9), collectively driving ectopic lesion growth. Additionally, the PCB-126/ERβ axis upregulates DNA methyltransferase 3A (Dnmt3a) expression, contributing to inflammatory and immune dysregulatory responses within ectopic endometrial tissue. PCB-126 also stimulates steroidogenic enzymes and aromatase activity, elevating local estrogen production and promoting AhR/ER signaling crosstalk. Treatment with BMS-777607, an AXL inhibitor, effectively suppresses ectopic lesion growth, offering a potential therapeutic strategy. These findings demonstrate that PCB-126 disrupts estrogen-mediated epigenetic regulation through multiple interconnected pathways, providing mechanistic insight into how environmental contaminants promote endometriosis development in exposed populations.

title: Neuroinflammation is responsible for pain in endometriosis - targeting the JAK-STAT pathway and mast cell activation
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis causes chronic pain through neuroinflammatory mechanisms involving mast cell activation and dysregulated JAK/STAT signaling, which together promote both immune activation and excessive nerve fiber growth in endometriotic lesions. Elevated inflammatory mediators including TNFα, interleukins, and nerve growth factor (NGF) in lesions activate peritoneal mast cells and macrophages, which colocalize with hyperinnervated nerve fibers and enhance nociceptive signaling through NGF-TrkA and TRPV1 pathways. The resulting central sensitization amplifies pain perception and produces measurable neurobiological changes including altered brain morphology, elevated glutamate in the anterior insula, and microglial activation in pain-processing regions. Emerging therapeutic approaches targeting JAK inhibitors and mast cell stabilizers show promise in modulating these pathways, while TRPV1 blockade and CGRP-RAMP1 inhibition have demonstrated effectiveness in reducing pain responses and lesion size in preclinical models.

title: Growth Arrest-Specific Protein 6 Is Elevated in Endometriosis but Shows Poor Diagnostic Performance
summary:
# Summary

Plasma levels of growth arrest-specific protein 6 (GAS6) are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients and contribute to disease pathophysiology through multiple mechanisms. GAS6 binds to the AXL receptor tyrosine kinase and activates downstream signaling cascades including JAK/STAT3, PI3K/AKT/mTOR, and FAK/Src/NF-κB pathways that promote cell proliferation, migration, angiogenesis, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, and immune modulation. Within endometriotic lesions, GAS6 is expressed by epithelial cells, stromal cells, macrophages, and fibroblasts, where it upregulates pro-fibrotic factors and facilitates efferocytosis—a process involving phagocytic clearance of apoptotic cells that perpetuates chronic inflammation. Elevated GAS6 levels correlate with increased pain symptoms including dysmenorrhea intensity and dyspareunia, while TAM receptor signaling modulates neuroinflammation and pain through microglial activation. Despite its strong association with endometriosis and its pathophysiological contributions, GAS6 demonstrates poor diagnostic performance as a standalone plasma biomarker, suggesting its role is more relevant to disease mechanism and symptom severity than disease detection.

title: Using systems biology and drug repositioning approaches to discover FDA-approved drugs candidates for endometriosis treatment
summary: This systems biology study identifies inflammatory and angiogenic pathways central to endometriosis pathogenesis and uses drug repositioning to discover FDA-approved therapeutic candidates. Endometriosis-associated chronic pelvic pain and infertility are driven by aberrant VEGF-VEGFR2 signaling that promotes angiogenesis, endothelial permeability, and ectopic lesion growth, while elevated IL-6 from immune cells activates macrophages to enhance endometrial proliferation. Key regulatory networks involve microRNAs (hsa-miR-155-5p, hsa-miR-335-5p, hsa-miR-199a-3p) and transcription factors (FOXC1, STAT3) that control both IL-6 and VEGFR2 expression. Through molecular docking analysis targeting VEGFR2, the study identified ponatinib as a promising FDA-approved drug candidate, while natural compounds including tanshinone IIA and ginsenoside Rg3 demonstrated therapeutic efficacy by inhibiting VEGF, phosphorylated Akt, and downstream signaling pathways. This multi-target approach offers an alternative to current GnRH-agonist therapy, which causes significant adverse effects including estrogen insufficiency, providing potentially safer options for endometriosis management.

title: 子宫内膜异位症患者腹腔镜术后复发危险因素及其预测价值
summary: This study identifies key clinical and biochemical predictors of endometriosis recurrence following laparoscopic surgery. Patient factors associated with postoperative recurrence include prior dysmenorrhea history, multiple pregnancies, smoking, alcohol consumption, and previous uterine cavity procedures. Serum inflammatory cytokines—particularly TGF-β1, IL-1β, IL-17, and IL-22—are significantly elevated in patients who experience recurrence and promote key pathogenic mechanisms including angiogenesis, tissue remodeling, fibrosis, and neurogenesis in ectopic endometrial lesions. IL-1β specifically enhances the invasiveness and adhesive capacity of ectopic endometrial tissue while upregulating COX-2 and prostaglandin E2 expression, contributing to dysmenorrhea. The combination of preoperative serum TGF-β1 plus IL-1β demonstrates high predictive value for recurrence (AUC 0.980), with further improvement when IL-22 is included (AUC 0.982), suggesting these biomarkers may serve as practical tools for risk stratification in endometriosis patients undergoing surgical treatment.

title: Retraction Note: NLRC5 exerts anti-endometriosis effects through inhibiting ERβ-mediated inflammatory response
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic gynecological disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth, causing pelvic pain and infertility in reproductive-age women, with estrogen playing a critical role in disease pathogenesis. The inflammatory microenvironment of endometriotic lesions is perpetuated through multiple interconnected pathways: TNF-α upregulates aromatase expression in endometrial stromal cells, promoting local estrogen production, while macrophages produce elevated levels of inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and IL-8 that drive NF-κB signaling activation within lesions. This inflammatory cascade is complemented by elevated VEGF and enhanced angiogenesis, which are essential for endometriotic tissue survival and disease progression. Although this retracted article originally proposed that NLRC5 could exert anti-endometriosis effects through inhibiting estrogen receptor-β-mediated inflammatory responses, the retraction indicates that the reported findings could not be substantiated, highlighting the complexity of targeting inflammatory pathways in endometriosis treatment.

title: Rethinking the Estrogen Receptor Beta Dominance Hypothesis in Endometriosis: Insights from Single Cell RNA Sequencing Meta-analysis
summary: This single-cell RNA sequencing meta-analysis challenges the prevailing estrogen receptor beta (ERβ) dominance hypothesis in endometriosis by revealing that ESR1 (encoding ERα) is substantially more highly expressed than ESR2 (encoding ERβ) across all major cell types in endometriotic tissues. Rather than ERβ driving pathology, the study demonstrates that ESR1+ stromal cells orchestrate disease-relevant processes through activation of PI3K/Akt and MAPK signaling pathways that promote proliferation and tissue remodeling, while simultaneously downregulating HLA genes to facilitate immune evasion. The findings show that ERα and ERβ function in complementary rather than antagonistic roles, with ESR2+ cells maintaining higher HLA expression and distinct signaling patterns. This reframing suggests that ERβ-selective modulators may be ineffective therapeutic strategies if ERβ is not the dominant receptor driving disease pathology. The study underscores that endometriosis arises from complex isoform-specific estrogen receptor signaling patterns that are both tissue and cell-type dependent, implicating altered estrogen-driven proliferation and immune dysregulation as central to the disease mechanism underlying dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility.

title: Shared and distinct molecular pathways in eutopic endometrium of endometriosis and adenomyosis patients: a transcriptomic study in Chinese women
summary: This transcriptomic study in Chinese women identified shared and distinct molecular pathways in the eutopic endometrium of endometriosis and adenomyosis patients. Both conditions share common clinical symptoms—chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and dysmenorrhea—and exhibit elevated serum markers CA125 and HE4, though adenomyosis shows significantly reduced AMH levels. Endometriosis-specific pathways involve natural killer cell-mediated cytotoxicity, oxidative phosphorylation, arachidonic acid metabolism, and immune processes including innate mucosal immunity and cytoplasmic translation, with key hub genes including PTPRC, TNF, ITGAM, and CD19.
