Adenomyosis is characterized by epithelial remodeling and Wnt signaling, with prominent involvement of metallo-sulfur cluster assembly and nucleoside bisphosphate metabolism. Both conditions converge on IL-17 signaling, chemokine signaling, immune cell proliferation, chemotaxis, and epithelial cell adhesion, with shared hub genes CXCR2 and MMP9 suggesting overlapping inflammatory and tissue remodeling mechanisms. These findings indicate that while endometriosis and adenomyosis share inflammatory immunological bases, they have distinct molecular signatures in the eutopic endometrium that may inform disease-specific therapeutic strategies.

title: Understanding Endometriosis: A Broad Review of Its Causes, Management, and Impact
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex gynecological disorder affecting approximately 10% of women worldwide, characterized by chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and increased risk of epithelial ovarian cancer. The disease develops through multiple mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia of peritoneal mesothelium, lymphovascular dissemination, and stem cell involvement, with contributions from genetic variants (WNT4, VEZT, CDKN2B-AS1, ID4, GREBI), hormonal dysregulation (progesterone resistance, aromatase overexpression, 17β-estradiol signaling), and somatic mutations (KRAS, PIK3CA). A dysregulated immune microenvironment drives pathogenesis through neutrophil infiltration and neutrophil extracellular trap formation, which release inflammatory mediators including CXCL1, CXCL2, CXCL8/IL8, IL6, and VEGF that promote angiogenesis, immune cell recruitment, and myeloid-derived suppressor cell infiltration. Beyond the primary gynecological manifestations, endometriosis causes substantial psychological burden including anxiety, depression, and chronic stress, along with gastrointestinal symptoms such as bloating, constipation, and fatigue. The interplay of genetic predisposition, epigenetic modifications, hormonal dysfunction, and inflammatory amplification establishes endometriosis as a multifactorial disease requiring integrated understanding of its molecular mechanisms for effective therapeutic intervention.

title: Identification of Adeno-Associate Virus (AAV) Serotype for Endometriosis Therapy and Effect of AAV-Mediated RNAi Delivery on Gene Expression and Cell Proliferation in In Vitro Endometrial Cell Culture
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent condition affecting 5-15% of reproductive-age women, causing dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility through a pathological positive feedback loop involving estrogen receptor beta (ERβ), cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2), and prostaglandin E2. This study evaluated adeno-associated virus (AAV) serotypes as vectors for gene therapy targeting this inflammatory cascade, identifying AAV3 as demonstrating selective infectivity toward endometrial cells while sparing ovarian tissue, a critical advantage over conventional therapies that suppress ovulation. Using AAV3-mediated RNA interference, researchers successfully downregulated ERβ and COX-2 expression in ectopic and eutopic endometrial cells, resulting in modest reductions in cellular metabolic activity under estradiol and inflammatory stimulation. The findings suggest that targeting the ERβ-COX-2-estrogen biosynthesis axis via AAV3 gene therapy offers a tissue-selective alternative to current hormone-suppressive treatments, potentially avoiding ovarian dysfunction while addressing key inflammatory and proliferative mechanisms in endometriosis. These results provide preclinical support for AAV3-mediated RNAi as a promising therapeutic approach to disrupt the positive feedback loop driving endometriotic lesion growth and persistence.

title: Lipidome atlas of human myometrium reveals distinctive lipid signatures associated with adenomyosis: Combination of high-coverage lipidomics and mass spectrometry imaging
summary:
# Summary

This comprehensive lipidomic analysis identified distinctive lipid signatures in myometrial tissues associated with adenomyosis, revealing that disturbed lipid metabolism plays a central role in the disease pathogenesis. Among 317 lipid species quantified in myometrial tissues, 83 showed significant alterations in adenomyosis patients, with lipid aberrations involving multiple pathways that promote inflammation, cell migration, and immune dysregulation—processes critical to abnormal endometrial invasion and proliferation. A diagnostic panel of five lipid species (CE 18:2, HexCer 18:0/16:0, LPC 18:2, TAG 50:3_FA 18:2, and TAG 52:2_FA 18:1) effectively distinguished adenomyosis samples, with specific lipids enriched in either ectopic endometrial tissue or myometrium. Notably, ceramides were significantly elevated in adenomyosis myometrium and suppress uterine contractions while promoting prostaglandin E2 production, while glycerophospholipids (phosphatidylethanolamine, phosphatidylglycerol, phosphatidylcholine) were markedly increased, whereas cholesterol esters and triacylglycerols were decreased. These findings establish lipid dysmetabolism as a fundamental mechanism underlying adenomyosis-associated symptoms including dysmenorrhea, menorrhagia, and chronic pelvic pain.

title: Elevated levels of TRAb IgG autoantibodies are not recognized in endometriosis by the current clinical methods
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth driven by estrogen-mediated inflammation and involves surprising connections to thyroid dysfunction. The endometrium expresses both TSH and thyroid hormone receptors, which are significantly elevated in ectopic endometrial lesions, and thyroxine directly stimulates proliferation of ectopic endometrial cells. Patients with endometriosis show higher prevalence of hypothyroidism and thyroid autoimmunity compared to controls, and those with concomitant thyroid dysfunction experience more severe chronic pelvic pain. Multiple studies have reported elevated TSH receptor antibodies (TRAb IgG and IgM) in endometriosis patients, yet current clinical diagnostic methods fail to reliably detect these autoantibodies in routine testing. Despite the documented immunological associations and the potential mechanistic role of TSH receptors and autoimmunity in endometriosis pathophysiology, further research is needed to establish the clinical significance of these findings and develop improved detection methods.

title: miRNA expression haplotype in Hispanics with endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study evaluated a microRNA expression haplotype as a diagnostic biomarker for endometriosis in Hispanic women, identifying miR-451a, miR-3613, and let-7b as the most diagnostically relevant individual markers with area-under-the-curve values ranging from 0.667 to 0.79. A composite panel comprising six microRNAs (miR-451a, miR-3613, miR-125b, let-7b, miR-150, and miR-342) achieved superior diagnostic accuracy, with logistic regression and classification and regression tree analyses yielding AUC values of 0.914, representing high sensitivity and specificity. The downregulation of these microRNAs in endometriosis patients impairs their normal negative regulation of key oncogenic and proliferative genes including Bcl-2, cyclin D, RAS, and EGFR, thereby promoting aberrant cell proliferation and ectopic endometrial implantation. These findings suggest that a microRNA-based diagnostic panel could serve as a highly sensitive and specific complementary tool for endometriosis diagnosis, offering an alternative to current imaging and invasive diagnostic approaches in this condition characterized by chronic inflammation, pelvic pain, and infertility.

title: ABSTRACTS
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, causing infertility and chronic pain through dysregulated inflammation and angiogenesis. The condition involves ectopic implantation of endometrial cells outside the uterus, driven by elevated estrogen production via increased aromatase expression and hyperactivation of the NF-κB signaling pathway. This leads to elevated TNF-α and IL-6 production by both ectopic endometrial cells and infiltrating peritoneal macrophages, which promote angiogenesis and lesion growth through VEGF signaling and support endometrial cell migration via epithelial-mesenchymal transition. The reduced expression of miRNA-145 in ectopic endometrial tissue contributes to disease pathology by failing to inhibit TNF-α and IL-6, suppress NF-κB signaling, and promote apoptosis of ectopic cells. Notably, miRNA-145 reduction is also associated with progesterone resistance, limiting the efficacy of standard progestin-based therapies. Experimental restoration of miRNA-145 through mimic transfection significantly reduced lesion size and inflammation in mouse models, suggesting miRNA-145 replacement therapy represents a novel therapeutic approach for endometriosis.

title: Comparison of serum markers of inflammation in endometrioma and benign ovarian cysts
journal: Revista Brasileira de Ginecologia e Obstetrícia
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease that triggers systemic inflammation and alterations in immune function, leading to characteristic clinical manifestations including pelvic pain, infertility, increased pain sensitivity, and mood disorders. The disease promotes a reduction in T-lymphocyte-mediated cytotoxicity while simultaneously causing neutrophilia, thrombocytosis, and lymphocytopenia, resulting in elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), and red cell distribution width (RDW) in patients with endometriomas compared to those with benign ovarian cysts. Peritoneal neutrophils and macrophages secrete pro-inflammatory cytokines including vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), interleukin-8 (IL-8), and interleukin-12 (IL-12), which promote the growth, invasion, and angiogenesis of endometriotic lesions. Notably, NLR demonstrates a significant positive correlation with cyst size and disease severity, establishing serum inflammatory markers as potential diagnostic indicators for endometriosis. Elevated postoperative NLR and PLR also correlate with advanced-stage ovarian pathology and increased cardiovascular and thrombotic complications, suggesting these markers have prognostic significance beyond diagnosis.

title: Special Issue “Circulating Non-Coding RNAs as Diagnostic and Prognostic Markers of Human Diseases, 2nd Edition”
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by complex interactions between immune dysregulation, aberrant angiogenesis, and hormonal signaling that collectively drive ectopic lesion establishment and progression. Macrophages play a central role in this pathophysiology by producing pro-inflammatory cytokines, particularly IL-6 and TNF-α, which are significantly elevated in endometriotic lesions and peritoneal fluid. Additional pathogenic mechanisms include angiogenesis and lymphangiogenesis, processes driven by VEGF that promote ectopic endometrial growth, alongside elevated prostaglandins and estrogen signaling that further enhance tissue remodeling. Alterations in endometrial cell behavior and immune cell populations, including dysregulation of macrophages and T cells, facilitate ectopic lesion establishment and maintain the chronic inflammatory microenvironment. These interconnected molecular mechanisms underlie the clinical manifestations of endometriosis, which include infertility and chronic pain in reproductive-age women. Understanding these circulating biomarkers and inflammatory pathways is essential for developing improved diagnostic and prognostic tools for endometriosis.

title: Comparative Ultrasonographic Evaluation of Morphology and Vascularization in Endometriomas and Ovarian Mature Cystic Teratomas †
summary: This ultrasound study compared the morphological and vascular characteristics of endometriomas and mature cystic teratomas to improve diagnostic differentiation between these benign ovarian masses. Endometriomas demonstrated a distinctive hemodynamic signature characterized by elevated resistance index (RI) values and reduced uterine artery resistance index (AURI), reflecting the pathological angiogenesis underlying endometriosis, whereas mature cystic teratomas exhibited lower RI values and more organized vascularization patterns. The elevated RI in endometriomas results from chronic bleeding and iron deposition that trigger tissue hypoxia, driving sustained VEGF overexpression that produces immature, dysfunctional vessels with incomplete pericyte coverage and surrounding fibrosis. Morphologically, endometriomas typically appeared as unilocular cysts with ground-glass echogenicity and demonstrated heightened angiogenic activity driven by estrogen-dependent mechanisms and inflammatory mediators including TNF-α and interleukins, while teratomas presented distinctive features such as Rokitansky nodules and echogenic sebaceous material. The combination of higher intratumoral RI and lower ipsilateral AURI could form a distinctive hemodynamic signature for endometriomas, offering objective Doppler parameters to supplement conventional B-mode ultrasound in differentiating these benign masses. These findings underscore how understanding vascularization patterns and morphological characteristics can enhance gynecologists' diagnostic assessment and clinical management of adnexal masses.

title: Identification of Proteins and MicroRNAs with Prognostic Value for Assisted Reproduction Technology Outcomes in Follicular Fluid of Women with Endometriosis: A Pilot Study
summary:
# Summary

This pilot study identified dysregulated proteins and microRNAs in follicular fluid as potential biomarkers for endometriosis and assisted reproduction outcomes. Endometriosis, which causes infertility and pelvic pain through inflammatory mechanisms, showed altered levels of key factors affecting oocyte quality: anti-Müllerian hormone (AMH) was reduced while oocyte-secreted factors GDF-9 and BMP-15 were elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls. Multiple microRNAs showed diagnostic promise, with miR-125b-5p and miR-212-3p significantly decreased in endometriosis follicular fluid (AUC 0.84 and 0.73 respectively), while miR-20a, miR-145, and miR-320a were elevated and involved in suppressing inflammatory TGF-β signaling and endometrial cell migration. In the control group, miR-145 and miR-320a in follicular fluid predicted biochemical pregnancy success, suggesting these microRNAs may serve as dual biomarkers for both disease diagnosis and reproductive outcomes in assisted reproductive technology.

title: Integrating Thyroid Autoantibodies With CA125 Enhances Diagnostic Precision for Ovarian Endometriosis: A Retrospective Study of 885 Patients
journal: The Journal of Obstetrics and Gynaecology Research
summary: This retrospective study of 885 patients demonstrates that integrating thyroid autoantibodies with CA125 substantially improves diagnostic accuracy for ovarian endometriosis, achieving an AUC of 0.924 compared to CA125 alone (0.895). Patients with ovarian endometriosis exhibited significantly elevated levels of CA125, thyroid peroxidase antibodies (TPOAb), and thyroglobulin antibodies (TGAb), with multivariate analysis identifying all three as independent risk factors (CA125: OR=18.4; TGAb: OR=4.03; TPOAb: OR=2.63). The high prevalence of autoimmune thyroid disease in endometriosis patients (51% versus 15% in controls) and the elevated thyroid autoantibodies suggest shared immunological mechanisms involving immune dysregulation, impaired natural killer cell cytotoxicity, and enhanced inflammatory responses. Thyroid autoantibodies may exacerbate pelvic inflammation through molecular mimicry and promote endometrial cell adhesion and angiogenesis, creating a permissive environment for ectopic endometrial tissue implantation. These findings establish thyroid autoantibodies as promising biomarkers that, combined with CA125, provide enhanced diagnostic precision for ovarian endometriosis and support thyroid autoimmunity as a mechanistic contributor to endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: The Role of Nutrition in Endometriosis Prevention and Management:
summary: Alcohol and dietary saturated and trans fats significantly increase endometriosis risk through multiple inflammatory and hormonal pathways. Alcohol consumption upregulates aromatase activity, leading to elevated estradiol levels, while its metabolism activates pro-inflammatory and oxidative stress pathways that culminate in nuclear factor-kappa B activation and peritoneal macrophage production of cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and CRP. Red meat consumption similarly elevates endometriosis risk by increasing estradiol through reduced sex hormone-binding globulin and amplifying oxidative stress via its high iron content; when grilled at high temperatures, red meat produces polycyclic aromatic hydrocarbons that dysregulate reproductive hormones including estradiol, testosterone, FSH, and LH. Antioxidant nutrients—vitamins A, C, E, selenium, and zinc—mitigate endometriosis pathogenesis by neutralizing reactive oxygen species and suppressing inflammatory cytokine production, while vitamin A metabolites regulate gut microbiota-derived butyrate, which maintains the intestinal barrier and suppresses systemic inflammation. Deficiencies in these protective micronutrients are associated with elevated oxidative stress in endometrial lesions and follicular fluid, whereas supplementation with all-trans retinoic acid and selenium demonstrates therapeutic potential by reducing estrogen levels and inhibiting angiogenesis.

title: Circulating microRNAs and endometriosis: a comprehensive analysis and validation of identified biomarkers in an Indian population
summary: This study identified circulating microRNAs as potential biomarkers for endometriosis in an Indian population, with miR-451a and miR-20a-5p significantly reduced in endometriosis patients compared to controls. MiR-451a functions as a tumor suppressor and anti-inflammatory regulator by targeting IL-6R/JAK2/STAT3 and MIF/PI3K/AKT pathways, while miR-20a-5p, a member of the miR-17-92 cluster, suppresses proinflammatory cytokines through NF-κB signaling modulation and targets JAK1 and RUNX3. The reduced expression of miR-20a-5p specifically suggests impaired immune evasion and lesion maintenance in endometriosis, with elevated miR-20a implicated in disease pathogenesis by suppressing Netrin-4, a molecule critical for cellular adhesion and angiogenesis. Circulating miRNAs detected in plasma, serum, exosomes, and other body fluids offer non-invasive diagnostic alternatives to laparoscopy, the current invasive gold standard, while conventional imaging modalities remain inadequate for detection and staging. The study validated nine miRNAs as biomarker candidates, with findings supporting a central role for dysregulated miRNA-mediated inflammation, angiogenesis, and lesion invasion in endometriosis pathogenesis.

title: Mediterranean diet adherence and healthy diet indicator might decrease odds of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that Mediterranean diet adherence and higher healthy diet indicator scores are associated with substantially reduced endometriosis odds, with women showing greater adherence experiencing approximately 94-95% lower odds of disease. The protective effects operate through multiple biological mechanisms: dietary patterns rich in fruits, vegetables, legumes, fish, and whole grains reduce systemic inflammation, modulate prostaglandin metabolism, and decrease oxidative stress. Specific dietary components contribute protective effects, including polyunsaturated fatty acids and omega-3 fatty acids that provide anti-inflammatory benefits, magnesium that prevents intracellular calcium elevation and pain signaling, and olive oil's oleocanthal compound that inhibits cyclooxygenase similar to ibuprofen. Conversely, high intake of meat, saturated fats, and excessive dairy products significantly increases endometriosis risk through pro-inflammatory pathways and elevated cytokine production. These findings establish diet as a key modifiable lifestyle factor capable of influencing both endometriosis onset and symptom severity, suggesting dietary interventions could improve quality of life and reduce pain in affected women.

title: Etiologies of endometriosis and model systems: is there a risk of a tunnel vision?
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex multifactorial disease characterized by immune dysregulation, recurrent inflammatory processes, and fibrosis that presents in diverse forms including superficial lesions, deep infiltrating lesions, and ovarian endometriomas, causing debilitating pain and infertility. While retrograde menstruation is the most widely accepted etiology, alternative mechanisms including coelomic metaplasia, embryonic rests, and stem cell origins also contribute, with genetic predisposition, environmental factors, and altered immune and endocrine functions serving as additional risk factors. Endometriotic lesions acquire critical characteristics through vascularization, innervation, and immune cell infiltration, driven by activated neutrophils and macrophages that release pro-angiogenic cytokines, while impaired natural killer cell function—suppressed by elevated IL-6—fails to clear ectopic endometrial cells. Molecular alterations including somatic mutations in KRAS and PIK3CA, aberrant expression of hormone receptors and metabolic enzymes (P450 aromatase, sulfatases), and dysregulated adhesion molecules (integrins) mediated by TNFα, TGFβ, and IL-1 enable lesion establishment and progression. Endometrial mesenchymal stem/stromal cells expressing SUSD2 contribute angiogenic and immunomodulatory functions essential for lesion survival, while epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition processes facilitate the transformation enabling metaplasia and lesion formation in the peritoneal environment.

title: Hypoxia-inducible factor-1 alpha expression in endometriosis: A retrospective observational case–control study of ovarian cyst capsules and endometrial tissue samples
journal: The Journal of International Medical Research
summary: This retrospective case-control study investigated hypoxia-inducible factor-1α (HIF-1α) expression in endometriosis, a condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. The researchers found significantly elevated HIF-1α expression in ovarian endometriotic cyst capsules and endometrial tissue from endometriosis patients compared to controls. HIF-1α acts as a master transcription factor that regulates multiple pathways critical to endometriosis pathogenesis, including angiogenesis (via vascular endothelial growth factor, interleukin-8, and cyclooxygenase-2), cellular metabolism, cell survival, epithelial-mesenchymal transition, and invasion through regulation of matrix metalloproteinases. The hypoxic microenvironment within endometriotic lesions exacerbates inflammatory responses and drives tissue remodeling and extracellular matrix degradation, processes essential for ectopic lesion establishment and progression. HIF-1α expression was detected across epithelial cells, macrophages, and stromal cells, with higher levels in ovarian endometriomas and advanced-stage disease, while obesity and lower body mass index were associated with altered HIF-1α expression patterns. These findings establish hypoxia and HIF-1α signaling as central mechanisms linking reduced oxygen availability to the pathogenic cascade of endometriosis.

title: Relationship Between Inflammation-Nutrition Composite Indices and Ovarian Endometrioma: A Retrospective Observational Study
summary: This retrospective study identifies inflammation-nutrition composite indices as diagnostic markers for ovarian endometrioma (OMA), a severe form of endometriosis characterized by chronic inflammation involving dysregulated immune cells including neutrophils, lymphocytes, macrophages, natural killer cells, and dendritic cells. Patients with OMA exhibited significantly elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio (NLR), platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), systemic immune-inflammation index (SII), and neutrophil percentage-to-albumin glycoprotein ratio (NP/AGR), alongside reduced albumin and albumin-to-globulin ratio (AGR), reflecting heightened inflammatory status and compromised nutritional status. The NP/AGR index demonstrated the strongest diagnostic value with an area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.799, outperforming individual inflammatory markers. Mechanistically, neutrophils drive endometriotic lesion formation by producing angiogenic factors (VEGF, IL-8, CXCL10) and reactive oxygen species, while IL-6 and TLR4 signaling promote neutrophil recruitment and activation, with monocyte products further reducing albumin production during inflammation. The study suggests that composite inflammation-nutrition indices provide superior diagnostic utility compared to single biomarkers, potentially offering a more comprehensive assessment for endometriosis management.

title: Altered miR‐410‐3p and Target Genes (PCDH8, VEGFA, NUMB) Expression in Ovarian Endometriosis: A Case‐Control Study
summary:
# Summary

This case-control study identifies a dysregulated microRNA pathway central to ovarian endometriosis pathogenesis. MicroRNA-410-3p is significantly downregulated in ectopic endometrial tissue compared to eutopic and control endometrium, and it directly regulates three target genes with opposing roles in angiogenesis: VEGFA and NUMB are elevated in ectopic lesions, while PCDH8 is reduced. The dysregulation creates a pro-angiogenic environment, as reduced miR-410-3p permits increased VEGFA and NUMB expression, both of which promote vascular endothelial growth factor signaling and blood vessel formation, while simultaneously allowing decreased PCDH8, which normally functions as a tumor suppressor to inhibit angiogenesis and epithelial-mesenchymal transition. NUMB additionally regulates VEGF receptor endocytosis and signaling, further amplifying pro-angiogenic mechanisms essential for ectopic endometrial establishment and lesion growth. The authors propose miR-410-3p as a promising biomarker for ovarian endometriosis and suggest that restoring its expression or inhibiting its target genes could represent potential therapeutic strategies targeting the angiogenic cascade driving endometriosis development.

title: Metabolome analysis as a potential source of endometriosis biomarkers with the use of multiomics approach in its diagnosis
summary:
# Summary

This metabolomic study identifies multiple circulating and peritoneal biomarkers that distinguish endometriosis patients from controls through a multiomics approach. Phosphatidylcholine (PC ae C30:2) emerges as a key diagnostic metabolite, being uniquely elevated in both plasma and peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients, and serves as a precursor to pro-inflammatory prostaglandins that promote inflammation, angiogenesis, cell proliferation, and invasion while simultaneously inhibiting apoptosis. Additional dysregulated lipid species including acylcarnitines, lysophosphatidylcholine, and glycerophospholipids accumulate in endometriosis, with acylcarnitines linked to incomplete fatty acid oxidation and oxidative stress, processes fundamentally associated with the disease pathology. Glycerophospholipids also generate lysophosphatidic acid, which drives endometrial cell proliferation and migration. Integration of metabolomic analysis with proteomic data yields superior diagnostic classification models, and timing analysis relative to the menstrual cycle phase further enhances discriminatory potential, suggesting that multiple metabolite alterations collectively characterize endometriosis's inflammatory and proliferative phenotype.

title: Reproductive tract microbiota dysbiosis in ovarian endometrioma and adenomyosis: multi-site 16S rRNA profiling and functional impact of key bacterial species on human endometrial stromal cells
summary: This study demonstrates that endometriosis and adenomyosis are associated with distinct reproductive tract microbiota dysbiosis characterized by enrichment of opportunistic and pro-inflammatory bacteria, particularly Enterococcus species in adenomyosis and Enterobacteriaceae family members in ovarian endometriomas (chocolate cysts), while beneficial Lactobacillus species are depleted in affected individuals compared to normal endometrium. Functional co-culture experiments reveal that pathogenic bacteria impair endometrial stromal cell viability and trigger distinct transcriptomic alterations: chocolate cyst-associated bacteria modulate genes involved in lipid metabolism and mismatch repair pathways (ADH1B, MYOM1, PTGFR, ACLY), while adenomyosis-associated bacteria alter inflammatory and proliferative pathways (CEBPB, SERPINE1, S100A1, CKS1B) and activate MAPK signaling. The dysbiosis is hypothesized to fuel endometriosis pathophysiology through multiple mechanisms including chronic inflammation, immune dysregulation, and altered estrogen metabolism, thereby contributing to the characteristic clinical manifestations of pelvic pain and infertility. These findings establish microbiota composition as a functional contributor to endometriosis pathogenesis beyond classical mechanisms like retrograde menstruation and immune dysfunction, suggesting microbial dysbiosis as a targetable therapeutic pathway.

title: Correction to: The role of purinergic P2X3 receptors and endometriosis-associated hyperalgesia
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting 10–15% of reproductive-age women that causes severe pelvic pain, infertility, and reduced quality of life through multiple interconnected pathological mechanisms. The disease involves aberrant endometrial gene expression, fibrosis, and aberrant signaling pathways including constitutively activated NF-κB, JAK/STAT, and Wnt/β-catenin pathways that collectively drive elevated production of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8) and prostaglandin E2. Enhanced angiogenesis and neuroangiogenesis occur via upregulation of VEGF and nerve growth factor, with increased nerve fiber density and elevated substance P and TRPV1 expression contributing to endometriosis-associated hyperalgesia. Immune dysfunction—characterized by reduced regulatory T cells, impaired pro-resolving mediators (lipoxins, resolvins), and elevated peritoneal macrophages—permits aberrant immune activation and lesion establishment. Progesterone resistance develops through reduced progesterone receptor expression, elevated aromatase driving local estrogen synthesis, and dysregulated microRNAs, rendering conventional progestin therapy less effective. Current treatments including GnRH agonists, progestins, and surgical excision target these pathways, though the corrected article emphasizes the complex, multifactorial nature of endometriosis requiring comprehensive understanding of inflammatory, angiogenic, neurogenic, and immunological mechanisms.

title: The role of purinergic P2X3 receptors and endometriosis-associated hyperalgesia
summary:
# Summary

This study elucidates the purinergic signaling pathway's critical role in endometriosis-associated chronic pain. Extracellular ATP accumulates in endometriotic lesions due to altered expression of the ectonucleotidases CD39 and CD73, which normally degrade ATP. This ATP accumulation activates P2X3 receptors on nerve fibers, triggering sensitization and generation of nociceptive, neuropathic, and inflammatory pain characteristic of the condition. The dysregulated purinergic system also intensifies local inflammation while immune cells proliferate within endometriotic foci, promoting lesion development and perpetuating the disease cycle. Additionally, affected endometrium exhibits reduced oxygen consumption and mitochondrial dysfunction, potentially contributing to aberrant ATP and adenosine signaling that impairs uterine immune defense. These findings identify the purinergic pathway as a key therapeutic target for managing endometriosis-associated hyperalgesia.

title: Microbiome dysbiosis and endometriosis: a systematic scoping review of current literature and knowledge gaps
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is associated with microbiome dysbiosis, with both gut and local microbiota alterations contributing to disease pathogenesis and variability. Fusobacterium nucleatum infection represents a particularly important pathogenic mechanism, promoting larger endometriotic lesions through increased M2 macrophage infiltration, transforming growth factor-β1 production, and transgelin-positive myofibroblast expansion. Multiple bacterial taxa show dysbiotic patterns in endometriosis patients, including elevated abundance of Prevotella, Eubacterium dolichum, Haemophilus, and Phascolarctobacterium species, alongside depletion of beneficial organisms such as Lachnospira and Fusicatenibacter. Broad-spectrum antibiotic treatment reduces endometriotic lesion weight in animal models, supporting the potential therapeutic relevance of targeting pathogenic microbiota. However, significant heterogeneity exists in taxonomic profiles and alpha/beta diversity measurements across studies, reflecting knowledge gaps regarding specific dysbiosis signatures and causal relationships in endometriosis development.

title: Red flags for thoracic endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-aged women, is characterized by the presence of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, most commonly in the pelvic cavity, and is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain that significantly impacts quality of life. The pathogenesis of endometriosis is fundamentally driven by chronic inflammation and dysregulated cytokine production, which together create a pathological microenvironment that promotes disease progression. These inflammatory mechanisms underlie the major clinical manifestations of the disease, making anti-inflammatory approaches potentially important therapeutic targets. The identification of inflammation and cytokine dysregulation as central to endometriosis pathogenesis provides a mechanistic framework for understanding why this condition causes both reproductive dysfunction and pain symptoms. Understanding these key inflammatory pathways is essential for developing more effective diagnostic and therapeutic strategies for this common gynecological disorder.

title: Exploring the mechanism of Zishen Quyu Jiedu formula in treating endometriosis based on network pharmacology and experimental verification
summary:
# Summary

Oxidative stress and inflammation are central pathogenic mechanisms in endometriosis, with excessive reactive oxygen species activating the nuclear factor kappa B signaling pathway to promote pro-inflammatory cytokines including TNF-α, IL-6, and IL-1β. The Zishen Quyu Jiedu formula (ZSQYJDF), a traditional Chinese medicine formulation, treats endometriosis by upregulating the NRF2 antioxidant pathway, which in turn increases expression of detoxifying enzymes heme oxygenase-1 and NAD(P)H quinone dehydrogenase 1 while simultaneously inhibiting NF-κB signaling. Network pharmacology and experimental validation identified seven core therapeutic targets (AKT1, IL1B, TNF, TP53, NRF2, IL6, and ESR1) primarily functioning through NRF2, TNF, and HIF signaling pathways. In vivo studies confirmed that ZSQYJDF significantly reduced inflammatory cell infiltration, pro-inflammatory cytokines, and oxidative stress markers in ectopic endometrial lesions while enhancing antioxidant enzyme expression. This study establishes a mechanistic link between NRF2-mediated antioxidant activation and therapeutic efficacy, offering a novel approach to endometriosis treatment targeting the oxidative stress-inflammation cycle.

title: Serum Galectin-1 as a Diagnostic Biomarker in Endometriosis: A Prospective Longitudinal Study
summary: Serum galectin-1 (Gal-1) emerges as a promising diagnostic biomarker for endometriosis through its central role in regulating angiogenesis, immune tolerance, and fibrosis—key pathological mechanisms underlying the disease. This prospective study demonstrates that preoperative serum Gal-1 levels are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients, particularly in moderate-severe (Stage III–IV) disease, and originate from stromal and endothelial cells within both ectopic lesions and eutopic endometrium. Mechanistically, neuropeptides such as corticotropin-releasing hormone and urocortin induce Gal-1 overexpression in ectopic tissue, where the protein promotes lesion angiogenesis and vascular expansion independently of classical VEGF signaling; genetic deletion or antibody neutralization of Gal-1 reduces lesion size and vascularity. Longitudinally, serum Gal-1 levels decreased immediately following surgical intervention but rebounded above baseline at one-year follow-up, suggesting potential utility in monitoring disease recurrence. Notably, serum Gal-1 showed no significant correlation with endometriosis-associated pain symptoms, indicating it functions as a biomarker of disease presence and severity rather than pain phenotype. Integration of Gal-1 with complementary galectins (Gal-3 for fibrosis and Gal-9 for immune checkpoints) may further enhance diagnostic accuracy for endometriosis detection.

title: Impact of the lipid–inflammation axis on endometriosis risk: a multicenter case–control study using mediation analysis
summary:
# Summary

This multicenter case-control study reveals that dyslipidemia significantly increases endometriosis risk primarily through systemic inflammation, with the systemic immune-inflammation index (SII) mediating 88-103% of the lipid-related effects. Women with endometriosis demonstrated characteristic lipid abnormalities including lower high-density lipoprotein cholesterol (HDL-C), elevated triglycerides, and increased non-HDL-cholesterol to HDL-cholesterol ratio (NHHR), alongside elevated inflammatory markers including elevated neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio and SII. Each 1 mmol/L decrease in HDL-C increased endometriosis risk by 55%, while each 1 mmol/L increase in triglycerides and each one-unit increase in NHHR raised risk by 21% and 54%, respectively, with NHHR proving the most consistent risk marker across all subgroups. The lipid-inflammation axis operates through well-defined mechanisms whereby HDL-C suppresses inflammatory cell counts (particularly neutrophils), while triglycerides and elevated NHHR activate immune-inflammatory responses. These findings establish the lipid-inflammation axis as a pivotal mechanistic pathway in endometriosis pathogenesis, suggesting that lipid metabolism disruptions drive chronic systemic inflammation that facilitates disease development.

title: A Multifunctional Molecular Probe for Multimodal Imaging‐Guided Potent Photothermal/Photodynamic Therapy of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study presents cRGD-ILD, a multifunctional molecular probe designed to treat endometriosis through targeted imaging and therapy. The probe combines a cyclic RGD peptide (cRGD) targeting integrin αvβ3 receptors, which are markedly overexpressed on ectopic endometrial tissue and lesion-associated vasculature, with indocyanine green (ICG), a near-infrared photosensitizer. Upon 808 nm laser activation, cRGD-ILD selectively accumulates at endometriosis lesion sites where it generates both cytotoxic reactive oxygen species through photodynamic therapy and localized hyperthermia through photothermal therapy, achieving superior cell death compared to non-targeted controls. The probe simultaneously enables multimodal imaging guidance including magnetic resonance imaging via gadolinium contrast, fluorescence imaging, and photoacoustic imaging for precise lesion localization and therapeutic monitoring. This integrative approach addresses endometriosis-associated symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility with minimal collateral damage to surrounding healthy tissue, representing a promising advancement in targeted theranostic treatment of this prevalent gynecological disorder.

title: Extracellular Trap-Related Genes as Potential Diagnostic Biomarkers for Endometriosis
summary: Neutrophil extracellular traps (NETs) play a significant role in endometriosis pathogenesis by promoting disease progression through alteration of the local immune microenvironment and inflammatory regulation. This study identified four NET-related genes—CEACAM1, FOS, PLA2G2A, and THBS1—as core diagnostic biomarkers that are substantially upregulated in ectopic endometrial tissue and strongly associated with immune cell infiltration. Gene ontology and pathway analysis revealed that these differentially expressed NET-related genes (DE-NETRGs) are enriched in biological processes including wound healing, leukocyte migration, coagulation, and key immune signaling pathways such as IL-17, PI3K-Akt, TNF, and AGE-RAGE. A four-gene diagnostic model integrating machine learning algorithms (Stepglm backward and Random Forest) achieved exceptional performance with an AUC of 0.962 in the training dataset and demonstrated robust generalizability across three independent validation cohorts. These findings establish NET-related gene signatures as promising diagnostic biomarkers for endometriosis, with potential applications in early detection and development of targeted therapeutic strategies. The association between these biomarkers and immune infiltration may significantly improve diagnostic accuracy and clinical management of this disease.

title: Exploring the impact of dietary theobromine on endometriosis risk: Evidence from Mendelian randomization and NHANES data
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disorder affecting approximately 176 million women globally, characterized by elevated oxidative stress and both localized and systemic inflammation that drives lesion development through impaired endometrial cell apoptosis and enhanced proliferation. This study used Mendelian randomization and NHANES observational data to investigate whether dietary theobromine, a methylxanthine compound found in cocoa and chocolate, affects endometriosis risk. The findings reveal that theobromine exerts a protective effect against endometriosis through its potent anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, with each 1 microgram increase in dietary theobromine intake associated with approximately a 1.49-year delay in endometriosis diagnosis age. Mechanistically, theobromine suppresses pro-inflammatory pathways and promotes apoptosis through signaling cascade modulation, counteracting the inflammatory milieu that perpetuates endometrial ectopia. The causal analysis demonstrated a significant inverse association between theobromine exposure and endometriosis risk, suggesting that increased dietary intake of theobromine-rich foods may reduce endometriosis development risk. These findings represent the first investigation of dietary theobromine's relationship with endometriosis and suggest a potentially modifiable dietary intervention for endometriosis prevention or delayed onset.

title: Red blood cell indices as predictor for severity of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Red blood cell (RBC) indices serve as predictive markers for endometriosis severity, with lower hemoglobin, hematocrit, mean corpuscular volume, and mean corpuscular hemoglobin independently associated with advanced stage III–IV disease, while elevated red cell distribution width (RDW) specifically correlates with severe endometriosis. The pathophysiology involves dysregulated iron metabolism and chronic systemic inflammation: iron overload promotes endometrial cell growth, neovascularization, and oxidative stress, while iron deficiency impairs hematopoiesis and exacerbates heavy menstrual bleeding characteristic of the disease. Chronic inflammation in endometriosis reduces RBC lifespan, impairs erythropoietin response, and increases circulating inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8) and reactive oxygen species, creating a feedback loop where elevated RDW promotes RBC membrane deformability that impairs microcirculation, further sustaining oxidative stress and inflammation. These interconnected mechanisms explain the frequent clinical presentations of anemia, fatigue, weakness, and chronic pelvic pain in endometriosis patients and suggest that RBC indices could serve as accessible, cost-effective biomarkers for non-invasive assessment of disease severity.

title: Exploring the Early Endometrial–Blastocyst Interactome in Endometriosis: An Integrative Study
summary: This integrative study reveals that endometriosis fundamentally disrupts the molecular dialogue between blastocysts and the receptive endometrium, compromising implantation success. Endometriosis triggers aberrant endometrial gene expression, including upregulation of 3,831 genes and downregulation of 3,420 genes, leading to exacerbated inflammatory pathways, enhanced cell death signaling, and dysregulated stress responses. The condition causes excessive activation of NK cells, CD4+ T cells, and CD8+ T cells, which interfere with the delicate embryo-endometrial communication, while estradiol-driven increases in prostaglandin E2 promote progesterone resistance and impaired decidualization. In healthy pregnancies, the blastocyst and endometrium engage in coordinated secretion of regulatory factors that orchestrate tissue remodeling, angiogenesis, and immune regulation; however, endometriosis significantly disrupts this interactome network by altering 30% of tissue remodeling genes and 41% of immune response genes. Consequently, endometriosis patients require substantially more in vitro fertilization attempts to achieve implantation, though pregnancy outcomes improve once implantation is successfully established, indicating that the disease primarily impairs early implantation processes rather than fetal development.

title: Uterine Contractility Changes in Adenomyosis: Evidence from a Systematic Review and Meta-Analysis
summary:
# Summary

Adenomyosis, defined by ectopic endometrial glands and stromal fibroblasts invading the myometrium, is associated with characteristic alterations in uterine contractility patterns that significantly impair fertility. Meta-analytic evidence demonstrates that women with adenomyosis exhibit substantially reduced uterine contraction frequency and fewer antegrade contractions, instead showing abnormal retrograde and hyperfrequent contraction patterns that disrupt normal sperm transport and embryo implantation. These contractile abnormalities stem from multiple mechanisms including ultrastructural modifications in smooth muscle cells, loss of nerve fibers in the junctional zone, altered calcium oscillation patterns, and chronic inflammation mediated by elevated TNF-α and IL-6. The resulting impaired uterine peristalsis, combined with progesterone resistance, chronic endometritis, and reduced endometrial receptivity, collectively contribute to the markedly lower clinical pregnancy rates and higher miscarriage rates observed in adenomyosis patients. Additionally, adenomyosis frequently coexists with endometriosis, and both conditions share the common pathophysiological feature of irregular contractility that promotes disease progression and contributes to the associated clinical symptoms of menorrhagia, dysmenorrhea, and chronic pelvic pain.

title: Endometriosis During Peri-Menopause and Post-Menopause: A Review of the Literature
summary: This literature review examines endometriosis persisting or newly arising in peri-menopausal and post-menopausal women, a population traditionally understudied despite well-established disease mechanisms. The central pathophysiology involves progesterone resistance driven by reduced progesterone receptor-B expression and epigenetic alterations, which fails to suppress local estrogen activity and inflammatory cytokine production. Endometriotic lesions exhibit an altered immune microenvironment with M2-skewed macrophages that produce elevated interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, and tumor necrosis factor-α, collectively promoting angiogenesis, tissue remodeling, and chronic inflammation. After menopause, sustained estrogen production through peripheral aromatization in adipose tissue and upregulated aromatase activity in endometriotic lesions perpetuates disease, with obesity further increasing this risk; unopposed estrogen-based hormone replacement therapy and tamoxifen exposure can reactivate lesions and potentially facilitate malignant transformation. Clinical presentations range from chronic pelvic pain and infertility to gastrointestinal symptoms (diarrhea, constipation, rectal bleeding) and rare extra-pelvic manifestations including diaphragmatic endometriosis causing recurrent pneumothorax. Management approaches include aromatase inhibitors to suppress extra-ovarian estrogen production, though bone mineral density reduction remains a significant concern requiring careful patient selection and monitoring.

title: May serum advanced oxidation protein products levels be diagnostic markers in endometrioma?
summary:
# Summary

This study investigates advanced oxidation protein products (AOPP) as potential diagnostic markers for endometrioma, a cyst-forming type of endometriosis. Serum levels of AOPP, along with calprotectin, CA 125, and CA19-9, were significantly elevated in endometrioma patients compared to controls, with logistic regression analysis confirming strong associations between these biomarkers and disease presence. Mechanistically, AOPP contributes to endometriosis pathogenesis by activating intracellular signaling pathways (ERK, p38 MAPK, and NF-κB) that promote endometrial epithelial cell proliferation and migration while inhibiting apoptosis. Among the tested biomarkers, CA 125 demonstrated the highest diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 0.864), followed by AOPP (AUC = 0.732), suggesting AOPP may serve as a complementary diagnostic tool for endometrioma detection. AOPP levels correlate with disease severity and were elevated in peritoneal and follicular fluid of women with deep infiltrative endometriosis and infertility, positioning oxidative stress as a key pathogenic mechanism underlying endometriosis development.

title: Integrating inflammatory biomarkers and demographic variables with machine learning to predict endometriosis risk
summary: This study developed a machine learning predictive model for endometriosis risk by integrating inflammatory biomarkers and demographic variables. Key inflammatory mediators elevated in endometriosis include IL-6, MCP-1, and interferon-gamma, which drive immune cell recruitment and activation of pathways promoting cell migration and angiogenesis. Circulating monocytes migrate to the endometrium where they differentiate into macrophages that secrete matrix metalloproteinases (MMP-12, MMP-9, MMP-14) and facilitate tissue remodeling, while stromal cells release thrombin and thromboxane to activate platelets. Demographic and hematological factors associated with endometriosis risk include older age, non-Hispanic White ethnicity, higher income, elevated monocyte percentage, elevated CRP and CAR, and increased platelet levels, whereas Mexican Americans showed lower prevalence. The XGBoost machine learning model achieved superior classification performance with an AUC of 0.87, outperforming Random Forest, K-Nearest Neighbors, Support Vector Machine, and Logistic Regression approaches. These findings demonstrate that integrating accessible demographic and inflammatory biomarkers can effectively predict endometriosis risk through advanced machine learning techniques.

title: Luminescent mouse model of endometriosis: three-dimensional morphology of lesions and cytokine profiles
summary: This study used a luminescent mouse model of endometriosis to characterize the inflammatory cytokine profiles driving lesion development and progression. Endometriosis, which causes pelvic pain and infertility in approximately 30-50% of affected women, results from the combined effects of genetic, immunological, hormonal, and environmental factors. The research demonstrated that pro-inflammatory cytokines—particularly IL-2, IL-6, TNF-α, IFN-γ, IL-1β, and IL-12p70—are significantly elevated in endometriosis and show strong positive correlations with lesion growth, with IL-6 and IL-2 displaying the strongest associations. Estradiol (E2) promotes both endometrial proliferation and angiogenesis through VEGF expression, facilitating rapid lesion expansion. The anti-inflammatory cytokine IL-10 suppresses pro-inflammatory cytokine production but shows a paradoxical pattern of initial elevation followed by decline, indicating an inflammatory imbalance. These findings establish that dysregulated cytokine signaling is a central mechanism in endometriotic lesion establishment, progression, and symptom severity, supporting the inflammatory basis of the disease.

title: Curcumol alleviates endometriosis via correcting the aberrant activation of Fas/RIPK1-mediated PANoptosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is characterized by a pathological interplay of apoptosis resistance, aberrant inflammatory cell death, and chronic inflammation. Ectopic endometrial lesions exhibit significantly elevated expression of Fas and RIPK1, which aberrantly activate a PANoptosis pathway involving Fas/FADD/Caspase-8 signaling, creating a vicious death-inflammation cycle mediated by NLRP3 inflammasome activation and macrophage recruitment. Curcumol, the active component of Curcuma zedoaria, alleviates endometriosis by directly binding to Fas and RIPK1 at their active sites, thereby inhibiting the Fas/FADD/Caspase-8/RIPK1 signaling axis while simultaneously suppressing JAK2/STAT3-mediated inflammatory cytokine production and promoting apoptosis through IGF-1R and p38 MAPK pathways. Additionally, curcumol triggers autophagic cell death via JNK signaling and programmed necrosis through Sirt1-mediated Atg5 deacetylation, collectively reducing lesion size and attenuating disease progression. These findings establish curcumol as a promising therapeutic agent that addresses multiple pathogenic drivers of endometriosis by correcting dysregulated cell death and suppressing the inflammatory cascade perpetuating ectopic lesion persistence.

title: Adenomyosis in patients with rheumatic diseases: a cross-disciplinary clinical observation
summary:
# Summary

Patients with rheumatic diseases (RDs) exhibit significantly elevated rates of adenomyosis, with a 2.81-fold increased odds compared to controls, alongside higher prevalence of heavy menstrual bleeding, dysmenorrhea, and abnormal uterine bleeding. Adenomyosis, characterized by abnormal endometrial tissue infiltration into the myometrium, shares pathophysiological mechanisms with endometriosis and RDs, including immune dysregulation, inflammation, and hormonal abnormalities such as elevated estrogen receptors and progesterone resistance. The underlying mechanisms linking RDs to adenomyosis involve systemic and local immune dysregulation that amplifies local inflammation, epithelial-mesenchymal transition facilitating endometrial cell migration, and dysregulated estrogen and progesterone signaling that modulates lymphocyte activity and cytokine production. Neurogenic inflammation may additionally contribute to exacerbated gynecological pain in RD patients. These menstruation-related disorders significantly impair quality of life, fertility, and sexual function in affected women. The diagnosis of adenomyosis has been enabled by transvaginal ultrasound and magnetic resonance imaging, facilitating better recognition of this condition in the RD population.

title: Association between the neutrophil percentage-to-albumin ratio and pelvic adhesion severity in endometriosis: A retrospective cross-sectional study
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that commonly leads to pelvic adhesions, a major cause of infertility and ectopic pregnancy. The chronic inflammatory state in endometriosis activates neutrophils, which infiltrate the peritoneal environment and release pro-fibrotic factors including reactive oxygen species, matrix metalloproteinases, and neutrophil extracellular traps that stimulate fibroblasts to deposit excessive collagen, thereby initiating adhesion formation. This study demonstrates that the neutrophil percentage-to-albumin ratio (NPAR) is an independent predictor of moderate-to-severe pelvic adhesions, with the risk increasing by 82% for every 10-unit increase in NPAR, and shows superior predictive ability compared to the classical marker CA125. Elevated NPAR reflects both elevated systemic inflammation markers (neutrophil count, C-reactive protein, fibrinogen, CA125, and neutrophil-to-lymphocyte ratio) and reduced albumin levels, which normally maintains osmotic pressure and scavenges free radicals but becomes depleted during chronic inflammation, exacerbating tissue edema and oxidative stress. As an easily accessible blood-based biomarker, NPAR can be used for preoperative risk stratification to guide surgical planning and individualized treatment in endometriosis patients with adhesions.

title: Hormonal milieu influences whole-brain structural dynamics across the menstrual cycle using dense sampling in multiple individuals
summary:
# Summary

Gonadal hormones dynamically regulate whole-brain structural organization across the menstrual cycle through widespread receptor distribution in limbic and cortical regions including the thalamus, hippocampus, amygdala, hypothalamus, and cerebral cortex. Estradiol and progesterone orchestrate critical neurobiological processes including synaptogenesis, myelination, and dendritic spine density modulation, ultimately influencing cognition, memory, stress responsiveness, and mood regulation. In endometriosis, hormonal dysregulation manifests as elevated estradiol synthesis with decreased inactivation and progesterone resistance in ectopic lesions, resulting in a significantly lower progesterone-to-estradiol ratio compared to typical cycles. This altered hormonal milieu associates with distinctive brain volume changes throughout multiple spatiotemporal patterns, with the progesterone-to-estradiol ratio explaining nearly half the variance in brain structural dynamics, particularly in the cerebellum, precuneus, and frontal regions. The clinical manifestations of endometriosis—chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility—likely reflect both ectopic growth of endometrial tissue and centrally mediated effects of pathological hormone signaling on brain function. These findings establish a mechanistic link between the unique hormonal profile of endometriosis and potential neurobiological contributions to the condition's diverse symptomatology.

title: Metabolic reprogramming and M2 macrophage depletion define the microenvironment of adenomyosis
summary: Adenomyosis is characterized by chronic inflammation, metabolic dysregulation, and a distinctive immune microenvironment centered on M2 macrophage depletion and keratan sulfate biosynthesis dysfunction. Four key genes involved in keratan sulfate production—CHST1, CHST6, B4GALT1, and B3GNT2—are significantly upregulated in the eutopic endometrium, and their dysregulation is coupled with marked reduction of anti-inflammatory M2 macrophages that normally support tissue remodeling. The adenomyotic microenvironment exhibits elevated arachidonic acid metabolism driving prostaglandin synthesis, alongside suppression of critical M2 polarization pathways including TGF-beta and macrophage stimulating protein signaling, which collectively promote chronic symptoms including dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, and infertility. Metabolic reprogramming in adenomyosis involves reduced serum levels of protective metabolites (3-hydroxybutyrate, glutamate, serine) and elevated myometrial metabolites related to oxidative stress, inflammation, and cell proliferation. Macrophage interactions with endometrial cells trigger epithelial-mesenchymal transition and fibrosis formation through direct cell contact and cytokine secretion, establishing an immune-metabolic crosstalk that sustains disease pathogenesis. The identified keratan sulfate biosynthesis genes emerge as promising biomarkers and therapeutic targets for adenomyosis intervention.

title: Formulation of Paeonia lactiflora root extract can induce atrophy of endometriotic lesions and accelerate embryo implantation following in vitro fertilization in endometriosis: An experimental study
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by inflammation and angiogenesis within ectopic lesions, leading to reduced embryo implantation rates and infertility through multiple reproductive dysfunctions including impaired oocyte maturation and altered embryo cleavage. The disease involves elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines tumor necrosis factor-alpha (TNF-α) and vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF), alongside decreased leukemia inhibitory factor (LIF) gene expression in the endometrium, which is critical for successful embryo implantation. Paeonia lactiflora root extract, containing bioactive compounds including saponins, tannins, flavonoids, and fatty acids, exerts potent anti-inflammatory and anti-angiogenic effects by suppressing production of prostaglandin E2, leukotriene B4, and nitric oxide while modulating lymphocyte proliferation and T helper/T suppressor differentiation. In an experimental murine model of endometriosis, Paeonia lactiflora administration significantly reduced lesion diameter, TNF-α and VEGF levels, while upregulating endometrial LIF gene expression. Consequently, mice treated with Paeonia lactiflora extract demonstrated markedly improved embryo implantation rates following in vitro fertilization, suggesting this herbal formulation may represent a novel therapeutic approach to enhance reproductive outcomes in endometriosis patients undergoing assisted reproductive technologies.

title: Educational Case: Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue implantation outside the uterus, commonly in the ovaries, peritoneum, bowel, and vagina. The condition arises through multiple proposed mechanisms including retrograde menstruation, coelomic metaplasia, lymphatic spread, and involvement of bone marrow stem cells. Clinically, endometriosis causes dysmenorrhea, abnormal uterine bleeding, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility, with severity ranging from superficial peritoneal lesions to deep infiltrating endometriosis involving lesions exceeding 5 mm in depth. Pathogenesis involves complex inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms wherein endometriotic implants release proinflammatory cytokines (interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, tumor necrosis factor-alpha) and angiogenic factors (vascular endothelial growth factor, prostaglandin E2) that promote lesion survival through local estrogen production via aromatase activity and enhanced infiltration through matrix metalloprotease activity. Molecular alterations in genes such as KRAS, PIK3CA, ARID1A, and PPP2R1A drive pathogenesis, and genetic predisposition is evident from familial clustering, with HLA-B7 association contributing to immune dysfunction through inhibition of natural killer lymphocyte cytotoxicity. Notably, endometriosis carries a significant malignant potential, with strong associations to ovarian clear cell and endometrioid carcinomas, sometimes sharing identical mutations with concurrent malignancies.

title: Gene Polymorphisms Determining Sex Hormone-Binding Globulin Levels and Endometriosis Risk
summary: Endometriosis is a hormone-dependent disease whose development is fundamentally influenced by sex hormone bioavailability, which is regulated by sex hormone-binding globulin (SHBG). SHBG binds and transports a substantial portion of circulating androgens and estrogens, thereby controlling their free bioactive levels—a mechanism critical to endometriosis pathogenesis. This study identified seven single nucleotide polymorphisms (SNPs) affecting SHBG levels that associate with endometriosis risk, including variants in ZBTB10, PRMT6, GCKR, PPP1R21, BAIAP2L1, JMJD1C, and NR2F2, which interact synergistically to increase disease susceptibility. The lead polymorphism rs440837 in ZBTB10 acts as an independent risk factor and modulates transcription factor binding, affecting the TGF-beta signaling pathway—a key pathway regulating apoptosis, angiogenesis, extracellular matrix remodeling, and cell cycle control. Network analysis revealed that endometriosis-associated protein interactions involve hub proteins including MYC, POU3F2, and CTCF, which participate in more than 15 biological pathways governing endocrine development, gene transcription, and cell proliferation. These findings establish a genetic and molecular framework linking SHBG-regulating variants to endometriosis pathogenesis through hormone bioavailability and inflammatory signaling mechanisms.

title: Association of Primate Veterinarians Guidelines for Weight Management in Research Nonhuman Primates
journal: Journal of the American Association for Laboratory Animal Science : JAALAS
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age that causes infertility and pelvic pain through multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms. The disease is characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth outside the uterus, which promotes elevation of pro-inflammatory cytokines including IL-6 and TNF-α, along with increased prostaglandin (PGE2) production that directly drives pain and inflammation. Local estrogen synthesis via aberrantly expressed aromatase in endometriotic lesions supports ectopic endometrial survival and lesion growth, while elevated vascular endothelial growth factor (VEGF) promotes angiogenesis critical for establishing ectopic tissue. Neurogenesis and increased nerve fiber density within lesions, facilitated by neurotrophic factors, contribute significantly to endometriosis-associated pain pathology. The NF-κB signaling pathway is constitutively activated in lesions, driving pro-inflammatory cytokine expression, and oxidative stress with impaired antioxidant defenses further perpetuates inflammation. Current hormonal treatments including progestins and GnRH agonists have limited efficacy and significant adverse effects, underscoring the need for targeted therapies addressing these underlying inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms.

title: Kisspeptins inhibit ectopic endometrial cell invasion and angiogenesis by suppressing PI3K/AKT signaling pathway via CREB5 in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that kisspeptins, encoded by the KISS1 gene, exert potent anti-invasive and anti-angiogenic effects in endometriosis by suppressing the PI3K/AKT signaling pathway through CREB5-mediated transcriptional regulation. In endometriosis mouse models and ectopic endometrial cell cultures, kisspeptin-10 treatment significantly reduced cell invasion and angiogenesis while decreasing expression of pro-angiogenic factors MMP9 and VEGF. The mechanism involves CREB5 downregulation of PI3K and AKT phosphorylation, with KISS1R receptor expression being substantially reduced in both eutopic and ectopic endometrium of diseased mice compared to controls. These findings establish kisspeptins as a novel therapeutic target for endometriosis-related symptoms including dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility by inhibiting the critical pathological processes of ectopic endometrial cell invasion and neovascularization.

title: An integrated multimodal approach to drug repurposing in endometriosis, using ROR1 as a target
summary: Endometriosis, a chronic condition characterized by endometrial-like tissue growth outside the uterus, involves complex immune, inflammatory, and fibrotic processes that contribute to chronic pelvic pain and infertility. This study identifies ROR1, a receptor tyrosine kinase-like protein, as significantly upregulated across multiple endometriosis lesion types including deep infiltrating and superficial peritoneal lesions, where it transduces non-canonical Wnt5a signaling to promote cell proliferation, migration, and survival. Through a multimodal drug repurposing approach using computational target prediction, rimegepant, a clinically approved calcitonin gene-related peptide antagonist, was identified as a ROR1-targeting compound that significantly reduced viability in endometriosis-derived cell models, with patient-derived organoids showing concentration-dependent antiproliferative responses. While two other candidates, cabergoline and pirenzepine, showed no antiproliferative effects, rimegepant represents the first evidence of a non-hormonal, non-surgical therapeutic candidate targeting ROR1 in human-derived endometriosis models. This finding is particularly significant given that current standard treatments rely heavily on hormonal suppression, surgery, or analgesia, offering a potential novel therapeutic avenue for patients with treatment-resistant disease.

title: Genetic and stem cell insights into the pathogenesis of endometriosis: A comprehensive review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory, estrogen-dependent disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women and is associated with multiple debilitating symptoms including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. The pathogenesis involves complex interactions between genetic factors (chromosomal regions 10q26 and 7p15.2, genes including WNT4, VEZT, GREB1, ESR2, and TGFβ1), hormonal dysregulation (elevated estrogen signaling through overexpressed ESR2 and reduced progesterone-mediated anti-inflammatory effects), and inflammatory mechanisms characterized by angiogenesis, lymphangiogenesis, and neurogenesis. Key molecular pathways include TGFβ1-mediated promotion of vascular endothelial growth factor expression under hypoxic conditions, driving neo-angiogenesis in ectopic lesions, along with altered immune responses linked to polymorphisms in TGFβ and IL2RB genes. Endometrial and bone marrow stem cells contribute to disease pathophysiology, potentially serving as sources for ectopic lesion development through their capacity to differentiate and migrate. Environmental and dietary factors, including cigarette smoke and high red meat consumption, increase disease risk, while omega-3 fatty acids demonstrate protective effects. The distinction between superficial and deep endometriosis phenotypes reveals that deep disease predominantly associates with pain and infertility, reflecting differences in tissue invasion and inflammatory burden.

title: The Nexus of Iron, Senescence, and Fibrosis in Endometriosis: A Narrative Review
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by the pathological presence of endometrium-like tissue in the peritoneal cavity and ovaries, causing pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility. This review identifies a critical mechanistic triad linking cellular senescence, iron overload, and fibrosis in endometriosis pathogenesis. Iron accumulation from erythrocyte breakdown during retrograde menstruation catalyzes reactive oxygen species formation, driving oxidative stress that induces both cellular senescence and fibrosis, while senescent cells perpetuate inflammation through their pro-inflammatory secretory phenotype. Fibrotic tissue further disrupts iron homeostasis, creating a self-sustaining cycle of oxidative stress and cellular dysfunction that mirrors "inflammaging" and contributes to pain, adhesions, and organ dysfunction. The endometrium exhibits accelerated molecular aging with dysregulated signaling pathways (VEGF, Sonic Hedgehog) and impaired stromal cell decidualization, mechanisms shared with age-related reproductive decline and implantation failure. These interconnected pathways suggest that targeting iron metabolism, senescence, or fibrosis may offer novel therapeutic approaches to endometriosis management.

title: Genetic and Epigenetic Components in the Pathogenesis of Adenomyosis and Endometriosis in Adolescents
summary:
# Summary

This review examines the genetic and epigenetic foundations of endometriosis and adenomyosis in adolescents, emphasizing their shared pathogenic mechanisms. Key susceptibility loci including WNT4, ESR1, VEZT, and FSHB converge on pathways controlling estrogen sensitivity, inflammatory activation, and epithelial-mesenchymal interactions, with heritability estimates suggesting approximately 50% of disease variance is genetically determined. Epigenetic modifications—particularly DNA methylation, histone remodeling, and microRNA alterations—occur during puberty's heightened plasticity, altering expression of genes regulating estrogen signaling, progesterone responsiveness, and inflammation that collectively promote endometriotic lesion establishment. Clinical risk factors including early menarche, short menstrual cycles, prolonged bleeding, nulliparity, and unopposed estrogen exposure during anovulatory adolescent cycles increase retrograde menstruation and peritoneal inflammation, facilitating disease manifestation in genetically predisposed individuals. Familial aggregation studies demonstrate markedly elevated endometriosis risk among first-degree relatives and higher concordance in monozygotic versus dizygotic twins, with familial cases exhibiting earlier symptom onset and more severe phenotypes including deep infiltrating and multifocal disease.

title: From Inflammation to Malignancy: The Link Between Endometriosis and Gynecological Cancers
summary:
# Endometriosis and Gynecological Cancer: Inflammatory Pathways to Malignancy

Endometriosis, a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease, is associated with significantly elevated risk of multiple gynecological cancers, particularly endometrioid and clear cell ovarian cancers and endometrial cancer, with women affected by the disease showing a 4.2-fold higher ovarian cancer risk compared to unaffected women. The pathophysiological link between endometriosis and malignancy involves dysregulated estrogen signaling through estrogen receptor beta (ERβ), which promotes chronic inflammation by inducing cytokine and prostaglandin release, resulting in oxidative DNA damage and impaired cellular repair mechanisms. Key signaling pathways implicated in malignant transformation include PI3K/AKT/mTOR and ARID1A alterations, while defective immune surveillance—characterized by impaired peritoneal macrophage function, reduced natural killer cell-mediated cytotoxicity, and elevated pro-inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6) in peritoneal fluid—allows both ectopic implantation and accumulation of genetically unstable cells. Progesterone resistance in endometriotic stromal cells, combined with increased local estradiol synthesis from elevated steroidogenic enzyme expression, sustains the chronic inflammatory microenvironment that promotes angiogenesis and neurogenesis in ectopic lesions. Biomarkers such as Cancer Antigen-125 and Human Epididymal Protein 4 are used for endometriosis detection, while understanding these inflammatory and molecular mechanisms offers therapeutic targets to reduce both endometriosis-related morbidity and cancer progression risk.

title: Coexistence of gynecological pathology with endometriosis and adenomyosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis and adenomyosis are chronic benign gynecological conditions with distinct pathogenic origins—endometriosis arising from functional endometrium via retrograde menstruation, and adenomyosis from basal endometrium through local invasion and cellular proliferation—yet they frequently coexist with multiple other pathologies including leiomyomas, endometrial polyps, and endometrial hyperplasia. Both conditions share a common pathophysiological mechanism centered on hyperestrogenism and aberrant angiogenesis, with local aromatase overexpression in endometriotic and fibroid tissue driving increased estrogen concentrations that perpetuate proliferation and inflammation. The study found that leiomyomas were the most common coexisting pathology (43.3% with endometriosis, 67.3% with adenomyosis), while endometrial cancer risk was significantly elevated in adenomyosis patients (9.3%) compared to control groups. Critically, endometriosis and adenomyosis confer substantially increased malignant transformation risk through prolonged estrogen exposure, genetic defects, immune dysregulation, and oxidative stress, with women harboring endometriosis demonstrating a 4.2-fold increased ovarian cancer risk and doubled risk of endometrioid carcinoma. These findings underscore the importance of comprehensive gynecological evaluation in patients presenting with endometriosis or adenomyosis, as the concurrent presence of multiple pathologies and potential malignant transformation significantly impacts clinical management and patient outcomes.

title: Single-cell and spatially resolved omics reveal transcriptional and metabolic signatures of ovarian endometriomas
summary: Using single-cell and spatially resolved omics, this study reveals that ovarian endometriomas are characterized by distinct cellular and molecular signatures that drive their pathogenesis and malignant potential. The epithelial cells within endometriomas show elevated expression of key markers including XBP1, VCAN, and CLDN7, alongside dysregulated immune-inflammatory mediators and enhanced complement signaling (particularly C3 upregulation), which promotes a pro-inflammatory microenvironment. Cell adhesion pathways, ECM-receptor interactions, and focal adhesion mechanisms are significantly enriched in the endometriotic tissue, with perivascular cells expressing elevated CD44 and markers (RGS5, NOTCH3, PDGFRB) that support tissue remodeling through extracellular matrix pathway activation. Metabolomic analysis reveals altered activity of cytochrome P450 enzymes, lipoprotein particles, and cholesterol metabolism, indicating dysregulated steroid and lipid processing that may amplify estrogen-dependent signaling in these lesions. Notably, the cellular composition shows reduced mesenchymal, endothelial, and myeloid cells while perivascular, epithelial, and T/NK cells are markedly elevated, suggesting a shift toward angiogenic and immune-infiltrated microenvironments. These findings establish ovarian endometriomas as precursor lesions with heightened hormonal sensitivity and well-defined molecular drivers of progression toward clear-cell and endometrioid ovarian carcinomas.

title: Identifying serum microRNAs as biomarkers for endometriosis in adolescents and young adults
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory and estrogen-dependent condition with complex pathophysiology involving retrograde menstruation, hormonal, genetic, epigenetic, and immunologic factors, with macrophage activation playing a key role.
