This study identified serum microRNAs as potential biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis in adolescents and young adults, including six upregulated microRNAs (miR-125b-5p, miR-150-5p, miR-342-3p, miR-451a, miR-3613-5p, and let-7b) and multiple downregulated microRNAs such as miR-17, miR-20a, miR-139-3p, miR-141, and miR-320a. Additional dysregulated microRNAs including elevated miR-501-5p, miR-19b-3p, miR-3940-3p, and miR-1255b-5p, and reduced miR-2116-5p, miR-455-3p, miR-4686, and others were identified as potential diagnostic indicators. Several of these microRNAs, particularly miR-34c-5p, miR-451, miR-204-5p, miR-21-3p, miR-34a-5p, and miR-19b-3p, have established roles in endometrial receptivity and endometriosis pathophysiology. Current endometriosis treatment options include nonsteroidal anti-inflammatory drugs, progestins, combined oral contraceptives, levonorgestrel intrauterine devices, and GnRH agonists and antagonists, though microRNA-based biomarkers offer promise for improved early detection in younger

title: Association Between Remnant Cholesterol and Endometriosis Findings from NHANES 1999–2006
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies elevated remnant cholesterol (RC) as a significant risk factor for endometriosis, establishing a mechanistic link between dyslipidemia and disease pathogenesis. Elevated RC levels promote oxidative stress through lipid peroxidation and reactive oxygen species generation, which activates the NF-κB signaling pathway and triggers a pro-inflammatory cytokine cascade (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-8) that perpetuates chronic peritoneal inflammation. TNF-α upregulates aromatase expression, increasing local estradiol levels, which then stimulates hepatic VLDL assembly in a positive feedback loop that sustains elevated RC. This inflammatory milieu facilitates ectopic endometrial cell adhesion and lesion development through enhanced angiogenesis and matrix remodeling mediated by MMPs and the NLRP3 inflammasome. The analysis of NHANES data (1999–2006) revealed that endometriosis patients had significantly higher RC and total cholesterol levels alongside increased smoking prevalence, with non-Hispanic White women disproportionately affected. Notably, lipid-lowering therapies (simvastatin and ezetimibe) reduced RC levels and suppressed key inflammatory markers (IL-6, malondialdehyde), suggesting a potential therapeutic avenue to mitigate endometriosis risk by targeting cholesterol metabolism.

title: Correction: Promotion of BST2 expression by the transcription factor IRF6 affects the progression of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue outside the uterus, affects 10-15% of reproductive-age women and causes chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and reduced quality of life. The disease involves complex pathophysiological mechanisms including peritoneal inflammation, angiogenesis, and nerve fiber innervation, with elevated cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-8) activating macrophages in the peritoneal fluid. Angiogenesis is critical for lesion survival, driven by elevated VEGF produced by stromal and epithelial cells, while estrogen dysregulation through elevated aromatase expression and altered estrogen receptor signaling promotes progression despite progesterone resistance. Elevated prostaglandins and increased nerve fiber density in lesions contribute to pain symptoms, establishing endometriosis as a multifactorial inflammatory and hormonal disorder requiring multi-targeted therapeutic approaches.

title: Urinary microRNAs for the non-invasive diagnosis of endometriosis identified by next-generation sequencing and machine learning
summary: This study demonstrates that urinary microRNA profiling combined with machine learning offers a promising non-invasive diagnostic approach for endometriosis. Using next-generation sequencing and recursive feature elimination, researchers identified a panel of 20 microRNAs significantly associated with endometriosis, with hsa-miR-183, hsa-miR-500a, hsa-miR-3184-5p, hsa-miR-151b, and hsa-mir-196a-1 among the most relevant biomarkers. Notably, hsa-miR-10400-5p was significantly downregulated in endometriosis patients compared to controls, and reduced expression of this microRNA may promote disease pathophysiology by increasing ARPC1A activity, leading to aberrant actin remodeling and enhanced tissue infiltration. A random forest machine learning model achieved superior diagnostic performance (AUC = 0.91; accuracy and F1-score = 0.81), outperforming traditional logistic regression and support vector machine approaches. These findings suggest that urinary microRNA panels could provide a completely non-invasive alternative to current diagnostic methods like laparoscopy and transvaginal ultrasound for detecting endometriosis, which is a chronic condition causing chronic pelvic pain and infertility in women of reproductive age.

title: The Histamine-Associated Inflammatory Landscape of Endometriosis: Molecular Profiling of HDC, HRH1-HRH4, and Cytokines Across Lesion Subtypes
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that histamine and its receptor signaling constitute a central inflammatory mechanism in endometriosis across all lesion subtypes. Histidine decarboxylase (HDC), the enzyme producing histamine, along with all four histamine receptors (HRH1-HRH4), were markedly elevated in peritoneal, deep infiltrating, and ovarian endometriosis compared to controls, with particularly high HRH3 expression in deep infiltrating lesions. HDC expression strongly correlated with elevated levels of pro-inflammatory and pro-angiogenic factors including IL-6, COX-2, and nerve growth factor (NGF), establishing histamine as a key driver of the inflammatory microenvironment. Histamine stimulates NGF production, which in turn promotes synthesis of neuropeptides like substance P and calcitonin gene-related peptide, establishing a pathway linking histamine signaling to the aberrant innervation characteristic of endometriosis and associated pain symptoms. Histamine receptor expression was localized to epithelial cells, immune cells, and sensory nerve fibers, with robust colocalization of all four receptors with CD45+ immune cells, particularly in ovarian endometriosis. These findings suggest that histamine receptor antagonism, supported by evidence that ketotifen reduces hyperalgesia in animal models, represents a potential therapeutic target for alleviating endometriosis-associated pain.

title: Molecular Imaging Advances in Endometriosis: The Promise of Radiopharmaceuticals
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, steroid hormone-dependent fibro-inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of women of reproductive age, causing dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, chronic pelvic pain, and infertility, often with deep infiltrating lesions affecting the rectum, bladder, and other organs. Pathologically, endometriosis involves extensive tissue remodeling and fibrosis driven by fibroblast activation protein (FAP) overexpression in lesion stroma, which correlates with tissue fibrosis and CD68 macrophage infiltration. Standard PET tracers like [18F]fluorodeoxyglucose and [68Ga]Ga-DOTATATE have shown limited diagnostic utility, with somatostatin receptor expression appearing selective to rectovaginal deep infiltrating lesions but absent in superficial peritoneal and ovarian endometriomas. FAP-targeting radiopharmaceuticals emerge as a promising molecular imaging approach, while integrin-targeting agents like 99mTc-maraciclatide successfully detect all endometriosis subtypes by targeting αvβ3 and αvβ5 integrins upregulated during angiogenesis and inflammation. Estrogen receptor-targeting [18F]FES and hemorrhage-visualization agents like 99mTc-RBC offer complementary strategies, with [18F]FES capable of discriminating scars and adhesions from metabolically active lesions, collectively advancing the molecular imaging arsenal for endometriosis diagnosis and phenotyping.

title: Bidirectional causal relationship between 91 blood cells and endometriosis at different sites: A Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study reveals bidirectional genetic associations between 91 blood cell types and endometriosis across multiple anatomical sites, with distinct site-specific immune signatures. Neutrophil and monocyte perturbation responses emerge as key drivers of endometriosis at the fallopian tube and intestine, while eosinophil responses appear protective against ovarian endometriosis, suggesting differential immune mechanisms by lesion location. Macrophages, differentiated from monocytes, mediate both inflammatory responses and tissue remodeling in ectopic lesions, with elevated TMAO (a bacterial metabolite) and bacterial lipopolysaccharide mimics like Pam3CSK4 amplifying inflammatory pathways through toll-like receptor activation. The study identifies specific blood cell perturbation patterns—such as neutrophil-2/neutrophil-4 ratios and red blood cell responses to environmental perturbants—as genetically associated with intestinal endometriosis risk and demonstrates that certain baseline immune ratios can maintain homeostasis and reduce disease. Notably, anti-inflammatory agents including colchicine and nigericin show potential therapeutic promise by modulating eosinophil and neutrophil responses. These findings establish blood cells as accessible biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis while highlighting the central role of chronic inflammation and immune dysregulation in this complex disease.

title: Pharmacophoric
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease affecting approximately 10% of reproductive-age women, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth that causes debilitating symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain. The pathophysiology of endometriosis involves marked upregulation of aromatase in diseased tissue, enabling local estrogen self-production through a positive feedback cycle—a mechanism distinct from healthy tissue where aromatase is undetectable. While existing treatments such as GnRH analogues and aromatase inhibitors like Letrozole can suppress disease progression, this study used molecular dynamics simulations to design novel isoflavanone-based aromatase inhibitors with improved binding affinity by mimicking the natural substrate androstenedione's hydrophobic interactions at the enzyme's active site. Structure-activity relationship analysis identified optimal pharmacophoric features, including small nonpolar groups at position 1, para geometry on the B ring, and small rather than bulky groups at position 3, which collectively enhance binding affinity. The most promising candidates, compounds 4 and 5, represent new lead molecules that could provide superior aromatase inhibition compared to existing therapies, offering potential therapeutic advances for endometriosis management through more potent suppression of local estrogen biosynthesis.

title: Deconvolution of DNA methylation data reveals cell type-specific epigenomic signatures in endometriosis and eutopic endometrium
summary: This epigenomic study reveals that DNA methylation aberrations and cell type composition distinguish endometriosis lesions from normal eutopic endometrium, providing mechanistic insights into disease pathogenesis. Using deconvolution analysis of methylation data, the researchers identified significant alterations in multiple cell populations: epithelial cells, fibroblasts, and natural killer cells are reduced in endometriosis lesions, while vascular endothelial cells, smooth muscle, heart fibroblasts, adipocytes, and breast basal epithelium are elevated. Key epigenetic regulators including the GATA family and HOXA10 gene show altered methylation patterns that contribute to progesterone resistance and impaired endometrial receptivity, both central features of endometriosis-associated infertility. A novel methylation signature termed "Factor 11" strongly correlates with endometriosis status and reflects the dysregulation of natural killer cells and endothelial cells, suggesting these cell types are critical drivers of the disease's inflammatory and angiogenic phenotype. Beyond infertility and pelvic pain, the study reinforces emerging evidence linking endometriosis to increased risk of endometrioid, clear-cell, and low-grade serous ovarian cancers. These findings highlight the potential of cell-free DNA methylation patterns as non-invasive biomarkers for endometriosis diagnosis and suggest targeting epigenetic modifications and cellular composition as therapeutic approaches.

title: Lateral distribution of endometriotic lesions: the anatomical recesses hypothesis. A systematic review and meta-analysis
summary: This systematic review and meta-analysis demonstrates a striking anatomical asymmetry in endometriotic lesion distribution that varies by anatomical location, challenging current etiological theories. Pelvic lesions show significant left-sided preponderance across multiple sites—ovarian endometriomas (58%), uterosacral ligaments (56%), ureters (71%), and bowel (72%)—while thoracic (98%) and inguinal (92%) lesions predominantly occur on the right side. This location-specific laterality is incompatible with coelomic metaplasia or embryonic cell remnant theories, which would predict symmetrical right-left distribution, but aligns better with the retrograde menstruation hypothesis modified by anatomical and physiological factors. The authors propose an "anatomical recesses hypothesis" wherein protective niches formed by pelvic structures—particularly the rectosigmoid sheltering endometrial fragments refluxed through the left fallopian tube—combined with gravity, peritoneal fluid circulation, and peristalsis preferentially promote lesion implantation on the left hemipelvis. Conversely, upper abdominal and thoracic dissemination follow different pathways influenced by diaphragmatic movements and the thoracoabdominal pump mechanism. The study concludes that endometriosis pathogenesis involves multifactorial mechanisms including genetic, epigenetic, and hormonal influences operating through site-specific anatomical and physiological channels that determine lesion laterality.

title: Burrowing behavior is a potential non-invasive proxy for lesion development in a syngeneic murine model of endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition associated with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue growth in extra-pelvic regions supported by estrogen-dependent mechanisms. In a syngeneic murine model, endometriosis-affected mice demonstrated significantly reduced burrowing activity compared to controls, with lesion-bearing mice showing decreased exploratory behavior and increased thermal pain sensitivity. Burrowing behavior emerged as a sensitive non-invasive proxy for lesion presence, achieving a combined area under the receiver operating characteristic curve of 0.883 and perfect discrimination in the C57BL/6j strain, reflecting how lesion-induced pain alters spontaneous animal behaviors. Ectopic lesions comprised proliferating epithelial cells (Ki67+), blood vessels (CD31+), and macrophage infiltration (F4/80+), consistent with endometriosis diagnosis criteria requiring endometrial glands and stroma. This work demonstrates that burrowing assay provides ethologically valid, non-evoked pain assessment and could serve as a practical alternative to invasive procedures for monitoring lesion development and disease burden in endometriosis models.

title: The diagnostic value of serum inflammation, coagulation and tumor indexes in differentiating stage III and IV ovarian endometriotic cysts
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder strongly associated with pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, with ovarian endometriotic cysts (OEC) promoting fibrosis and adhesion formation through accumulation of hemolyzed blood and hemosiderin. Chronic inflammation in advanced endometriosis activates the coagulation cascade and platelet aggregation, leading to abnormal fibrinolysis that contributes to disease progression. Stage IV OEC is characterized by significantly elevated platelet-to-lymphocyte ratio (PLR), fibrinogen (FIB), and carbohydrate antigen 125 (CA125), alongside decreased lymphocyte counts and elevated platelet counts, with these parameters positively correlating with cyst size, pelvic adhesions, rectouterine pouch closure, and dysmenorrhea severity. Multivariate analysis identified PLR, FIB, and CA125 as independent predictors of stage IV disease, with elevated fibrinogen particularly contributing to adhesion formation through hyperfibrinogenemia. A combined nomogram integrating PLR, FIB, and CA125 achieved superior diagnostic performance (AUC 0.81) for differentiating stage III from stage IV OEC, providing a practical approach to staging advanced endometriosis and guiding surgical management decisions.

title: Immunohistochemical Detection of the Mechano-Gated Piezo Channels in the Normal Endometrium and in Endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is an inflammatory, estrogen-dependent disorder causing chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and infertility through ectopic endometrial tissue invasion that triggers inflammation, angiogenesis, and fibrosis. This study identified Piezo1 and Piezo2 mechano-gated ion channels as important players in endometriosis pathogenesis, with Piezo1 normally present in healthy endometrial glands and significantly elevated across ectopic endometriosis lesions in the ovary, bladder, and vagina. While Piezo2 expression remained largely restricted in normal endometrium, it showed selective upregulation specifically in ovarian and vaginal endometriosis foci. Both Piezo channels function as mechanotransducers involved in multiple pathophysiological processes including cellular migration, inflammation, angiogenesis, fibrosis, and pain sensation, with dysregulation of Piezo1 previously associated with fibrotic disorders, chronic pain syndromes, and inflammatory diseases. The anatomically variable expression patterns of these channels suggest that location-specific mechanical forces and tissue remodeling processes in different endometriosis lesions may differentially engage Piezo-mediated mechanisms. These findings suggest Piezo channels represent novel targets for understanding endometriosis-associated symptoms and potentially developing mechanotransduction-targeting therapeutics.

title: Mucin Biology as a Local Diagnostic and Promising Therapeutic Target in Endometriosis: Expression and Glycosylation Profiling
summary:
# Summary

Multiple mucin proteins, particularly MUC1, MUC2, MUC5AC, MUC6, and MUC16, are consistently upregulated in endometriotic lesions compared to control tissue, with MUC1 showing the most robust elevation across all lesion subtypes including peritoneal, ovarian, and deep infiltrating endometriosis. These heavily O-glycosylated proteins undergo altered glycosylation patterns in endometriosis, including enrichment of core 1 O-glycans (T-antigen) on MUC1, which may enhance adhesive and invasive properties of ectopic endometrial cells. The dysregulated mucin expression and aberrant glycosylation patterns facilitate key pathogenic mechanisms including altered epithelial cell adhesion dynamics, immune evasion through antigenic epitope masking, and lesion persistence, thereby contributing to endometriosis-associated symptoms of chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. While protein levels of MUC6 and MUC16 are elevated in lesion tissue, their transcript levels remain unchanged, suggesting post-translational mechanisms such as enhanced protein stability, altered inflammatory turnover, and modified shedding dynamics regulate their accumulation. These findings identify mucins and their glycosylation patterns as promising diagnostic biomarkers and therapeutic targets for endometriosis management.

title: Copper-Targeted Therapy in Experimental Endometriosis: Effects of Ammonium Tetrathiomolybdate on Markers of the Interconnected Processes of Inflammation, Innervation, and Fibrogenesis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease characterized by elevated copper levels, fibrosis, pelvic pain, and infertility. The study demonstrates that ammonium tetrathiomolybdate (TM), a copper chelator, effectively reduces endometriosis symptoms in a mouse model by suppressing three interconnected pathological processes: inflammation, abnormal nerve fiber growth and pain signaling, and fibrogenesis. TM treatment significantly reduced inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-6, TGF-β) and neurotrophic factors (NGF, BDNF) at both mRNA and protein levels, as well as nociceptive markers including substance P, CGRP, and TRPV1 that contribute to pain perception. Additionally, TM decreased fibrogenic markers including vimentin, α-smooth muscle actin, and collagen deposition in endometriotic lesions. These findings suggest that copper-mediated elevation of neurotrophic factors and pro-inflammatory cytokines drives the interconnected pathophysiology of endometriosis, making copper chelation a promising therapeutic approach to simultaneously address inflammation, innervation, and fibrosis in this debilitating condition.

title: The Fibrinogen-to-Albumin Ratio in Endometriosis: A Step Toward Personalized Non-Invasive Diagnostics
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disease characterized by dysregulated immune responses and elevated systemic inflammation, manifesting clinically as pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and subfertility. The pathophysiology involves elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, TNF-α, IL-1β, IL-8) in peritoneal fluid that suppress natural killer cell cytotoxicity, promote angiogenesis, and activate hepatic acute-phase protein synthesis, resulting in increased fibrinogen and decreased albumin production. The fibrinogen-to-albumin ratio (FAR) emerges as a novel non-invasive biomarker significantly elevated in women with endometriosis compared to controls, reflecting the shift in acute-phase protein production driven by inflammatory signals and the increased vascular permeability associated with chronic inflammation. While FAR does not correlate with disease stage or deep infiltrating endometriosis severity, it captures the systemic inflammatory burden of the disease and may offer diagnostic utility independent of traditional markers like CA-125. This ratio-based approach represents a step toward personalized diagnostics by leveraging the opposing acute-phase response dynamics of fibrinogen and albumin as integrated inflammation indicators. Future validation of FAR in larger cohorts could establish it as a practical, cost-effective screening tool for endometriosis diagnosis.

title: Drospirenone promotes apoptosis in ectopic but inhibits proliferation in eutopic human endometrial stromal cells
summary: This study demonstrates that drospirenone, a fourth-generation progestin with anti-androgenic properties, exerts differential effects on endometrial stromal cells depending on their origin. Drospirenone induces apoptosis in ectopic endometrial stromal cells (EC-ESCs) while inhibiting proliferation in eutopic endometrial stromal cells (EU-ESCs), primarily through activation of the extrinsic apoptotic pathway via upregulation of p53 and PTEN tumor suppressors, coupled with increased caspase-3 and BAX expression and decreased anti-apoptotic BCL2. The drug also reduces cell viability as measured by Ki-67 expression and promotes decidualization of eutopic endometrium, mechanisms that contribute to its therapeutic benefits. Beyond direct cellular effects, drospirenone suppresses key inflammatory mediators (IL-1β, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α) and angiogenic factors (VEGF, NGF) implicated in endometriosis pathogenesis, while suppressing ovulation to induce amenorrhea. These findings support drospirenone's clinical efficacy in treating endometriosis and alleviating associated pain symptoms including dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, and chronic pelvic pain through coordinated anti-inflammatory, anti-angiogenic, and pro-apoptotic mechanisms.

title: Patient-derived epithelial cell organoids mimic the phenotypic complexity of endometriosis subtypes
summary:
# Summary

This study established patient-derived epithelial cell organoids from three endometriosis subtypes—ovarian endometriomas (OMA), deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), and superficial peritoneal endometriosis (SUP)—to model the phenotypic complexity of this chronic inflammatory condition affecting approximately 190 million people and causing chronic pelvic pain, infertility, and reduced quality of life. The organoids demonstrated markedly different establishment success rates (OMA 71.4%, DIE 63.6%, SUP 30%) and distinct morphological characteristics, with OMA organoids being predominantly solid and containing stromal populations, DIE organoids showing mixed morphology, and SUP organoids appearing predominantly cystic. Importantly, progesterone receptor expression was well-retained in SUP and DIE-derived organoids but substantially reduced in OMA organoids, suggesting differential hormone responsiveness among subtypes. Hormonal treatment at tissue collection significantly reduced both organoid establishment success and biospecimen mass, and OMA organoids exhibited lower proliferative capacity and required alternative culture conditions enriched in Wnt3a with reduced FGF compared to other endometriosis-derived organoids. These findings indicate that organoid models faithfully recapitulate the heterogeneity of endometriosis subtypes and provide a powerful platform for investigating disease-specific pathological mechanisms and therapeutic responsiveness.

title: Correction: Endometriosis burden and trends among women of childbearing age from 1990 to 2021
summary: Endometriosis, a debilitating condition affecting women of childbearing age, is strongly associated with infertility and chronic pelvic pain, with significant burden and rising trends documented from 1990 to 2021. The pathophysiology involves elevated inflammatory mediators including TNF-alpha and IL-6 in endometriotic lesions, which promote aberrant cell proliferation and activate the NF-kappa B inflammatory pathway. Angiogenesis and neovascularization are critical to lesion establishment and persistence, driven by prostaglandin E2 and VEGF signaling, alongside increased macrophage infiltration into affected tissue. Enhanced estrogen signaling upregulates aromatase expression, creating a local hormonal microenvironment that perpetuates endometrial cell growth outside the uterus. Additionally, elevated oxidative stress markers characterize endometriotic lesions and likely contribute to the chronic inflammatory state. Together, these interconnected inflammatory, angiogenic, and hormonal mechanisms underscore the complex pathobiology of endometriosis and identify multiple targets for therapeutic intervention.

title: The therapeutic potential of pirfenidone in alleviating fibrosis and restoring ovarian function in a novel ovarian endometriosis mouse model
summary: Ovarian endometriosis (OEMs) is a severe form of endometriosis that causes female infertility primarily through fibrosis-driven pathology, characterized by excessive collagen deposition, elevated α-smooth muscle actin (α-SMA), and myofibroblast activation that disrupts the ovarian follicular microenvironment. OEMs impairs ovarian function by downregulating follicle-stimulating hormone receptor (FSHR) expression, reducing granulosa cell proliferation, and increasing apoptosis, ultimately compromising oocyte quality and folliculogenesis. Pirfenidone, an FDA-approved antifibrotic agent, demonstrates strong therapeutic potential by significantly reducing lesion size, fibrosis burden, and collagen I deposition while restoring ovarian reserve and folliculogenesis markers. Beyond its conventional antifibrotic and anti-inflammatory mechanisms, pirfenidone operates through a novel pathway involving modulation of iron homeostasis and ferroptosis activation, reducing pathological ferric iron deposition in lesions and downregulating extracellular matrix organization pathways. These findings suggest that pirfenidone addresses both the structural fibrotic abnormalities and the underlying inflammatory environment that drives OEMs-associated infertility, offering a mechanistically distinct approach to current surgical and hormonal treatment strategies.

title: Enhanced CD56 Expression and Increased Number of CD56+bright Cells in the Peripheral Blood of Untreated Endometriosis Patients
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that untreated endometriosis patients exhibit elevated peripheral CD56+bright natural killer cells, a subset normally enriched in the endometrium that produces immunosuppressive cytokines (TGF-β and IL-10) but possesses minimal cytotoxic capacity. The elevated CD56+bright cells originate from ectopic endometrial lesions shed during retrograde menstruation and contribute to impaired overall NK cell cytotoxicity, allowing survival of displaced endometrial tissue. A critical mechanism underlying NK cell dysfunction involves endometriotic lesions secreting exosomes carrying NKG2D ligands that downregulate the NKG2D activating receptor on circulating NK cells, further compromising immune surveillance. Notably, surgical removal of ectopic tissue combined with hormonal (progestogen) treatment normalized both CD56+bright cell numbers and NKG2D receptor expression, suggesting that these immunological alterations are reversible upon elimination of the primary lesions. The findings indicate that elevated CD56+bright NK cells could serve as a potential diagnostic biomarker for endometriosis and support exploration of NK cell-based immunotherapies to restore anti-endometriotic immune responses.

title: Global RNA expression analysis of patient samples identified potential diagnostic biomarkers specific for peritoneal, ovarian and deep endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This global RNA expression study identified distinct molecular signatures differentiating peritoneal, ovarian, and deep endometriosis subtypes, establishing potential diagnostic biomarkers for each form of this debilitating gynecological disorder. Endometriosis causes chronic pain, infertility, dysmenorrhea, and dyspareunia through mechanisms involving chronic inflammation, fibrosis, and altered immune cell composition including reduced natural killer cells and increased M2 macrophages. Deep endometriosis showed elevated expression of secreted proteins (ANGPTL7, GDF6, ADIPOQ, BMP5) and the transcription factor NKX2-3, while ovarian endometriosis exhibited distinct upregulation of steroidogenic genes (CYP11A1, CYP17A1, CYP19A1, STAR) and the ciliogenic protein KLHDC8A. Multiple inflammatory mediators including PLA2G2A, PLA2G5, CCL11, and classical serum biomarkers (CA-125, IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, BDNF) were elevated across endometriosis subtypes, with non-canonical Wnt signaling pathway components (SFRP2, TBX18, TCF21) and long non-coding RNAs (MIR202HG, LINC02381, FENDRR) showing subtype-specific upregulation patterns. These findings provide a molecular framework for differential diagnosis and understanding the pathophysiology underlying endometriosis-associated symptoms.

title: Comparative evaluation of ACRS and PRP on inflammation and lesion activity in a rat model of peritoneal endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and pro-angiogenic factors (VEGF, VEGFA) in peritoneal fluid and endometriotic lesions, leading to persistent symptoms including chronic pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility. This comparative study evaluated two autologous biological therapies—Autologous Cytokine-Rich Serum (ACRS) and Platelet-Rich Plasma (PRP)—in a rat peritoneal endometriosis model, finding that both treatments significantly reduced TNF-α and IL-6 expression and decreased histopathological endometriosis grading. However, the mechanisms of action differed: ACRS demonstrated superior anti-inflammatory effects through its high content of IL-1 receptor antagonist (IL-1ra), though it paradoxically increased VEGFA and α-SMA expression, suggesting potential pro-angiogenic and pro-fibrotic activity. PRP, enriched with growth factors including PDGF, VEGF, EGF, TGF-β, and IGF, promoted fibroblast proliferation and extracellular matrix synthesis while also reducing inflammatory markers. These findings suggest that ACRS may be a promising therapeutic option targeting inflammation in endometriosis, though its pro-angiogenic effects warrant further investigation regarding long-term lesion persistence and recurrence.

title: Elevated GPX3 expression in endometriosis: implications for pathogenesis and immune cell infiltration
summary: This study reveals that glutathione peroxidase 3 (GPX3), a secretory antioxidant enzyme, is markedly elevated in ectopic endometrial lesions compared to normal tissue and plays a dual role in endometriosis pathogenesis. While GPX3 protects ectopic cells from oxidative stress by scavenging reactive oxygen species, its high expression paradoxically associates with enhanced immune cell infiltration, particularly CD68+ macrophages and PD-1-positive T cells, through activation of inflammatory signaling pathways including JAK-STAT, chemokine, and cytokine receptor interactions. GPX3 knockdown suppresses reactive oxygen species accumulation and inhibits cell migration capacity, indicating that the enzyme actively supports ectopic cell survival and progression. The elevated GPX3 expression correlates strongly with increased inflammation and cytolytic immune activity, suggesting it functions as an immunomodulatory factor that promotes the peritoneal inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of endometriosis. These findings establish GPX3 as a promising diagnostic biomarker with perfect diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 1.0) and identify it as a potential therapeutic target for reducing both oxidative stress and pathological immune activation in endometriosis.

title: Endometriosis and Oocyte Quality: Morphological Alterations, Developmental Competence, and Modifiable Strategies for Reproductive Longevity
summary: Endometriosis impairs fertility through multiple interconnected mechanisms involving somatic mutations, epigenetic remodeling, immune dysregulation, and aberrant steroid signaling that collectively compromise oocyte quality and endometrial receptivity. The disease causes pelvic anatomical distortion and impaired folliculogenesis, while intrinsic oocyte alterations including abnormal zona pellucida structure, cytoplasmic granularity, vacuolization, and mitochondrial dysfunction are significantly more frequent in affected women. Within the follicular microenvironment, elevated cytokines and reactive oxygen species impair meiotic progression and cytoplasmic maturation by disrupting granulosa cell function. Epigenetic dysregulation involving altered DNA methylation and histone modifications reinforces estrogen dominance and progesterone resistance by hypermethylating progesterone receptor and estrogen receptor-α promoters while hypomethylating estrogen receptor-β, thereby impairing decidualization and endometrial receptivity. Aberrant splicing produces protein isoforms with dominant-negative functions that amplify pro-inflammatory and pro-survival signaling through the TGF-β, PI3K–AKT, and NF-κB pathways, while dysregulation of genes implicated in epithelial-mesenchymal transition and chromatin remodeling (including ARID1A, PIK3CA, ITGB1, and FGF2) sustains the disease phenotype. These findings highlight endometriosis as a multifaceted condition where epigenetic and post-transcriptional alterations create a hostile reproductive microenvironment affecting both oocyte developmental competence and embryo implantation.

title: Therapeutic Effects of Vitamins in Endometriosis Patients: A Systematic Review of Randomized Controlled Trials
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory condition characterized by ectopic endometrial tissue that causes dysmenorrhea, chronic pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and infertility through dysregulated molecular pathways including elevated aromatase-driven estradiol production, progesterone resistance, elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α), and activation of NF-κB and MAPK signaling that promote angiogenesis and cellular adhesion. This systematic review demonstrates that vitamin supplementation offers therapeutic benefit: high-dose vitamin D (50,000 IU) significantly reduces pelvic pain and inflammatory markers (hs-CRP) while increasing total antioxidant capacity, potentially through downregulation of β-catenin in the Wnt/β-catenin pathway; combined vitamin C and E therapy acts synergistically to reduce oxidative stress biomarkers (malondialdehyde and reactive oxygen species) and produces clinically meaningful reductions in pelvic pain (43%), dysmenorrhea (37%), dyspareunia, and daily pain symptoms over eight weeks. These vitamins exert their therapeutic effects through immunomodulatory and antioxidant mechanisms that directly counter the inflammatory and oxidative pathways driving endometriosis pathogenesis, suggesting that micronutrient supplementation represents a viable adjunctive strategy for symptom management in endometriosis patients.

title: Exploring the therapeutic potential of disulfiram in endometriosis: mechanisms targeting inflammation, oxidative stress, pyroptosis, and angiogenesis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological disorder involving multiple interconnected pathogenic mechanisms including inflammation, oxidative stress, pyroptosis, and angiogenesis. The disease is characterized by the growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, leading to pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility, with pathogenesis driven by retrograde menstruation, peritoneal immune dysfunction, and dysregulated angiogenesis. Disulfiram, a repurposed antialcoholism drug, emerges as a promising multitarget therapeutic agent that simultaneously addresses these pathways by inhibiting toll-like receptor and NF-κB signaling, suppressing ROS production and NLRP3 inflammasome activation, blocking GSDMD pore formation to prevent pyroptosis-mediated IL-1β and IL-18 release, and inhibiting angiogenic factors including VEGF. These mechanistic actions collectively reduce pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6, IL-1β), prevent ectopic lesion growth, and enhance cellular antioxidant defenses through modulation of the GSK-3β/Nrf2 axis. The evidence demonstrates that disulfiram's multi-targeted approach simultaneously disrupts the inflammatory cascade, oxidative stress amplification, and pyroptotic cell death that sustain endometriosis pathology, positioning it as a potentially efficacious therapeutic option for this treatment-resistant condition.

title: Identification of a circulating immunological signature as a liquid biopsy approach for the diagnosis of endometriosis
summary: This study identifies a circulating immunological signature as a potential liquid biopsy for endometriosis diagnosis, revealing systemic immune dysregulation as a key disease mechanism. Endometriosis causes pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility while being associated with autoimmune and digestive diseases, with immune dysfunction playing a critical role. The analysis reveals a characteristic immunological pattern including elevated TNF and reduced levels of multiple soluble immune checkpoints (sCD25, sPD-L1, sLAG-3, sCTLA-4, sPD-1, and others) alongside reduced chemokines (TGF-β1, IP-10, MCP-1, IL-8), reflecting a proinflammatory state with impaired T cell reactivity and NK cell cytotoxicity. An eight-factor immunological signature achieves high diagnostic accuracy (AUC 0.8881), while a simplified four-factor minimal signature incorporating sCD25, sPD-L1, sLAG-3, and IP-10 with demographic factors maintains robust performance across age groups, BMI categories, disease subtypes, and infertility status. These findings support soluble immune checkpoints and circulating cytokines as reliable biomarkers for non-invasive endometriosis diagnosis, offering a practical alternative to surgical confirmation.

title: Dydrogesterone and Letrozole Combination for the Treatment of Endometriosis: A Mechanism‐Based Therapeutic Approach
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a complex, estrogen-dependent disease characterized by chronic inflammation and fibrosis that causes pelvic pain and infertility. The dydrogesterone–letrozole combination offers a mechanism-based therapeutic approach by simultaneously targeting estrogen production and progesterone signaling: letrozole inhibits aromatase to suppress localized estrogen biosynthesis in ectopic endometrial tissue, while dydrogesterone activates progesterone receptors to inhibit epithelial proliferation and induce decidualization without suppressing ovulation. This dual therapy effectively reduced lesion volume, suppressed cell proliferation markers (PCNA-positive cells), decreased fibrosis, and reduced inflammatory mediators (TNF-α, IL-1β, and VEGF) more effectively than monotherapy or established treatments. The combination's superior efficacy disrupts multiple pathways driving endometriosis progression, including the estrogen-VEGF-angiogenesis axis and epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, while preserving fertility by maintaining normal ovulation.

title: Mechanisms of Luoshi Neiyi prescription (LSNYP) in endometriosis: a network pharmacology and experimental study
summary: Luoshi Neiyi prescription (LSNYP), a traditional Chinese medicine, effectively treats endometriosis by inhibiting key mechanisms driving ectopic endometrial tissue progression. The compound suppresses cell migration, invasion, and adhesion of endometriotic stromal cells through downregulation of critical target proteins—ICAM1, MMP9, and VEGFA—which are central to the adhesion-invasion-angiogenesis cascade underlying endometriosis pathology. LSNYP also disrupts the inflammation-estradiol cycle, improves hypoxic conditions, and enhances endometrial receptivity, addressing multiple pathogenic pathways simultaneously. Network pharmacology and experimental validation revealed that LSNYP's multi-component formulation stably binds these molecular targets, preventing the tumor-like biological properties characteristic of ectopic endometrial lesions. By targeting inflammatory responses, focal adhesion signaling, and VEGF pathway activation, LSNYP offers a promising alternative therapeutic approach to conventional treatments for endometriosis-associated symptoms including infertility, dysmenorrhea, and chronic pelvic pain.

title: Retraction Note: APEX1/miR-24 axis: a promising therapeutic target in endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition affecting 10-15% of reproductive-age women that causes infertility and chronic pelvic pain through multiple interconnected mechanisms. Ectopic endometrial lesions trigger intense inflammation characterized by elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (TNF-α, IL-6) and reduced anti-inflammatory mediators (IL-10), driven by macrophage polarization toward the pro-inflammatory M1 phenotype and dysregulated immune cell populations including reduced regulatory T cells and natural killer cell activity. The disease involves estrogen-dependent pathways, with increased aromatase expression generating local estrogen that promotes cell proliferation, alongside elevated prostaglandins from COX-2 upregulation that contribute to pain and inflammation. Angiogenesis and aberrant neuroangiogenesis are essential for lesion establishment and progression, while nerve fiber infiltration producing substance P and NGF drives chronic pain symptoms. Current therapeutic strategies including aromatase inhibitors, GnRH agonists, progestins, and NSAIDs target specific pathways, though progesterone resistance limits treatment efficacy. Emerging evidence implicates oxidative stress, fibrosis via TGF-β signaling, epithelial-to-mesenchymal transition, and microRNA dysregulation as additional pathogenic contributors warranting therapeutic investigation.

title: Elevated CHI3L1 and COX-2 levels correlate with menstrual pain severity in endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory gynecological disease associated with severe pelvic pain, and this study identifies chitinase-3-like protein 1 (CHI3L1) and cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) as key mediators of menstrual pain severity. CHI3L1 and COX-2 levels were significantly elevated in endometriotic cyst walls compared to controls, and these markers demonstrated strong positive correlation with each other and with menstrual pain intensity, suggesting they work together in an inflammatory pain pathway. CHI3L1 is secreted by macrophages and neutrophils in response to pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-1, IL-6, IL-13, TNF-α), which are themselves stimulated by local estrogen production, and subsequently activates COX-2 to produce prostaglandin E2, a potent inflammatory mediator. This cascade perpetuates a vicious cycle in which inflammation-induced estrogen production via aromatase activation provides positive feedback, amplifying local hyperestrogenism and chronic inflammation. These findings suggest that CHI3L1 may serve as both a biomarker for endometriosis severity and a potential therapeutic target in managing inflammatory pain associated with this condition.

title: Impact of estetrol and drospirenone combination therapy on alleviation of the pelvic pain of endometriosis: a randomized control trial
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by elevated proinflammatory mediators including prostaglandin E2, nerve growth factor, and interleukins that activate sensory nerves and promote neuroangiogenesis, leading to dysmenorrhea, intermenstrual pelvic pain, and dyspareunia. This randomized controlled trial demonstrated that estetrol combined with drospirenone (E4/DRSP) effectively reduces endometriosis-associated pelvic pain, particularly nonmenstrual pain, with higher responder rates compared to ethinyl estradiol/drospirenone (EE/DRSP), translating to improved quality of life. Mechanistically, estetrol selectively inhibits estrogen-induced invasion and migration of endometrial stromal cells by upregulating estrogen receptor alpha and progesterone receptors while downregulating estrogen receptor beta and GPER-1 signaling. The E4/DRSP combination was as effective as EE/DRSP in reducing severe pelvic pain at 12 weeks while demonstrating a more favorable safety profile, with lower rates of adverse effects including nausea and less impact on hemostasis. These findings suggest that estetrol's selective tissue activity and improved tolerability make it a promising alternative to conventional estrogen-progestin therapies for managing endometriosis-related pain and enhancing patient quality of life.

title: Looking at endometriosis–diagnosis and disease mechanisms through a mechanical lens
summary:
# Summary

This article examines endometriosis through the lens of cellular and tissue mechanics, proposing that abnormal mechanical properties serve as both diagnostic markers and disease drivers. Endometriosis, characterized by ectopic growth of endometrial tissue outside the uterus, causes chronic pain and infertility; notably, cells and tissues from endometriotic lesions—particularly deep infiltrating endometriosis—display significantly elevated stiffness (up to nine times greater than surrounding tissue), heightened contractility, and increased motility compared to healthy tissue. The mechanical microenvironment plays a critical role in pathogenesis: immune dysregulation and chronic inflammation trigger the release of proinflammatory cytokines and matrix metalloproteinases, which remodel the extracellular matrix and progressively stiffen peritoneal and lesional tissues, thereby promoting endometrial cell adhesion and invasive behavior through pathways such as RhoA/ROCK signaling. The authors emphasize that menstrual effluent and mechanical profiling via techniques like atomic force microscopy and transvaginal elastography offer promising non-invasive diagnostic approaches that could complement or improve upon current laparoscopy-based gold-standard diagnosis. This mechanical framework unifies understanding of endometriosis pathogenesis and suggests that biophysical properties of cells may serve as functional biomarkers for disease detection and stratification.

title: 2-Deoxyglucose dendrimer-enabled niclosamide delivery to FRβ-expressing macrophages alleviates endometriosis progression and associated hyperalgesia
summary: Endometriosis progression is driven by dysregulated folate receptor-beta (FRβ)-expressing macrophages that promote chronic inflammation, angiogenesis, and neurogenesis in ectopic lesions. This study demonstrates that niclosamide, an FDA-approved anthelmintic drug, effectively suppresses endometriotic lesion growth and associated pain by inhibiting FRβ+ macrophages and blocking multiple inflammatory pathways including STAT3, NFκB, and WNT signaling. To overcome niclosamide's poor aqueous solubility, the researchers developed a 2-deoxyglucose dendrimer-niclosamide conjugate (FA-2DG-D-Niclo) that selectively targets FRβ+ macrophages through folate receptor-mediated endocytosis, achieving lesion-specific accumulation with minimal off-target effects. A single intraperitoneal dose of FA-2DG-D-Niclo significantly reduced FRβ+ macrophage burden, suppressed lesion number and volume, decreased vascularization and innervation, and markedly improved endometriosis-associated hyperalgesia within two weeks. These findings establish macrophage-targeted nanomedicine as a promising therapeutic strategy for alleviating both the structural lesions and pain symptoms of endometriosis through selective immune modulation.

title: Metabolomics Analysis Reveals Gut Microbiota-Associated Sakuranin Modulates Endometrial Stem Cell Differentiation and Inflammation to Alleviate Pain in Endometriosis
summary: This metabolomics study identifies sakuranin, a gut microbiota-derived metabolite, as a promising therapeutic agent for endometriosis-associated pain through multiple interconnected mechanisms. The research demonstrates that sakuranin levels are significantly reduced in patients with endometriosis pain and show an inverse correlation with pain severity, with high predictive value for pain assessment (AUC=0.8027). At the cellular level, sakuranin suppresses the pathological differentiation and migration of ectopic endometrial stem cells while simultaneously reducing their production of inflammatory cytokines (IL-1β, IL-6, TNF-α) and downregulating pro-angiogenic factors including VEGF and ALCAM. In mouse models of endometriosis, sakuranin administration suppressed ectopic lesion growth, reduced inflammation, modulated angiogenesis, decreased proliferation markers (Ki-67), and regulated both sympathetic and sensory nerve markers associated with pain transmission. The findings underscore the importance of the gut microbiota-endometriosis axis, as dysbiosis-associated alterations in microbial metabolites contribute to disease pathogenesis through estrogen metabolism and immune dysregulation. This work suggests that restoring sakuranin levels through microbiota-targeted interventions could represent a novel therapeutic strategy addressing the multifactorial mechanisms underlying endometriosis pain.

title: Association of endometriosis with hematuria markers: A Mendelian randomization study
summary: This Mendelian randomization study identifies several blood and urine biomarkers associated with endometriosis risk through distinct pathophysiological mechanisms. Elevated triglycerides and calcium emerge as risk factors for endometriosis, with triglycerides promoting inflammation and oxidative stress in endometrial tissue, while calcium dysregulation impairs cell-signaling pathways and vascular function. Insulin-like growth factor-1 acts as a significant endometriosis risk factor by stimulating endometrial cell proliferation while simultaneously inhibiting apoptosis, facilitating abnormal cell growth. In contrast, high-density lipoprotein exerts protective effects against endometriosis through its anti-inflammatory and antioxidant properties, with elevated levels associated with reduced disease risk. The study demonstrates bidirectional associations between these biomarkers and endometriosis, suggesting that management strategies targeting blood lipid control and calcium stability may ameliorate disease progression. These findings support the clinical utility of measuring triglycerides, calcium, and insulin-like growth factor-1 in blood and urine as accessible biomarkers for early endometriosis diagnosis and monitoring.

title: Unraveling molecular heterogeneity: a systematic review of susceptibility gene profiles in ovarian, deep infiltrating, and superficial peritoneal endometriosis
summary: This systematic review reveals distinct molecular signatures across endometriosis phenotypes, with specific genetic drivers underlying each form. Genome-wide association studies have identified susceptibility loci including WNT4, FN1, and VEZT, which show differential expression patterns across ovarian endometriomas (OMA), deep infiltrating endometriosis (DIE), and superficial peritoneal lesions (SUP). Deep infiltrating endometriosis is characterized by aggressive invasiveness driven by FN1 and MMP2 upregulation, which promote epithelial-mesenchymal transition and extracellular matrix deposition, while NGF and VEGF overexpression triggers neurogenic inflammation associated with severe pelvic pain, dyspareunia, and dyschezia. Ovarian endometriomas exhibit a distinct iron metabolism signature with upregulation of HMOX1 and FTH1, creating a ferroptosis-resistant phenotype that protects against iron-induced cell death. In contrast, superficial peritoneal lesions are defined by acute inflammatory markers including IL-6, COX-2, and TNF-alpha, with reduced fibrotic markers, contributing to inflammatory ascites and infertility. Across all phenotypes, ESR2 upregulation and associated epigenetic alterations suppress the progesterone receptor, driving progesterone resistance that may limit treatment efficacy.

title: Serum metabolic fingerprinting for diagnosis and therapeutic applications of ovarian endometriosis
summary: Ovarian endometriosis manifests as dysmenorrhea, irregular menstruation, pelvic pain, and infertility in up to 50% of patients, with additional associations to anxiety, depression, and increased ovarian cancer risk. Serum metabolic fingerprinting reveals dysregulation across multiple metabolic pathways including pyruvate, β-alanine, arginine, proline, pyrimidine, tryptophan metabolism, and the citric acid cycle, with a distinct "black module" of metabolites strongly correlating with disease characteristics such as dysmenorrhea, elevated CA125, and ovarian cysts. A diagnostic panel of six serum metabolites—N-acetyl-aspartic acid, lactic acid, (R)-3-hydroxybutyric acid, hippuric acid, indole-3-acetyl-valine, and ascorbic acid—effectively distinguishes ovarian endometriosis patients from healthy controls with superior specificity and sensitivity compared to traditional CA125 markers. Three downregulated metabolites with therapeutic potential—gallic acid, ascorbic acid, and hippuric acid—inhibit endometriosis-related proliferation, inflammation, and angiogenesis, while simultaneously downregulating the pro-inflammatory cytokine IL-6. Serum metabolic profiling also identifies systemic metabolic changes including reduced cholesterol and triglyceride levels in endometriosis patients, offering a non-invasive diagnostic approach with potential therapeutic targets for disease management.

title: Wearable Biomolecular Sensing Nanotechnologies in Chronic Disease Management
summary: Endometriosis is characterized by dysregulation of multiple hormonal and inflammatory mediators that can be monitored through wearable biomolecular sensing technologies. The condition is strongly associated with elevated levels of prostaglandins (PGE2 and PGF2α), estradiol, and the inflammatory mediator leukotriene B4, all of which contribute to pain and disease progression. Additionally, alterations in sex-hormone binding globulin levels accompany these endocrine disturbances, affecting the bioavailability of circulating sex hormones in affected individuals. The coordinated elevation of these prostaglandins and leukotrienes drives the inflammatory microenvironment characteristic of endometriosis, promoting angiogenesis and perpetuating tissue lesion growth. Wearable nanotechnology platforms capable of detecting these biomarkers could enable non-invasive, real-time monitoring of endometriosis severity and treatment response in chronic disease management. This approach represents a significant advancement toward personalized monitoring strategies for menstrual disorders associated with aberrant hormone and inflammatory signaling.

title: Systemic Oxidative Stress Markers in Endometriosis: Elevated Advanced Glycation End Products and Sestrin 2 in Women with Ovarian Endometrioma
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies advanced glycation end products (AGEs) and sestrin 2 (SESN2) as significantly elevated systemic oxidative stress markers in women with ovarian endometrioma. AGEs accumulate through non-enzymatic glycation reactions accelerated by the chronic oxidative and inflammatory environment characteristic of endometriosis, and upon formation, bind to their receptor RAGE to activate nuclear factor-κB and trigger reactive oxygen species generation. SESN2, a stress-inducible protein involved in cellular redox homeostasis, is upregulated in response to the elevated oxidative stress in endometriotic lesions, where it regenerates antioxidant defenses and regulates cellular metabolism through AMPK activation and mTORC1 inhibition. While both biomarkers were significantly elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls, SESN2 demonstrated superior diagnostic accuracy (AUC = 0.893, 87.39% sensitivity, 86.05% specificity) compared to AGEs (AUC = 0.656), suggesting it more specifically reflects the cellular stress response in endometriosis. These findings provide insight into the oxidative stress-inflammatory pathways driving endometriosis pathogenesis and identify SESN2 as a promising diagnostic biomarker for ovarian endometrioma.

title: Oxidative Stress Mediated by Macrophages Promotes Angiogenesis and Early Development of Endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

This study demonstrates that macrophage-mediated oxidative stress is a critical driver of angiogenesis and early endometriosis development. Endometriosis, a hormone-dependent disease characterized by cyclic pelvic pain and female infertility, progresses through four stages including inflammation, immune evasion, tissue remodeling, and angiogenesis. Macrophages infiltrating early endometriotic lesions generate reactive oxygen species through the lipid peroxidative enzyme Alox15, which activates the HIF-1α hypoxia pathway to promote neovascularization and lesion growth. Experimental depletion of macrophages or inhibition of Alox15 and HIF-1α significantly reduced oxidative stress, lesion size, and angiogenesis, demonstrating that this macrophage-Alox15-HIF-1α axis is essential for endometriotic lesion establishment. These findings identify oxidative stress and HIF-1α signaling as potential therapeutic targets for preventing early endometriosis development.

title: Endometriosis Is Associated with Increased Serum and Peritoneal Fluid Concentrations of Chromogranin A and Its Derivatives
summary:
# Summary

This study identifies chromogranin A (CgA) and its proteolytic derivatives, catestatin and pancreastatin, as significantly elevated biomarkers in both serum and peritoneal fluid of endometriosis patients, with levels correlating to disease progression. CgA, a neuroendocrine glycoprotein, modulates angiogenesis and innate immunity—two critical mechanisms that facilitate endometriotic lesion survival and growth. While CgA and pancreastatin exert pro-inflammatory effects by activating macrophages and stimulating reactive oxygen species production, catestatin exhibits counter-inflammatory activity through M2 macrophage polarization and reduced inflammatory responses. These findings suggest that CgA and its derivatives serve as potential pathogenic mediators linking neuroendocrine dysregulation to the inflammatory and angiogenic processes underlying endometriosis, offering novel targets for understanding disease progression in this chronic gynecological condition characterized by pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, and infertility.

title: Risk of cardiovascular disease and mortality among women with endometriosis: Genetic insights
summary: This systematic review demonstrates a significant epidemiological association between endometriosis and increased cardiovascular disease risk in women. The authors identified a shared genetic architecture underlying both conditions, implicating multiple genes involved in immune system dysregulation, inflammation, and angiogenesis. Key genetic risk factors common to both diseases include VEGF and ESR1, which regulate vascular growth and estrogen signaling, as well as pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-10, IL-16, TNF-α, IL-1α) and their regulatory pathways (NF-κB, FOXP3). Additional shared susceptibility loci encompass genes affecting metabolism (CYP19A1, MTHFR), cell cycle control (TP53, CDKN2B), immune cell trafficking (CCL21, MIF), and ABO blood group antigen, along with noncoding RNA polymorphisms (ANRIL). These findings suggest that endometriosis and cardiovascular disease share common pathophysiological mechanisms centered on chronic inflammation, abnormal angiogenesis, and immune dysregulation, which may explain the elevated cardiovascular mortality risk observed in women with endometriosis.

title: The Role of Chronic Endometritis in Endometriosis: A Personalized Diagnostic Tool?
summary: This study reveals a significant pathophysiological link between endometriosis and chronic endometritis (CE), both chronic inflammatory diseases that co-occur with notably high prevalence and share overlapping pathogenic mechanisms. Chronic endometritis, characterized by plasma cell infiltration (CD138+ cells) and localized endometrial inflammation, is strongly associated with adenomyosis and correlates with elevated endotoxin levels in menstrual and peritoneal fluid, suggesting a microbial component involving gram-negative and intracellular bacteria that dysregulate the endometrial microbiome. Both conditions activate similar inflammatory cascades, including B cell and plasma cell infiltration alongside elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6 and TNF-α), while endotoxins released by bacteria such as E. coli activate macrophages that perpetuate sustained inflammation and tissue remodeling. The retrograde menstruation theory mechanically connects these pathologies, as menstrual reflux through the fallopian tubes introduces bacterial endotoxins and endometrial tissue into the peritoneal cavity, triggering local inflammation that promotes adhesion formation and fibrosis. Both endometriosis and CE cause overlapping clinical symptoms including pelvic pain, dysmenorrhea, dyspareunia, abnormal uterine bleeding, and infertility, suggesting that screening for CE may serve as a personalized diagnostic tool to better stratify endometriosis patients and refine treatment approaches targeting the underlying inflammatory and microbial drivers.

title: Correction Notice
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory condition characterized by elevated levels of pro-inflammatory cytokines (IL-6, IL-8, and TNF-α) in peritoneal fluid, driven primarily by macrophage infiltration into ectopic endometrial lesions. The TLR4-NF-κB signaling axis is central to this pathogenic process, as estrogen upregulates TLR4 expression while damage-associated molecular patterns (DAMPs) produced by ectopic lesions activate TLR4 on immune cells, initiating a cascade of signaling through MyD88, IRAK proteins, and TRAF6 that culminates in NF-κB p65/p50 dimer nuclear translocation and pro-inflammatory gene transcription. Curcumin, a natural polyphenolic compound, potently inhibits this inflammatory pathway at multiple levels by downregulating TLR4 expression, suppressing TRAF6 ubiquitination and IκB phosphorylation, and blocking NF-κB nuclear translocation, thereby reducing cytokine production in both peritoneal macrophages and endometrial stromal cells. In vitro studies demonstrate that curcumin treatment significantly reduces IL-6, IL-8, TNF-α, and MCP-1 production in response to inflammatory stimuli, suggesting therapeutic potential for alleviating endometriosis-associated pelvic pain and infertility. However, the clinical translation of curcumin requires overcoming its poor bioavailability through development of enhanced formulations and analogs, with further clinical trials needed to establish safety and efficacy.

title: The predictive diagnostic value of combined serum detection of CXCL12, VEGF, and CA125 for endometriosis
summary:
# Summary

Serum levels of CXCL12, VEGF, and CA125 are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients compared to controls and correlate with disease severity and symptom intensity. CXCL12, a proinflammatory chemokine, promotes cell chemotaxis, adhesion, and migration through CXCR4/CXCR7 receptor binding while activating multiple intracellular pathways including PI3K/AKT/mTOR and MAPK signaling. VEGF drives angiogenesis and lesion establishment through similar downstream pathways, with elevated expression in ectopic endometrial tissues and peritoneal fluid facilitating endometriotic lesion growth and persistence. Combined serum detection of all three biomarkers demonstrated superior diagnostic sensitivity and specificity for endometriosis identification, with CA125 and VEGF specifically elevated in advanced rASRM stage IV disease and severe dysmenorrhea. These findings establish inflammatory and angiogenic mechanisms as central to endometriosis pathogenesis and support the clinical utility of multi-marker approaches for noninvasive diagnosis and disease stratification.

title: HDL-related lipid ratios reflecting metabolic inflammation are associated with endometriosis status and ASRM stage
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a chronic estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease driven by systemic metabolic inflammation, which can be effectively assessed through HDL-related lipid ratios. This study demonstrates that monocyte-to-HDL ratio (MHR), neutrophil-to-HDL ratio (NHR), and non-HDL-to-HDL ratio (NHHR) are significantly elevated in endometriosis patients and positively correlate with disease severity, while lymphocyte-to-HDL ratio (LHR) is inversely associated with disease status. These ratios reflect systemic metabolic-inflammatory dysregulation, as chronic inflammation causes protective HDL particles to undergo functional remodeling into pro-inflammatory phenotypes that perpetuate local inflammation. The strongest biomarkers for disease classification were NHR for overall endometriosis status (AUC 0.715) and MHR for advanced ASRM stage (AUC 0.763), indicating that composite lipid ratios integrating both immune cell burden and HDL function may serve as valuable indicators for disease diagnosis and staging in clinical practice.

title: Differential expression of c-kit, E-cadherin, and beta-catenin in endometriosis and normal endometrial tissue
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

This study examined the differential expression of three key molecular markers—c-kit, E-cadherin, and beta-catenin—in endometriotic tissue versus normal endometrium to elucidate the pathogenic mechanisms of endometriosis. The findings revealed that c-kit expression is significantly elevated in endometriosis and endometriomas but absent in normal endometrial tissue, while beta-catenin levels are substantially reduced in endometriotic lesions across all disease types. E-cadherin showed no significant differences between groups. The reduced beta-catenin expression impairs the E-cadherin-mediated adhesion complex, weakening epithelial cell-cell adhesion and facilitating cellular detachment, migration, and ectopic implantation. Together, increased c-kit signaling and diminished beta-catenin expression contribute to lesion formation and persistence by promoting endometrial cell mobility and invasion. These findings support the hypothesis that stem cell-related pathways and adhesion molecule dysfunction are central to endometriosis pathogenesis and suggest potential therapeutic targets for this condition associated with pelvic pain and infertility.

title: The effect of tinzaparin sodium and leuprolide acetate in an experimental mouse model of endometriosis: The rol of the WNT/beta-catenin pathway
journal: Turkish Journal of Obstetrics and Gynecology
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis is a benign inflammatory condition driven by estrogen in which ectopic endometrial tissue develops through retrograde menstruation and peritoneal implantation, with cellular migration and tissue invasion serving as prerequisites for lesion formation. Dysregulated WNT/β-catenin signaling plays a critical role in endometriosis pathobiology by enhancing the migratory and invasive capacity of endometrial cells through WNT-responsive genes that govern cell growth, directional movement, and matrix penetration. This experimental mouse model study evaluated tinzaparin sodium (a low-molecular-weight heparin) and leuprolide acetate (a GnRH agonist that suppresses gonadotropin secretion and induces ovarian quiescence) as potential therapeutic agents targeting the WNT/β-catenin pathway. Tinzaparin demonstrated potent anti-inflammatory effects by reducing inflammatory markers and achieving statistically significant reductions in disease severity scores, while leuprolide primarily reduced epithelial content and upregulated HIF1α transcript levels. Both pharmacological agents modified the transcript abundance of pathway-associated genes and demonstrated measurable therapeutic effects on endometriotic tissue, suggesting that targeting the WNT/β-catenin signaling axis through heparin-class compounds and conventional hormonal therapies represents a promising multi-modal approach to endometriosis treatment.

title: From lipid profiles to disease onset: Triglycerides as predictive biomarkers in endometriosis
summary: This study identifies elevated triglycerides as a significant predictive biomarker for endometriosis risk, with both observational and genetic evidence supporting a causal relationship. Using multivariable analysis, researchers found that individuals in the highest triglyceride quartile had a 1.71-fold increased odds of endometriosis compared to the lowest quartile, an association that persisted even after adjustment for age, race, and other covariates. Mendelian randomization analyses provided genetic evidence confirming that lifelong elevation of triglycerides causally contributes to endometriosis risk through multiple statistical approaches. Notably, other lipid parameters including total cholesterol, low-density lipoprotein, and high-density lipoprotein showed no significant associations with endometriosis, suggesting triglycerides as a unique lipid biomarker. Since blood lipids are involved in hormone synthesis and inflammatory reactions—both central to endometriosis pathophysiology—this finding implicates lipid metabolism as a potential therapeutic target. The results suggest that triglyceride-lowering interventions including statins, fibrates, niacin, and bile acid sequestrants warrant investigation as potential preventive or adjunctive strategies for endometriosis management.

title: Attenuated Cyclooxygenase-Mediated Vasodilation in Cutaneous Microvasculature with No Difference in Platelet Aggregation in Women with Endometriosis
summary:
# Endometriosis and Cyclooxygenase-Mediated Vascular Dysfunction

Cyclooxygenase-2 (COX-2) is upregulated in endometriosis through inflammatory cytokine signaling, particularly via NF-κB activation and elevated interleukin-1β, interleukin-6, and TNF-α, with increased COX-2 expression found in ectopic lesions, eutopic endometrium, and peritoneal macrophages. Women with endometriosis demonstrate attenuated cutaneous vasodilation in response to anodal stimulation, a response entirely mediated by COX-dependent mechanisms, indicating impaired cyclooxygenase-mediated vasodilation despite elevated COX-2. Notably, platelet aggregation characteristics were similar between endometriosis patients and healthy controls across multiple agonists, and serum levels of thromboxane B2 and prostaglandin E2 did not differ between groups, suggesting that systemic COX metabolite dysregulation does not account for the vascular dysfunction. The dysregulated COX pathway in endometriosis contributes to neuroinflammation and sensory sensitization through mechanisms independent of platelet activation, linking the condition's elevated inflammatory state to chronic pain and systemic complications including increased risk of myocardial infarction and stroke. These findings suggest that endometriosis-associated vascular dysfunction results from impaired COX-dependent vasodilation in the microvasculature rather than abnormal platelet function or circulating metabolite levels.

title: Integrating ultrasound findings and extreme CA-125 elevation in the diagnosis of spontaneous endometrioma rupture: a case report
journal: International Journal of Surgery Case Reports
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis, which arises through retrograde menstruation combined with immune dysregulation and endocrine disruption, causes dysmenorrhea, infertility, and chronic pelvic pain through mechanisms involving neutrophil and macrophage-derived cytokines that promote angiogenesis and proliferation. Ectopic endometrial tissue grows in a self-sustaining manner as it both requires and produces estrogen locally, with ovarian endometriomas particularly susceptible to rupture and spillage of iron-rich chocolate fluid containing free radicals that trigger chemical peritonitis and mesothelial cell damage. This case report demonstrates that spontaneous endometrioma rupture, occurring in approximately 3% of cases, produces markedly elevated serum CA-125 levels (5315 U/ml)—a glycoprotein normally produced by coelomic epithelial derivatives—which when integrated with ultrasound findings provides diagnostic clarity and distinguishes the condition from epithelial ovarian cancer. Post-operative management combines gonadotropin-releasing hormone agonists like leuprolide with progestin therapy such as dienogest to suppress residual microscopic lesions and prevent recurrence, which occurs in 10-20% of patients within 18 months after surgical cystectomy. For superficial lesions, CO2 laser vaporization offers tissue-sparing advantages over cystectomy by preserving ovarian follicles and fertility potential while effectively ablating ectopic endometrial implants.

title: Menstrual Effluent in the Pathogenesis and Diagnosis of Endometriosis—A Systematic Review
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic, estrogen-dependent inflammatory disease characterized by multiple interconnected pathophysiological mechanisms including progesterone resistance, immune dysregulation, cellular senescence, DNA damage, and enhanced angiogenesis. Menstrual effluent (ME) has emerged as a non-invasive biospecimen that effectively captures these core disease mechanisms through its content of intact stromal cells, immune cells, extracellular vesicles, and soluble factors such as TGF-β1, VEGF, and aromatase. Analysis of ME biomarkers demonstrates exceptional diagnostic accuracy, with aromatase mRNA achieving an AUC of 0.977, followed by TGF-β1 (AUC 0.973) and IGFBP1 (AUC 0.92), substantially outperforming traditional imaging modalities like transvaginal sonography. The disease manifests clinically across multiple symptom domains including dysmenorrhea, pelvic pain, dyspareunia, subfertility, and gastrointestinal and psychological comorbidities including anxiety and depression. ME-derived factors actively condition the peritoneal niche by reprogramming macrophages and inducing mesothelial cell alterations, thereby establishing a microenvironment permissive for ectopic endometrial growth. This systematic review establishes ME as a biologically relevant and clinically superior diagnostic tool that reflects endometriosis pathophysiology while offering superior non-invasive assessment compared to current imaging-based diagnostic strategies.

title: Endometriosis-Related Impairment in Assisted Reproductive Technologies: Inflammatory Profiles, Oocyte Competence, and Embryo Development
summary:
# Summary

Endometriosis impairs fertility and assisted reproductive technology outcomes through dysregulation of the follicular inflammatory microenvironment. Women with endometriosis exhibit significantly elevated pro-inflammatory cytokines, including TGF-β1 and NF-κB, alongside reduced anti-inflammatory IL-10 in both serum and follicular fluid, creating a pro-inflammatory milieu that compromises oocyte competence and embryo development. This chronic inflammatory state correlates with diminished ovarian reserve markers (reduced antral follicle counts and lower anti-müllerian hormone levels), requiring higher gonadotropin doses and resulting in fewer oocytes retrieved, reduced maturation, and lower fertilization rates. Embryos from endometriosis patients exhibit increased multinucleation and persistent fragmentation, with significantly fewer grade I embryos and higher proportions of poor-quality grade III embryos. These morphological abnormalities translate to substantially reduced biochemical pregnancy rates (31.3% versus 61.1% in controls) and elevated miscarriage rates (40% versus 18%), demonstrating that endometriosis-associated inflammation impairs both reproductive cell quality and clinical pregnancy outcomes across multiple stages of assisted reproduction.

title: Epigenetic biomarkers of progestin-resistance in endometriosis
summary: Endometriosis is a chronic inflammatory disorder characterized by widespread epigenetic dysregulation involving aberrant DNA methylation, histone modifications, and dysregulated non-coding RNAs, which collectively contribute to immune dysfunction and altered leukocyte activation. Chronic inflammation in endometriosis drives epigenetic reprogramming that impairs progesterone responsiveness, particularly through aberrant methylation of the progesterone receptor and downstream targets such as Hoxa10, resulting in approximately one-third of patients exhibiting progestin resistance despite standard hormonal therapy. A three-gene methylation signature composed of MMP20, NRXN1, and RNA5-8SN5 has been identified as a biomarker that distinguishes progestin responders from non-responders with high diagnostic accuracy. Importantly, circulating leukocyte methylation profiles can serve as noninvasive biomarkers to predict progestin responsiveness, offering a potential clinical tool to identify patients who will benefit from hormone-based treatment versus those requiring alternative therapeutic approaches.
